topics.title: Familiarization of 8051 Based Kit and Simulation Platforms Using Assembly Language Programs for Data Transfer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEBL408 - MICROCONTROLLER PROGRAMMING AND INTERFACING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Familiarization of 8051 Based Kit and Simulation Platforms Using Assembly Language Programs for Data Transfer

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Familiarization of 8051 Based Kit and Simulation Platforms** refers to the systematic process of identifying, understanding, and operating the hardware components, memory architecture, addressing modes, instruction set, and software development environment (Keil µVision, Proteus VSM, EdSim51) used to design, code, simulate, debug, and execute Assembly Language Programs (ALP) that perform **Data Transfer Operations** — such as moving single bytes, blocks of data, bits, and exchanging contents between Internal RAM, External RAM, Special Function Registers (SFRs), and Code (Program) Memory of the Intel 8051 (and its derivatives like AT89C51/P89V51RD2) Microcontroller.

**Data Transfer** in 8051 terminology is defined as the movement of an **8-bit operand** from a *Source* (any addressing mode) to a *Destination* (the Accumulator `A`, `Rn`, Direct, or `@Ri` for internal RAM only), without altering the source contents. The internal architecture of the 8051 follows a strict **Harvard Architecture** with separate **64 KB Program Memory** and **64 KB Data Memory** address spaces.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **post office in a small town**:

- The **post office building** = the 8051 microcontroller chip.
- The **letter boxes inside the office** = the **128 bytes of Internal RAM** (addresses `00H` to `7FH`), where the lower 16 bytes (`00H–0FH`) are the four **Register Banks** (R0–R7), `10H–1FH` is the **Bit-Addressable Area**, and `20H–7FH` is the **General Purpose RAM**.
- The **separate warehouse outside town** = the **64 KB External Data Memory** (accessed via `MOVX` instructions).
- The **filing cabinets with special forms** = the **128 bytes of SFRs** (addresses `80H–FFH` including ports, timers, serial buffer, etc.).
- The **rule book of the post office** = the **64 KB Code (Program) Memory** accessed by `MOVC`.
- The **postal workers (Couriers)** = the **Assembly instructions** like `MOV`, `MOVX`, `MOVC`, `XCH`, `XCHD`, `PUSH`, `POP`.

When you (the programmer) issue a `MOV` instruction, it's like giving the courier a letter and saying: *"Pick up this item from Box A and deliver it to Box B."* The courier follows a strict **addressing mode protocol** — *which box? which floor? which locker?* — to complete the transfer.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Architectural Fact for KTU Lab:** The 8051 has a **8-bit ALU**, a **16-bit Data Pointer (DPTR)**, four **8-bit I/O ports (P0–P3)**, two **16-bit Timers (T0, T1)**, a **Full-Duplex UART**, and **128 bytes of user-accessible Internal RAM** (256 bytes in 8052 variants).

## 1.3 Hardware Familiarization — The 8051 Kit

When a student powers up a typical **Microcontroller Trainer Kit** (e.g., Nvis 5002, VPL-8051, or Elcon 8051 trainer), the following components must be identified and labeled:

| Physical Component on Kit | KTU Terminology | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| 40-pin DIP IC socket | 8051 MCU (e.g., P89V51RD2) | Central Processing |
| 16×2 LCD Display | Visual Output Device | Display register/memory contents |
| 4×4 Matrix Keypad | Input Device | Enter addresses/data manually |
| 7-Segment LED Display | Visual Output | Address/hex data display |
| 8-Pin DIP Switches (Logic Inputs) | Digital Input Port | Provide data from switches |
| LED Array (8 LEDs) | Digital Output Port | Visual indication of port data |
| Step/Continuous Run Switches | Execution Control | Run, Break, Reset, Single-step |
| RS-232 Serial Port / USB-UART | Communication | PC ↔ Kit serial link |
| External RAM/ROM Socket | Memory Expansion Slot | Add-on program/data memory |
| Power Jack (+5V DC) | Power Supply | Operate the trainer |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Viva Favorite Question:** *"How many bytes of on-chip RAM are present in 8051?"* — Answer: **128 bytes** (Internal RAM, addresses `00H` to `7FH`).

## 1.4 Software Familiarization — Simulation Platforms

The three most commonly used platforms in KTU Kerala engineering colleges are:

1. **Keil µVision IDE (µVision 5)** — Industry-standard *Integrated Development Environment*. Used for editing, assembling, compiling (.asm → .obj → .hex), and simulating 8051 code with built-in **µVision Debugger** (logic analyzer, memory window, registers, breakpoints).
2. **Proteus VSM (Virtual System Modelling)** — A schematic capture and *real-time* simulation tool. Allows drawing the 8051 circuit, loading the `.hex` file, and observing live logic on **Virtual Terminal**, **Oscilloscope**, **Logic Analyser**, and LEDs.
3. **EdSim51 / 8051 Simulator (Java-based)** — A lightweight, browser-friendly simulator useful for first-time learners. Shows the **Internal RAM, SFRs, ports, registers, code memory, and a 7-segment display panel** in real-time.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** A common student mistake is to confuse **Code Memory (MOVC)**, **Internal Data Memory (MOV)**, and **External Data Memory (MOVX)**. Each is addressed by a *different* instruction mnemonic and *different* addressing pins (PSEN for code, RD/WR for external data).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The 8051 Memory Architecture — The "Big Picture"

The 8051 has a **strict Harvard Memory Architecture**, meaning **Program Memory** and **Data Memory** are accessed via **separate buses** and **separate instructions**. This is the *single most important concept* for any data-transfer experiment.

### 2.1.1 The Four Distinct Address Spaces

| Space | Size | Addressing Range | Instruction | Access Signal |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Code (Program) Memory** | 64 KB | `0000H` – `FFFFH` | `MOVC A, @A+DPTR` or `MOVC A, @A+PC` | `PSEN̄` (Program Store Enable) |
| **Internal Data RAM** | 128 B | `00H` – `7FH` | `MOV` (direct, indirect, register) | — (on-chip bus) |
| **SFR (Special Function Registers)** | 128 B | `80H` – `FFH` | `MOV` (direct only, address ≥ 80H) | — (on-chip bus) |
| **External Data Memory** | 64 KB | `0000H` – `FFFFH` | `MOVX` (@DPTR or @Ri) | `RD̄` and `WR̄` (Ports 0 & 2) |

> [!NOTE]
> **SFR Address Trick:** SFRs are *physically separate* from the lower 128 bytes of internal RAM, but their addresses are `80H–FFH`, which is the *upper half* of the same 256-byte space. Only **Direct Addressing** can access SFRs.

### 2.1.2 The Internal RAM — Byte by Byte

The 128 bytes of internal RAM are partitioned as follows:

- **Register Banks (00H–1FH):** Four banks of 8 registers each (R0–R7). Only one bank is *active* at a time, selected by bits **RS0, RS1** in the **Program Status Word (PSW)**. Default: Bank 0.
- **Bit-Addressable Area (20H–2FH):** 16 bytes containing 128 individually addressable bits (`00H–7FH` of bit-address space). Bits in this area can be set/cleared using `SETB` and `CLR` instructions.
- **General Purpose RAM (30H–7FH):** 80 bytes used for scratch-pad storage, stack, and variables. Can only be accessed by **byte addressing** (not bit-addressable individually).

## 2.2 The Data Transfer Instruction Set — Complete Listing

The 8051 instruction set has **8 categories** of instructions. The **Data Transfer Group** contains the following 14 standard instructions (and 3 variations of `MOVX`):

| Mnemonic | Operation | Addressing Modes | Bytes | Machine Cycles |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `MOV A, Rn` | A ← Rn | Register | 1 | 1 |
| `MOV A, direct` | A ← (direct) | Direct | 2 | 1 |
| `MOV A, @Ri` | A ← (Ri) | Indirect | 1 | 1 |
| `MOV A, #data` | A ← #data | Immediate | 2 | 1 |
| `MOV Rn, A` | Rn ← A | Register | 1 | 1 |
| `MOV Rn, direct` | Rn ← (direct) | Direct | 2 | 2 |
| `MOV Rn, #data` | Rn ← #data | Immediate | 2 | 1 |
| `MOV direct, A` | (direct) ← A | Direct | 2 | 1 |
| `MOV direct, Rn` | (direct) ← Rn | Direct | 2 | 2 |
| `MOV direct, direct` | (direct) ← (direct) | Direct | 3 | 2 |
| `MOV direct, @Ri` | (direct) ← (Ri) | Indirect | 2 | 2 |
| `MOV direct, #data` | (direct) ← #data | Direct + Immediate | 3 | 2 |
| `MOV @Ri, A` | (Ri) ← A | Indirect | 1 | 1 |
| `MOV @Ri, direct` | (Ri) ← (direct) | Indirect | 2 | 2 |
| `MOV @Ri, #data` | (Ri) ← #data | Indirect + Immediate | 2 | 1 |
| `MOV DPTR, #data16` | DPTR ← 16-bit imm | Immediate | 3 | 2 |
| `MOVC A, @A+DPTR` | A ← Code[A+DPTR] | Code/Indexed | 1 | 2 |
| `MOVC A, @A+PC` | A ← Code[A+PC] | Code/Indexed | 1 | 2 |
| `MOVX A, @DPTR` | A ← Ext RAM[DPTR] | External Indirect | 1 | 2 |
| `MOVX A, @Ri` | A ← Ext RAM[Ri+P2] | External Indirect | 1 | 2 |
| `MOVX @DPTR, A` | Ext RAM[DPTR] ← A | External Indirect | 1 | 2 |
| `MOVX @Ri, A` | Ext RAM[Ri+P2] ← A | External Indirect | 1 | 2 |
| `XCH A, Rn` | A ↔ Rn | Register | 1 | 1 |
| `XCH A, direct` | A ↔ (direct) | Direct | 2 | 1 |
| `XCH A, @Ri` | A ↔ (Ri) | Indirect | 1 | 1 |
| `XCHD A, @Ri` | A.3..0 ↔ (Ri).3..0 | Indirect | 1 | 1 |
| `SWAP A` | A.3..0 ↔ A.7..4 | Accumulator | 1 | 1 |
| `PUSH direct` | Stack ← (direct) | Direct | 2 | 2 |
| `POP direct` | (direct) ← Stack | Direct | 2 | 2 |

## 2.3 Addressing Modes — The "GPS Coordinates" of Data

A single `MOV` instruction can mean different things depending on **how the operand is specified**. There are **6 addressing modes** in 8051:

1. **Immediate Addressing** — Operand is a *constant* in the instruction (e.g., `MOV A, #25H`). Symbol: `#`.
2. **Register Addressing** — Operand is one of R0–R7 in the current bank (e.g., `MOV A, R0`).
3. **Direct Addressing** — Operand is the *8-bit address* of an internal RAM byte or SFR (e.g., `MOV A, 30H`).
4. **Register Indirect Addressing** — The *value in a register* is the address of the operand. Only R0, R1, or DPTR can be used, and the operand is denoted `@Ri` (e.g., `MOV A, @R0`).
5. **Indexed Addressing** — Used **only** with `MOVC`. Combines base (DPTR or PC) + index (A) to address Code Memory.
6. **Relative Addressing** — Used in branch instructions, not data transfer.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Equations & Memory Map for Lab Exam**

| Parameter | Formula / Value | Unit / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **External Oscillator / Crystal Frequency** | $f_{osc}$ | **11.0592 MHz** is *standard* (gives exact 9600 baud for UART) |
| **Machine Cycle Frequency** | $f_{MC} = \dfrac{f_{osc}}{12}$ | Hz — One machine cycle = 12 oscillator periods |
| **Machine Cycle Period** | $T_{MC} = \dfrac{12}{f_{osc}}$ | seconds |
| **Example: For 11.0592 MHz** | $T_{MC} = \dfrac{12}{11.0592 \times 10^{6}}$ | $\approx 1.085 \, \mu s$ |
| **Baud Rate (UART, Mode 1, Timer 1 Auto-Reload)** | $\text{Baud} = \dfrac{2^{SMOD}}{32} \times \dfrac{f_{osc}}{12 \times (256 - TH1)}$ | bits/second (SMOD is bit of PCON) |
| **For TH1 = 0xFD (253) at 11.0592 MHz** | $\text{Baud} = \dfrac{9600 \text{ bps (exact)}}$ | — |
| **Internal RAM Size** | $128 \, \text{bytes}$ | $00H – 7FH$ |
| **SFR Size** | $128 \, \text{bytes}$ | $80H – FFH$ (only some implemented) |
| **Total Internal Data Addressable Space** | $256 \, \text{bytes}$ | $00H – FFH$ |
| **Program Memory (on-chip + off-chip)** | $4 \, \text{KB on-chip}$ (typical), expandable to $64 \, \text{KB}$ | $0000H – 0FFFH$ (on-chip for 8751/8951) |
| **External Data Memory** | $64 \, \text{KB}$ | $0000H – FFFFH$ |
| **Number of Register Banks** | $4$ | Banks 0–3, selected by PSW.3 (RS0) and PSW.4 (RS1) |
| **Registers per Bank** | $8$ | R0–R7 (R0–R1 can do indirect addressing) |
| **Bit-Addressable RAM Bytes** | $16$ | $20H – 2FH$ |
| **Bit-Addressable SFRs** | $16$ | P0, TCON, P1, SCON, P2, IE, P3, IP, … |
| **Total Bit-Addressable Bits** | $128$ (RAM) + $128$ (SFR) $= 256$ | — |
| **Stack Pointer Initial Value (on Reset)** | $\text{SP} = 07H$ | First `PUSH` writes to $08H$ |
| **Number of I/O Ports** | $4$ | P0, P1, P2, P3 (8 bits each = 32 I/O lines) |
| **Number of 16-bit Timers** | $2$ | T0, T1 (T2 in 8052) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the 11.0592 MHz crystal?** It is a multiple of 1.8432 MHz × 6, which when divided by 12 yields 921.6 KHz, and dividing again by 32 yields exactly 28,800 Hz, which is the standard baud rate tick for generating **error-free** serial communication at 9600, 4800, 2400, 1200 bps. Using 12 MHz would introduce a *non-zero percent error* in baud rate.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Mastering data transfer instructions in 8051 is the **gateway to embedded systems**. Every real-world embedded application begins with *data transfer*:

- **Industrial Control:** Reading sensor data from external ADC (via `MOVX`) into the accumulator, then transferring to internal RAM for processing.
- **Display Drivers:** Copying lookup tables (font data) from Code Memory to external display memory using `MOVC`.
- **Serial Communication:** Loading bytes into `SBUF` for UART transmission.
- **Stacks & Subroutines:** Saving context registers via `PUSH`/`POP` during interrupts.
- **I/O Expansion:** Transferring data from an external parallel EEPROM (AT24C02) into internal RAM.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Industry Connect:** Every modern SoC (System-on-Chip) still relies on the same fundamental Harvard architecture and data transfer principles introduced in the 8051. ARM Cortex-M microcontrollers use the same `LDR`/`STR` (load/store) data-movement paradigm.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> This section is the **core of the experiment**. KTU lab exams usually ask students to *write, trace, and verify* assembly programs for data transfer between memory spaces. Below are the **three canonical programs** with exhaustive derivations and trace tables.

## 3.1 Program 1: Single-Byte Data Transfer Between Internal RAM Locations

**Aim:** To copy the contents of internal RAM location `30H` to location `40H` using a simulator.

### 3.1.1 Algorithm (Step-by-Step Logic)

1. **START**: Begin execution at program address `0000H` (default reset vector).
2. **LOAD SOURCE** into the accumulator `A` (the universal data "shuttle").
3. **STORE ACCUMULATOR** into the destination address.
4. **END / HALT**: Stop program execution (use `SJMP HERE` infinite loop, as there is no `HLT` in 8051).

### 3.1.2 Assembly Language Program

```assembly
; === Program: Single Byte Internal RAM to Internal RAM Data Transfer ===
; === Source:  Internal RAM location 30H ===
; === Destination: Internal RAM location 40H ===
        ORG     0000H           ; Assembler directive: origin at reset vector
START:  MOV     A, 30H          ; Step 1: A ← (30H), load source into accumulator
        MOV     40H, A          ; Step 2: (40H) ← A, copy accumulator to destination
        SJMP    $               ; Step 3: Short Jump to self = infinite loop (HALT)

        END                     ; End of program
```

### 3.1.3 Code Size and Cycle Count Calculation

The **machine code** generated by the assembler is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Bytes} &= 3 \, \text{(MOV A,30H)} + 3 \, \text{(MOV 40H,A)} + 2 \, \text{(SJMP \$)} = 8 \, \text{bytes} \\[6pt]
\text{Machine Cycles} &= 1 \, \text{(MOV A,30H)} + 1 \, \text{(MOV 40H,A)} + 2 \, \text{(SJMP \$)} = 4 \, \text{cycles} \\[6pt]
\text{Execution Time at 11.0592 MHz} &= 4 \times T_{MC} = 4 \times \dfrac{12}{11.0592 \times 10^{6}} \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{48}{11.0592 \times 10^{6}} \approx 4.34 \, \mu s
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **SJMP \$ Derivation:** The `$` symbol is a Keil assembler shorthand for the *current program counter* value. The instruction `SJMP $` encodes as `80 FE` (FE = –2 in 2's complement, the relative offset to jump back to itself). It is the **de-facto HALT** in 8051 assembly.

### 3.1.4 Trace Table (Trace Window in Keil)

| Step | PC | Instruction | Op-Code | Bytes | Cycles | A (Hex) | Internal RAM `30H` | Internal RAM `40H` |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0000H | `ORG 0000H` | — | 0 | 0 | xx | 5AH | xx |
| 2 | 0000H | `MOV A,30H` | `E5 30` | 2 | 1 | **5AH** | 5AH | xx |
| 3 | 0002H | `MOV 40H,A` | `F5 40` | 2 | 1 | 5AH | 5AH | **5AH** |
| 4 | 0004H | `SJMP $` | `80 FE` | 2 | 2 | 5AH | 5AH | 5AH |

*Initial value assumed: `30H = 5AH`.*

## 3.2 Program 2: Block Data Transfer (Array Copy) — Most Common Lab Question

**Aim:** To copy a block of **10 bytes** of data from Source (Internal RAM starting at `30H`) to Destination (Internal RAM starting at `60H`).

### 3.2.1 Algorithm Using Registers and a Counter

1. Load source start address `30H` into pointer register `R0` (R0 is used as the **source pointer**).
2. Load destination start address `60H` into pointer register `R1` (R1 is used as the **destination pointer**).
3. Load the count (10) into a counter register `R2`.
4. **LOOP**: Load `A ← @R0` (move source byte to A), then store `A → @R1` (move A to destination).
5. Increment `R0` and `R1` (using `INC`).
6. Decrement `R2` (using `DJNZ R2, LOOP` — Decrement and Jump if Not Zero).
7. When R2 = 0, the loop terminates and the program halts.

### 3.2.2 Assembly Program

```assembly
; === Block Transfer: 10 bytes from [30H..39H] -> [60H..69H] ===
        ORG     0000H
        MOV     R0, #30H        ; R0 ← source pointer (start address)
        MOV     R1, #60H        ; R1 ← destination pointer
        MOV     R2, #0AH        ; R2 ← counter = 10 (decimal)
LOOP:   MOV     A, @R0          ; A ← source byte pointed by R0
        MOV     @R1, A          ; destination byte ← A
        INC     R0              ; R0 ← R0 + 1 (next source address)
        INC     R1              ; R1 ← R1 + 1 (next destination address)
        DJNZ    R2, LOOP        ; R2 ← R2 - 1; if R2 ≠ 0, jump to LOOP
HERE:   SJMP    HERE            ; Halt: infinite loop

        END
```

### 3.2.3 Trace Table (First 3 Iterations + Termination)

| Step | R0 | R1 | R2 | A | RAM[30H] | RAM[31H] | RAM[60H] | RAM[61H] |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Init | 30H | 60H | 0AH | xx | 11H | 22H | FFH | FFH |
| Iter 1 | 30H | 60H | 0AH | **11H** | 11H | 22H | **11H** | FFH |
| After INC/DJNZ | 31H | 61H | 09H | 11H | 11H | 22H | 11H | FFH |
| Iter 2 | 31H | 61H | 09H | **22H** | 11H | 22H | 11H | **22H** |
| After INC/DJNZ | 32H | 62H | 08H | 22H | 11H | 22H | 11H | 22H |
| Iter 3 | 32H | 62H | 08H | **33H** | 11H | 22H | 11H | 22H |
| ... | ... | ... | ... | ... | ... | ... | ... | ... |
| Iter 10 | 39H | 69H | 01H | **AAH** | 11H | 22H | 11H | 22H |
| After DJNZ | 3AH | 6AH | 00H | AAH | 11H | 22H | 11H | 22H |
| Exit to HERE | 3AH | 6AH | 00H | AAH | — | — | — | — |

### 3.2.4 Total Code Size and Execution Time Derivation

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Bytes} &= 2 + 2 + 2 + 1 + 1 + 1 + 1 + 2 + 2 = 14 \, \text{bytes of program memory} \\[6pt]
\text{Per Iteration} &: 1 (\text{MOV A,@R0}) + 1 (\text{MOV @R1,A}) + 1 (\text{INC R0}) + 1 (\text{INC R1}) + 2 (\text{DJNZ}) = 6 \, \text{cycles} \\[6pt]
\text{Total Cycles for 10 iterations} &= 10 \times 6 = 60 \, \text{cycles} \\[6pt]
\text{Setup Cycles} &: 1 + 1 + 1 = 3 \, \text{cycles} \\[6pt]
\text{Total Cycles} &= 60 + 3 + 2 \, (\text{final SJMP}) = 65 \, \text{cycles} \\[6pt]
T_{\text{total}} &= 65 \times T_{MC} = 65 \times 1.085 \, \mu s \approx 70.5 \, \mu s
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** If the source and destination blocks *overlap* (e.g., source = `30H–39H`, destination = `35H–3EH`), the direction of copying matters. Always copy **from low to high addresses** to avoid corrupting the source before it is read. This is a classic viva question: *"What happens if destination overlaps source in a forward copy?"*

## 3.3 Program 3: Data Exchange Between Internal RAM and External RAM

**Aim:** To transfer **one byte** of data from external RAM address `0000H` to internal RAM address `50H`.

### 3.3.1 Algorithm

1. Load DPTR with `0000H` (external address).
2. Use `MOVX A, @DPTR` to read from external RAM into A.
3. Use `MOV 50H, A` to copy A into internal RAM.
4. Halt.

### 3.3.2 Assembly Program

```assembly
        ORG     0000H
        MOV     DPTR, #0000H    ; DPTR ← 0000H (16-bit external address)
        MOVX    A, @DPTR        ; A ← External RAM[0000H]
        MOV     50H, A          ; (50H) ← A
HERE:   SJMP    HERE
        END
```

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Using `MOV A, @DPTR` (without the **X**) will access **Internal RAM** at offset 0 (which is R0 of bank 0), *not* external memory. The `X` in `MOVX` is non-negotiable for external memory.

## 3.4 Program 4: Look-Up Table (Code Memory) Transfer using `MOVC`

**Aim:** To read the 5th element (index 4) of a look-up table stored in program memory and copy it to internal RAM `70H`.

### 3.4.1 Algorithm

1. Clear accumulator (offset = 0).
2. Load DPTR with the table base address.
3. Add the index `04H` to A (`ADD A, #04H`).
4. Execute `MOVC A, @A+DPTR` to read the table entry.
5. Store A in `70H`.
6. Halt.

### 3.4.2 Assembly Program

```assembly
        ORG     0000H
        MOV     A, #00H         ; Offset = 0 (will add index)
        MOV     DPTR, #TABLE    ; DPTR points to TABLE
        MOVC    A, @A+DPTR      ; A ← Code[DPTR + 0] = TABLE[0]
        MOV     70H, A          ; (70H) ← A
HERE:   SJMP    HERE

        ORG     0100H           ; Place table at a fixed address
TABLE:  DB      11H, 22H, 33H, 44H, 55H, 66H, 77H, 88H
        END
```

### 3.4.3 Why `MOVC` and not `MOV`?

The **`C`** stands for **Code**. The instruction `MOVC A, @A+DPTR` computes the effective address as:

$$
\text{Effective Code Address} = \text{DPTR} + \text{A} \; (\text{contents of accumulator})
$$

The read operation uses the **PSEN̄** (Program Store Enable) signal to fetch from ROM/Flash, whereas `MOVX` uses **RD̄** to fetch from external data RAM. **Mixing these signals is a frequent viva trap**.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of All Four Data Transfer Programs

| Feature | Prog 1: Internal→Internal | Prog 2: Block Copy | Prog 3: External→Internal | Prog 4: Code→Internal (LUT) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Instruction Set** | `MOV` | `MOV`, `INC`, `DJNZ` | `MOVX`, `MOV` | `MOVC`, `ADD` |
| **Addressing Mode** | Direct | Indirect (`@R0`, `@R1`) | External Indirect (`@DPTR`) | Indexed |
| **Memory Spaces** | Internal RAM only | Internal RAM only | Internal + External RAM | Code + Internal RAM |
| **Loop?** | No | Yes (`DJNZ`) | No | No |
| **Counter?** | No | Yes (`R2`) | No | Implicit (index in A) |
| **Typical Use** | Single byte copy | Array copy, string move | Read external sensor/ADC | Lookup tables, fonts, constants |
| **Cycles (approx.)** | 4 | 65 (for N=10) | 5 | 6 |
| **Code Size** | 8 bytes | 14 bytes | 9 bytes | 14 bytes |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: 8051 Microcontroller Internal Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    CPUBlock[CPU ALU 8 bit] --> PSWBlock[PSW Register]
    CPUBlock --> AccBlock[Accumulator A]
    CPUBlock --> BRegBlock[B Register]
    AccBlock --> DataBus[(Internal Data Bus 8 bit)]
    BRegBlock --> DataBus
    PSWBlock --> DataBus
    
    DataBus --> IntRAM[Internal RAM 128 bytes]
    DataBus --> SFRBlock[Special Function Registers 128 bytes]
    DataBus --> IRAM[Instruction Register and Decoder]
    DataBus --> StackReg[Stack Pointer SP]
    
    IntRAM --> RegBanks[Four Register Banks R0 to R7]
    IntRAM --> BitArea[Bit Addressable 20H to 2FH]
    IntRAM --> GenPurpose[General Purpose 30H to 7FH]
    
    SFRBlock --> PortGroup[Ports P0 P1 P2 P3]
    SFRBlock --> TimerGroup[Timers T0 T1]
    SFRBlock --> UARTSFR[UART SBUF SCON]
    SFRBlock --> ControlSFR[TCON IE IP PCON]
    
    CodeMem[Code Memory 64 KB PSEN] --> IRAM
    
    ExtData[External Data Memory 64 KB RD WR] <--> DataBus
    ExtCode[External Code Memory 64 KB] <--> CodeMem
    
    Oscillator[Oscillator 11.0592 MHz] --> TimingBlock[Timing and Control Unit]
    TimingBlock --> CPUBlock
    TimingBlock --> DataBus
```

## 4.3 Program Flow Diagram: Block Data Transfer Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Start Power On Reset PC 0000H]) --> InitR0[R0 equals 30H Source Pointer]
    InitR0 --> InitR1[R1 equals 60H Destination Pointer]
    InitR1 --> InitR2[R2 equals 0AH Counter 10]
    InitR2 --> LoadOp[Load A from @R0]
    LoadOp --> StoreOp[Store A to @R1]
    StoreOp --> IncR0[Increment R0]
    StoreOp --> IncR1[Increment R1]
    IncR0 --> IncR1
    IncR1 --> DecR2[Decrement R2 DJNZ]
    DecR2 --> Check{Z equals 0}
    Check -->|No| LoadOp
    Check -->|Yes| HaltLoop[Infinite Loop SJMP Dollar]
    HaltLoop --> HaltLoop
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`
**Differentiate between the three 8051 memory spaces: Internal RAM, SFR, and External RAM. State the instructions used to access each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Memory Space | Address Range | Size | Access Instruction | Addressing Mode |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Internal RAM | `00H` to `7FH` | 128 bytes | `MOV A, @Ri` / `MOV A, direct` | Direct, Indirect, Register |
| SFR | `80H` to `FFH` | 128 bytes | `MOV A, direct` (only) | Direct only |
| External RAM | `0000H` to `FFFFH` | 64 KB | `MOVX A, @DPTR` / `MOVX A, @Ri` | External Indirect |

**Key Distinction (1 mark each):** Internal RAM and SFR occupy the same 8-bit address bus but are *physically separate*. The upper 128 addresses (`80H–FFH`) can only be accessed by *direct addressing* and refer to SFRs; *indirect addressing* in the same range returns garbage. External RAM is accessed via separate `MOVX` instructions that use `RD̄` and `WR̄` signals on Ports 0 and 2.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Explain the function of the `DJNZ` instruction in 8051 assembly language with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

`DJNZ Rn, rel_addr` stands for **Decrement and Jump if Not Zero**. It performs two operations in a single instruction:
1. Decrements the contents of register `Rn` by 1.
2. If the result is **not zero**, the program counter is updated to the label `rel_addr` (relative address).

**Example:** `DJNZ R2, BACK` — Decrement R2; if R2 ≠ 0, jump to `BACK`.

This instruction is the **most commonly used loop-control construct** in 8051 assembly. It is a 2-byte instruction (1 byte opcode + 1 byte relative offset), executes in **2 machine cycles**, and supports a backward jump of up to **–128 bytes** and a forward jump of up to **+127 bytes** from the current PC.

**Typical Use:** Controlling the iteration count in block-data-transfer programs (e.g., copying 10 bytes, where R2 = 10 and the loop body is the copy-increment sequence).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A: `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**(a)** *Write an 8051 assembly language program to copy a block of 20 bytes of data from internal RAM locations starting at `30H` to internal RAM locations starting at `50H`. List the algorithm and explain the role of `R0`, `R1`, and `R2`. **[7 Marks]***

**(b)** *What is the difference between `MOV`, `MOVX`, and `MOVC` instructions? Give one example of each. **[7 Marks]***

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

**(a) Block Transfer Program and Algorithm (7 Marks)**

**Algorithm (2 marks):**
1. Initialize source pointer `R0` = `30H`.
2. Initialize destination pointer `R1` = `50H`.
3. Initialize counter `R2` = `14H` (20 in decimal).
4. **LOOP**: Read source byte → A (`MOV A, @R0`).
5. Write A → destination (`MOV @R1, A`).
6. Increment both pointers.
7. Decrement counter using `DJNZ R2, LOOP`; repeat if R2 ≠ 0.
8. Halt with `SJMP $`.

**Program (3 marks):**
```assembly
        ORG     0000H
        MOV     R0, #30H        ; Source start
        MOV     R1, #50H        ; Destination start
        MOV     R2, #14H        ; Counter = 20 decimal
LOOP:   MOV     A, @R0          ; Read source
        MOV     @R1, A          ; Write destination
        INC     R0              ; Next source
        INC     R1              ; Next destination
        DJNZ    R2, LOOP        ; Loop if not done
HERE:   SJMP    HERE            ; Halt
        END
```

**Role of Registers (2 marks):**
- `R0` = **Source pointer** (holds the address of the byte to be read). Used in *register indirect* mode via `@R0`.
- `R1` = **Destination pointer** (holds the address where the byte will be written). Also used in indirect mode via `@R1`.
- `R2` = **Loop counter** (loaded with 20 and decremented by `DJNZ`).

---

**(b) Differences between MOV, MOVX, and MOVC (7 Marks)**

| Instruction | Full Form | Memory Space Accessed | Address Bus Used | Control Signal | Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **MOV** | Move | Internal RAM / SFR | 8-bit | Internal | `MOV A, 30H` |
| **MOVX** | Move eXternal | External Data RAM | 16-bit (DPTR or P2+R0/R1) | `RD̄`/`WR̄` | `MOVX A, @DPTR` |
| **MOVC** | Move Code | Code (Program) Memory | 16-bit (DPTR+A or PC+A) | `PSEN̄` | `MOVC A, @A+DPTR` |

**Key Distinctions (1.5 marks each):**
1. `MOV` works only within the on-chip data memory (00H–FFH). It does *not* generate `RD̄`, `WR̄`, or `PSEN̄` signals.
2. `MOVX` is the *only* instruction that can read/write external data memory; it places the 16-bit address on P0 (low byte) and P2 (high byte) and asserts `RD̄` or `WR̄`.
3. `MOVC` is the *only* instruction that can read from code memory (ROM/Flash); it asserts `PSEN̄` and supports *indexed* addressing only (no direct or register indirect for code memory).
4. `MOV` supports 5 addressing modes; `MOVX` supports only `@DPTR` and `@Ri`; `MOVC` supports only `@A+DPTR` and `@A+PC`.

---

### Question B: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` *(Alternative Choice)*

**(a)** *Explain the data transfer instruction set of 8051 with suitable examples. Categorize the instructions based on the destination operand. **[7 Marks]***

**(b)** *Write an 8051 ALP to read a byte of data from external RAM address `2000H` and store it in internal RAM address `30H`. Draw the flowchart and verify the execution using Keil simulator. **[7 Marks]***

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

**(a) Data Transfer Instruction Set (7 Marks)**

The 8051 data transfer instructions can be categorized by the **destination operand**:

**Category 1: A ← Source** (Loading the Accumulator — 2 marks)
- `MOV A, Rn` → e.g., `MOV A, R0`
- `MOV A, direct` → e.g., `MOV A, 30H`
- `MOV A, @Ri` → e.g., `MOV A, @R0`
- `MOV A, #data` → e.g., `MOV A, #25H`
- `MOVX A, @DPTR` → External RAM read
- `MOVC A, @A+DPTR` → Code memory read
- `MOVC A, @A+PC` → Code memory read

**Category 2: Rn ← Source** (Register Load — 1 mark)
- `MOV Rn, A` / `MOV Rn, direct` / `MOV Rn, #data`

**Category 3: Direct ← Source** (Internal RAM/SFR Write — 2 marks)
- `MOV direct, A` / `MOV direct, Rn` / `MOV direct, direct` / `MOV direct, @Ri` / `MOV direct, #data`

**Category 4: @Ri ← Source** (Indirect Write — 1 mark)
- `MOV @Ri, A` / `MOV @Ri, direct` / `MOV @Ri, #data`

**Category 5: Exchange & Stack (1 mark)**
- `XCH A, Rn` / `XCH A, direct` / `XCH A, @Ri` / `XCHD A, @Ri` / `SWAP A`
- `PUSH direct` / `POP direct`

---

**(b) External-to-Internal RAM Transfer Program (7 Marks)**

**Flowchart (3 marks):**
```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0([Start]) --> S1[DPTR equals 2000H]
    S1 --> S2[Execute MOVX A at DPTR]
    S2 --> S3[Execute MOV 30H at A]
    S3 --> S4[SJMP Dollar Halt]
    S4 --> S4
```

**Program (3 marks):**
```assembly
        ORG     0000H
        MOV     DPTR, #2000H    ; Load 16-bit external address
        MOVX    A, @DPTR        ; A ← External RAM[2000H]
        MOV     30H, A          ; Internal RAM[30H] ← A
HERE:   SJMP    HERE
        END
```

**Keil Simulation Steps (1 mark):**
1. Open Keil µVision → New Project → Select `AT89C51`.
2. Create new `.asm` file, write program, save.
3. Build target (F7) → generates `filename.hex`.
4. Open **Memory Window** (View → Memory Window), enter `D:0030H`.
5. Open another Memory Window for external data: `X:2000H`, place a value (e.g., `5AH`).
6. **Debug** (Ctrl+F5) → Step (F11) → Observe accumulator `A` and memory `30H` after each step.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Lose Marks in Data Transfer Programs:**
> 1. **Forgetting the `#` symbol in Immediate Addressing:** Writing `MOV A, 30H` instead of `MOV A, #30H`. The first means *load the contents of RAM address 30H*, the second means *load the constant 30H*. A 1-mark deduction if confused.
> 2. **Using `MOV A, @DPTR` for External RAM:** Will cause a *read from internal RAM at offset 0* (which is R0 of bank 0). Always use `MOVX A, @DPTR` for external memory.
> 3. **Not initializing the stack pointer (SP):** Required before using `PUSH`/`POP` or interrupts. Default `SP = 07H` may overlap with Register Bank 0. Best practice: `MOV SP, #60H` at the start of the program.
> 4. **Loop counter off-by-one:** Using `MOV R2, #0AH` for 10 iterations is correct, but if you intend 11 iterations, the counter is `#0BH`. Always re-verify: the loop runs *until* the counter reaches 0.
> 5. **Memory overlap in block copy:** Copying from `30H–39H` to `35H–3EH` will corrupt source data if copied forward. Use backward copy: load `R0 = 39H`, `R1 = 3EH`, and `DEC` instead of `INC`.
> 6. **Incorrect SFR addresses:** `MOV A, 80H` loads the *value* at SFR `P0` (because `80H` is the SFR address of `P0`), not the constant `80H`. To load constant, use `MOV A, #80H`.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Must Memorize Before Lab Exam)**

### 🔹 Architecture & Memory
- ☐ 8051 has **Harvard architecture**: separate program and data memory spaces.
- ☐ **4 KB on-chip ROM** (typical, e.g., AT89C51); expandable to 64 KB external.
- ☐ **128 bytes on-chip RAM** (`00H–7FH`) + **128 bytes SFR** (`80H–FFH`).
- ☐ Internal RAM is split into **4 register banks** (16 bytes), **16-byte bit area** (`20H–2FH`), and **80-byte general purpose** (`30H–7FH`).
- ☐ **256 bit-addressable bits** (128 in RAM, 128 in SFR).
- ☐ **DPTR** is a 16-bit data pointer for accessing external memory and look-up tables.
- ☐ **Reset vector** is at `0000H`; **default SP** is `07H`.

### 🔹 Instruction Mnemonics
- ☐ `MOV` = Internal data transfer (RAM + SFR).
- ☐ `MOVX` = eXternal data memory (XRAM, max 64 KB).
- ☐ `MOVC` = Code memory (ROM/Flash), indexed only (`@A+DPTR` or `@A+PC`).
- ☐ `XCH` = Byte exchange between A and a memory/register.
- ☐ `XCHD` = Exchange **lower nibble** only.
- ☐ `SWAP A` = Exchange the two nibbles of A.
- ☐ `PUSH direct` / `POP direct` = Stack operations using SP.

### 🔹 Addressing Modes
- ☐ **Immediate** uses `#` prefix (constant in code memory).
- ☐ **Register** uses `R0–R7` (current bank only).
- ☐ **Direct** uses 8-bit address (for RAM and SFR).
- ☐ **Indirect** uses `@R0` or `@R1` (for RAM `00H–7FH` only); `@DPTR` for external.
- ☐ **Indexed** uses `@A+DPTR` or `@A+PC` (code memory only).

### 🔹 Timing & Cycles
- ☐ **1 Machine Cycle = 12 oscillator periods**.
- ☐ At **11.0592 MHz**, T(MC) ≈ **1.085 µs**.
- ☐ Most `MOV` instructions = 1 cycle; `MOVX` = 2 cycles; `MOV direct, direct` = 2 cycles.
- ☐ `DJNZ` = 2 cycles; `SJMP` = 2 cycles; `LCALL` = 2 cycles; `RET` = 2 cycles.

### 🔹 SFR Quick Reference
- ☐ `P0 = 80H`, `P1 = 90H`, `P2 = 0A0H`, `P3 = 0B0H`
- ☐ `SP = 81H`, `DPL = 82H`, `DPH = 83H`
- ☐ `TCON = 88H`, `TMOD = 89H`, `TH0 = 8CH`, `TL0 = 8AH`, `TH1 = 8DH`, `TL1 = 8BH`
- ☐ `SCON = 98H`, `SBUF = 99H`
- ☐ `IE = 0A8H`, `IP = 0B8H`, `PCON = 87H`, `PSW = 0D0H`, `ACC = 0E0H`, `B = 0F0H`

### 🔹 Programming Best Practices
- ☐ Always start programs with `ORG 0000H`.
- ☐ Initialize the stack: `MOV SP, #60H`.
- ☐ End with `SJMP $` (or a label) — no `HLT` in 8051.
- ☐ Use `DJNZ` for tight loops; `DEC + JNZ` is also possible (saves 1 byte but takes 3 cycles).
- ☐ Use `MOVC` (not `MOVX`) to read look-up tables.
- ☐ Verify in Keil's **Memory Window** and **Watch Window** before submitting to examiner.

### 🔹 Kit Familiarization Checklist
- ☐ Identify the **40-pin MCU** (P89V51RD2 / AT89C51).
- ☐ Locate the **11.0592 MHz crystal** (or sometimes 12 MHz).
- ☐ Find the **reset button**, **run/stop/single-step switches**.
- ☐ Understand the **LCD module** (data and command registers).
- ☐ Identify **port connectors** (P0.0–P0.7, etc.) and external add-on slots.
- ☐ Connect the kit to a **PC via RS-232/USB** and use **Keil** to download the `.hex` file.

### 🔹 Simulator-Specific Tips
- ☐ In **Keil**, use the **Memory Window** to view `D:0030H` (internal RAM) and `X:2000H` (external XRAM).
- ☐ In **Proteus**, double-click the 8051 IC, load the `.hex` file into the *Program File* field.
- ☐ In **EdSim51**, use the **DIALOG** option to view register state step-by-step.

> **🎯 Final KTU Mantra:** *"The 8051 has only one worker — the accumulator. Every data transfer flows through `A`. Master the three `MOV` variants, and you master 80% of the lab exam."*

---
