## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*List any four unconditional jump instructions of the 8051 microcontroller. Differentiate between `LJMP` and `SJMP` in terms of opcode size and addressing range.*

**Model Answer:**

The four unconditional jump instructions are: `LJMP`, `AJMP`, `SJMP`, and `JMP @A+DPTR`.

| Feature | `LJMP` | `SJMP` |
|---|---|---|
| Opcode size | 3 bytes | 2 bytes |
| Addressing mode | Absolute (direct 16-bit address) | Relative (signed 8-bit offset) |
| Range | Full 64 KB (`0000H` to `FFFFH`) | −128 to +127 bytes from next instruction |
| Use case | Far jumps | Short local jumps, tight loops |

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*Explain the function of the `DJNZ` instruction with a suitable example. How is it different from `CJNE`?*

**Model Answer:**

`DJNZ` (Decrement and Jump if Not Zero) decrements its operand (a register or a RAM location) by 1, and if the result is non-zero, jumps to the target label.

**Example:**
```assembly
        MOV  R2, #05H     ; Initialize counter
LOOP:   ; body of loop
        DJNZ R2, LOOP     ; Decrement R2; loop until R2 = 0
```

This loop executes exactly 5 times.

**Difference from `CJNE`:**

| Aspect | `DJNZ` | `CJNE` |
|---|---|---|
| Primary action | Decrements operand | Compares two operands |
| Loop count | Decrements counter | Conditional on inequality |
| Comparison | Single operand | Two operands |
| Flag effect | None | Sets Carry if first < second |
| Typical use | `for`-loop iterator | `if-else` decisions |

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

**Q3A. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**(a)** Write an 8051 Assembly Language Program to add two 8-bit numbers stored in internal RAM locations `20H` and `21H`. Store the result in `22H` and the carry in `23H`. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Modify the above program to add **N** numbers stored consecutively starting from `40H`, where N is stored in `30H`. Store the final 8-bit sum in `31H` and the overflow (carry) in `32H`. Use the `DJNZ` instruction. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

**Algorithm:**
1. Load accumulator with the value at `20H`.
2. Add the value at `21H` using `ADD` (which sets the Carry flag for overflow).
3. Store the result in `22H`.
4. Move the Carry flag (via `MOV C, ...`) into a bit, then store in `23H`.

**Program:**
```assembly
        ORG     0000H
        LJMP    MAIN

        ORG     0050H
MAIN:
        MOV     A, 20H         ; A = [20H]
        ADD     A, 21H         ; A = A + [21H], CY updated
        MOV     22H, A         ; Store sum
        MOV     23H, C         ; Store carry (C is bit-addressable)
        SJMP    $              ; Halt
        END
```

**Expected Output:** If `20H = 0x55` and `21H = 0xAA`, then `22H = 0xFF` and `23H = 0` (no carry since `0x55 + 0xAA = 0xFF < 0x100`). If `20H = 0xC0` and `21H = 0x80`, then `22H = 0x40` and `23H = 1` (carry set since `0xC0 + 0x80 = 0x140`).

**Explanation of Carry Storage:**
The `MOV 23H, C` instruction moves the 1-bit Carry flag into the bit-addressable location `23H` (lower 128 bits of internal RAM are bit-addressable). The assembler resolves `MOV direct, C` automatically.

**Model Solution (b):**

**Algorithm:**
1. Use `R0` as a pointer to the data block (starting at `40H`).
2. Use `R1` as the loop counter (initialized from `30H`).
3. Clear accumulator and clear carry (`CLR C`).
4. Loop: `ADD A, @R0` and `JNC SKIP` (skip carry update if no overflow). On overflow, set a carry flag bit.
5. `INC R0`; `DJNZ R1, LOOP`.
6. Store final sum and overflow bit.

**Program:**
```assembly
        ORG     0000H
        LJMP    MAIN

        ORG     0100H
MAIN:
        MOV     R0, #40H        ; Pointer to data
        MOV     R1, 30H         ; Counter = N (from RAM)
        CLR     A               ; Sum = 0
        CLR     C               ; Clear carry tracker
        MOV     20H, #00H       ; Temporary carry storage (bit-addressable)

LOOP:
        ADD     A, @R0          ; A = A + @R0
        JNC     SKIP            ; If no carry, skip
        SETB    00H             ; Set bit 00H of RAM 20H (use 20H.0)

SKIP:
        INC     R0              ; Next element
        DJNZ    R1, LOOP        ; Loop until counter = 0

        MOV     31H, A          ; Store sum
        MOV     C, 00H          ; Move bit to carry
        MOV     32H, C          ; Store final overflow flag
        SJMP    $               ; Halt
        END
```

**Note on the carry bit storage:** A cleaner approach is to use a bit-addressable SFR like `20H` (which is internally RAM location `20H`, bit-addressable as `00H`–`07H`). We use bit `00H` (i.e., `20H.0`) as our running carry indicator.

---

**Q3B. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**(a)** Write an 8051 assembly program to find the **largest** of two 8-bit numbers stored in `R0` and `R1`. Store the result in `R2`. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write an 8051 assembly program using a subroutine and the `LCALL`/`RET` instructions to **swap** the contents of two RAM locations `50H` and `51H` using a third temporary location `52H`. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

```assembly
        ORG     0000H
        LJMP    MAIN

        ORG     0050H
MAIN:
        MOV     A, R0           ; A = first number
        CJNE    A, R1, CHECK    ; Compare A with R1
CHECK:
        JNC     STORE           ; If A >= R1 (no carry), store A
        MOV     A, R1           ; Else A = R1 (the larger one)

STORE:
        MOV     R2, A           ; R2 = largest
        SJMP    $               ; Halt
        END
```

**Logic:** `CJNE A, R1, CHECK` sets `C = 1` if `A < R1` (unsigned). At `CHECK`, we use `JNC` (jump if no carry) — meaning if `A >= R1`, we keep `A`. Otherwise, we replace `A` with `R1`.

**Model Solution (b):**

**Subroutine approach with LCALL/RET:**

```assembly
        ORG     0000H
        LJMP    MAIN

        ORG     0050H
MAIN:
        LCALL   SWAP_ROUTINE    ; Call the swap subroutine
        SJMP    $               ; Halt

; --- Subroutine definition ---
SWAP_ROUTINE:
        MOV     A, 50H          ; A = [50H]
        MOV     52H, A          ; Temp = [50H]
        MOV     A, 51H          ; A = [51H]
        MOV     50H, A          ; [50H] = [51H]
        MOV     A, 52H          ; A = Temp
        MOV     51H, A          ; [51H] = Temp
        RET                     ; Return to caller
        END
```

**Stack Trace:**
1. `LCALL SWAP_ROUTINE` pushes return address (address of the `SJMP $`) onto the stack.
2. SP increases by 2.
3. Subroutine executes, swapping the bytes.
4. `RET` pops the return address, restoring PC.
5. SP decreases by 2.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the `RET` instruction** in the subroutine. Without `RET`, the 8051 will execute whatever bytes follow in code memory, leading to erratic behavior. (Deducts 2 marks.)
> 2. **Stack Pointer not initialized.** Although the 8051 resets SP to `07H`, students who explicitly write `MOV SP, #60H` (using upper RAM) get a bonus mark for good lab practice.
> 3. **Using `MOV A, R0` instead of `MOV A, 50H`.** Always double-check whether the operand is a **register** or a **direct address**. `MOV A, 50H` reads the value at internal RAM location `50H`, whereas `MOV A, R0` reads register R0 of the current bank.
> 4. **Confusing `JNC` and `JC`.** The `CJNE` instruction sets `C = 1` for *less than*. So "jump if A is less than" = `JC`; "jump if A is greater or equal" = `JNC`.
> 5. **Using `END` incorrectly.** The `END` directive terminates the assembly source. It is **not** a machine instruction. Placing it inside a code flow causes assembler errors.
> 6. **Off-by-one in `DJNZ`.** A common mistake is initializing the counter to `N-1` instead of `N`, causing the loop to run one time too few.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Program Flow Control Instructions** alter the sequential execution of the 8051's **16-bit Program Counter (PC)**.
- **Four families:** Unconditional jumps, Conditional jumps, Compare-and-iterate (`CJNE`, `DJNZ`), and Subroutine calls.
- **Jump ranges:** `LJMP`/`LCALL` (64 KB, 3 bytes) > `AJMP`/`ACALL` (2 KB page, 2 bytes) > `SJMP` (relative ±128, 2 bytes).
- **Computed jump:** `JMP @A+DPTR` is the only **1-byte unconditional jump**, ideal for switch-case dispatch tables.
- **`DJNZ` is the workhorse loop** — combines decrement and branch test in one instruction.
- **`CJNE` does NOT modify operands** — it only sets the Carry flag and triggers a conditional jump. The relation is `C = 1` if `op1 < op2` (unsigned).
- **`LCALL` pushes 2 bytes onto the stack** (high byte first), `RET` pops them in the same order. **Always pair every `LCALL`/`ACALL` with a corresponding `RET`/`RETI`.**
- **`RETI` is mandatory** at the end of an ISR to re-enable the interrupt system.
- **`SJMP $` is the idiomatic way to halt** — `$` denotes the current program counter, creating an infinite loop.
- **Address `0000H` is the reset vector** — execution begins here after a hardware reset. Use `LJMP MAIN` immediately after `ORG 0000H` to relocate actual code.
- **Default Stack Pointer** is `07H` after reset, which overlaps with Register Bank 0. Always **reinitialize SP** (e.g., `MOV SP, #60H`) at the start of any nontrivial program.
- **Carry flag storage:** `MOV direct, C` stores the 1-bit Carry flag into a bit-addressable RAM or SFR location.
- **8051 machine cycle = 12 oscillator periods.** For 11.0592 MHz crystal, $T_{\text{machine}} \approx 1.085\ \mu s$.
- **The 8051 has a Harvard architecture** — code and data memory are separate; flow control only manipulates **code memory** addresses.
- **The closed-form check** $S_N = \frac{N(N+1)}{2}$ is useful for **verifying** loop output in lab exams.
- **Relative offset formula for SJMP/DJNZ/CJNE/conditional jumps:** $\text{rel} = \text{Target} - \text{PC}_{\text{after\_instruction}}$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Assembly Language Program to Generate Waveforms Using Timers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEBL408 - MICROCONTROLLER PROGRAMMING AND INTERFECTING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Assembly Language Program to Generate Waveforms Using Timers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of the **Intel 8051 microcontroller family**, waveform generation using timers refers to the technique of producing time-precise digital output signals (square waves, pulse trains, PWM signals) by leveraging the on-chip **hardware timer/counter peripherals** (Timer 0 and Timer 1). A waveform is synthesized by repeatedly toggling a port pin after a calculated time delay has elapsed, where the delay is generated by allowing the internal timer register to count up from a preloaded value until an overflow flag (`TF0` or `TF1`) is set.

The mathematical relationship between the desired waveform frequency and the timer preload value is governed by the oscillator crystal frequency ($f_{osc}$), the machine cycle period, and the timer mode (8-bit or 16-bit).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Definition (Board Standard):**
> Waveform generation using timers is the process of producing periodic digital signals on a microcontroller output pin by using the overflow of Timer 0 or Timer 1 to determine the half-period (toggle interval). The accuracy of the waveform is determined by the crystal oscillator frequency and the resolution of the timer's counting register.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a **metronome** used by a musician. The metronome produces a regular "tick-tock" sound at a fixed rate (say, 60 beats per minute). In our microcontroller:

- The **crystal oscillator** is like the spring/winding mechanism that keeps time accurately.
- The **timer register** is like the swinging pendulum that counts swings.
- The **timer overflow flag** is the "ding" that signals one complete period has elapsed.
- The **microcontroller pin** is the bell that rings each time the flag is set.
- The **reload value** loaded into THx/TLx is like adjusting the pendulum length — a shorter pendulum swings faster (higher frequency), a longer pendulum swings slower (lower frequency).

In simple terms: **Timer counts internally → when it overflows → microcontroller toggles a pin → repeated continuously → a waveform appears on the pin.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Why use a timer instead of a software delay loop?**
> A software delay loop (e.g., nested `DJNZ` instructions) is non-deterministic because it depends on the exact instruction fetch-execute cycle. A hardware timer is **crystal-accurate**, runs in parallel with the CPU, and is the only reliable way to generate frequencies in the audible range (20 Hz – 20 kHz) and beyond.

---

### 1.3 Standard Physical Constants (Bold Highlight)

| Parameter | Standard Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Crystal Frequency ($f_{osc}$)** | **11.0592 MHz** (most common in KTU labs) or **12 MHz** | Drives the machine cycle |
| **Machine Cycle Period ($T_{MC}$)** | **12 / $f_{osc}$** = **1.085 µs** (at 11.0592 MHz) | Time for one timer increment |
| **Timer 0 SFR Address** | **88H (TL0), 8CH (TH0)** | 8-bit registers |
| **Timer 1 SFR Address** | **8AH (TL1), 8DH (TH1)** | 8-bit registers |
| **TMOD Register** | **89H** | Timer Mode control |
| **TCON Register** | **88H** | Timer Control (flags + start bits) |
| **Max Count (Mode 1)** | **65,536** ($2^{16}$) | 16-bit mode full range |
| **Max Count (Mode 2)** | **256** ($2^{8}$) | 8-bit auto-reload mode |

---

### 1.4 Why This Experiment Matters in KTU Labs

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **second lab experiment** in the PCEBL408 syllabus because it builds directly on:
> - Experiment 1 (Basic 8051 assembly programming, SFR access, port manipulation).
> - It introduces **hardware peripheral programming**, a critical skill for all real-time embedded systems (motor control, signal generation, real-time clocks, baud rate generation for serial communication).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 8051 Timer/Counter Architecture — Step-by-Step

The 8051 contains **two 16-bit timer/counter registers** (Timer 0 and Timer 1) that can operate in four modes. For waveform generation, only Modes 1 and 2 are practically useful.

#### Operational Logic Steps:

1. **Select the mode** by writing the appropriate byte to the `TMOD` register at address **89H**. `TMOD` is **not bit-addressable** — the entire byte must be loaded as one value.
2. **Load the initial count value** into the high byte (`THx`) and low byte (`TLx`) of the chosen timer. For Mode 1 (16-bit), both bytes are user-controlled. For Mode 2 (8-bit auto-reload), only `TLx` is user-controlled; `THx` holds the **reload value** that is automatically copied into `TLx` on overflow.
3. **Start the timer** by setting the **TR0** or **TR1** bit in the `TCON` register (bit-addressable at 88H).
4. **The timer increments** on every machine cycle (when configured as a timer) or on every external pulse (when configured as a counter).
5. **On overflow** from `FFFFH → 0000H` (Mode 1) or `FFH → 00H` (Mode 2), the hardware sets the **TF0** or **TF1** overflow flag in `TCON`.
6. **The CPU detects the flag** using an instruction such as `JNB TF0, $` (jump back to itself if not set = wait loop).
7. **The CPU clears the flag** (`CLR TF0`) and reloads the initial count value (Mode 1 only — Mode 2 reloads automatically).
8. **The CPU toggles the output pin** (e.g., `CPL P1.0`) to flip the waveform from HIGH to LOW or LOW to HIGH.
9. **The process repeats** indefinitely, producing a periodic waveform on the output pin.

---

### 2.2 TMOD Register — Bit Layout

| Bit | 7 | 6 | 5 | 4 | 3 | 2 | 1 | 0 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Function** | GATE1 | C/T1̄ | T1M1 | T1M0 | GATE0 | C/T0̄ | T0M1 | T0M0 |
| **Timer 0** | — | — | — | — | GATE0 | C/T0̄ | T0M1 | T0M0 |
| **For Waveform Gen** | — | — | — | — | 0 | 0 | 0/1 | 0/1 |

> **Mode Selection (T0M1:T0M0):**
> - `00` = Mode 0 (13-bit, legacy 8048 compatibility — rarely used)
> - `01` = Mode 1 (16-bit timer) — **most common for waveform generation**
> - `10` = Mode 2 (8-bit auto-reload) — **best for precise, jitter-free periodic waveforms**
> - `11` = Mode 3 (split timer — only for Timer 0)

---

### 2.3 TCON Register — Control and Flag Bits

| Bit | 7 | 6 | 5 | 4 | 3 | 2 | 1 | 0 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Name** | TF1 | TR1 | TF0 | TR0 | IE1 | IT1 | IE0 | IT0 |
| **Function** | Timer 1 Overflow | Timer 1 Run | Timer 0 Overflow | Timer 0 Run | — | — | — | — |

- **`TR0 = 1`** → Start Timer 0
- **`TR0 = 0`** → Stop Timer 0
- **`TF0 = 1`** → Set by hardware on overflow, **must be cleared by software** in Mode 1

---

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **These four formulas cover 95% of all KTU 2024 Scheme waveform-generation problems.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T_{MC} = \dfrac{12}{f_{osc}}$ | Machine cycle period | seconds (s) |
| 2 | $T_{delay} = \text{Count} \times T_{MC}$ | Total delay generated by timer | seconds (s) |
| 3 | $\text{Count} = \dfrac{T_{delay}}{T_{MC}}$ | Number of timer increments required | dimensionless |
| 4 | $\text{Timer\_Value} = 2^{n} - \text{Count}$ | Initial value to load into THx/TLx (n = 8 or 16) | dimensionless |
| 5 | $f_{wave} = \dfrac{1}{2 \times T_{delay}}$ | Square wave frequency (factor of 2 because of toggle) | Hertz (Hz) |
| 6 | $\text{THx} = \text{Timer\_Value} \gg 8$ | Extract high byte (right-shift by 8) | hex |
| 7 | $\text{TLx} = \text{Timer\_Value} \ \& \ \text{0xFF}$ | Extract low byte (bit-mask) | hex |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the factor of 2 in Formula 5?**
> A full cycle of a square wave has **two transitions** (HIGH→LOW and LOW→HIGH). Since the timer is set to generate delay for **only one toggle** (one half-period), the period of the resulting square wave is $2 \times T_{delay}$.

---

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Real-Time Clock (RTC) generation** in embedded systems (1 Hz tick from a 32.768 kHz crystal divided and timed).
- **Buzzer / speaker tone generation** in electronic doorbells, alarms, and DTMF dial tones.
- **Pulse Width Modulation (PWM) for DC motor speed control** — Timer sets the period, compare register sets the duty cycle.
- **Servo motor control** — 50 Hz PWM with 1–2 ms pulse width controls shaft angle.
- **Baud rate generator** for the 8051's built-in UART (Timer 1 in Mode 2 is the baud rate clock source).
- **Data acquisition timing** — triggering ADC conversions at precise intervals.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Worked Example: Generate a 50 Hz Square Wave on P1.0 (Crystal = 11.0592 MHz, Mode 1)

This is the **classic KTU lab problem** that appears in nearly every internal assessment.

#### Given:
- Desired square wave frequency: $f_{wave} = 50$ Hz
- Crystal frequency: $f_{osc} = 11.0592 \times 10^{6}$ Hz
- Timer mode: **Mode 1 (16-bit)**
- Output pin: **P1.0**

---

#### Step 1: Calculate the required toggle delay (half-period)

The full period of a 50 Hz waveform is:

$$
T_{full} = \frac{1}{f_{wave}} = \frac{1}{50} = 0.02 \text{ s} = 20 \text{ ms}
$$

Since the pin toggles every timer overflow, the **timer must generate half the period**:

$$
T_{delay} = \frac{T_{full}}{2} = \frac{20 \text{ ms}}{2} = 10 \text{ ms} = 10{,}000 \text{ µs}
$$

---

#### Step 2: Calculate the machine cycle period

$$
T_{MC} = \frac{12}{f_{osc}} = \frac{12}{11.0592 \times 10^{6}} = 1.085 \text{ µs}
$$

**Explanation:** The 8051 divides the oscillator frequency by 12 to create the internal machine cycle. This division factor is fixed in hardware and is the single most important constant to memorize.

---

#### Step 3: Calculate the required timer count

The timer must count up for $10{,}000$ µs, with each count taking $1.085$ µs:

$$
\text{Count} = \frac{T_{delay}}{T_{MC}} = \frac{10{,}000 \text{ µs}}{1.085 \text{ µs}} \approx 9216.59
$$

**Rounding decision:** We round **down** to 9216, because rounding up would cause the timer to expire *before* the desired interval, producing a frequency slightly higher than 50 Hz. This introduces a small, acceptable error.

---

#### Step 4: Calculate the 16-bit preload value (Timer_Value)

In Mode 1, the timer counts from the preload value up to $2^{16} = 65{,}536$ (i.e., overflow from `FFFFH` to `0000H`). Therefore:

$$
\text{Timer\_Value} = 2^{16} - \text{Count} = 65{,}536 - 9216 = 56{,}320
$$

---

#### Step 5: Convert Timer_Value to TH0 and TL0 bytes

We split the 16-bit value 56,320 into two 8-bit bytes:

$$
56{,}320 \div 256 = 220.0 \quad \text{(integer division, quotient = high byte)}
$$

$$
220 = \text{DCH} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{TH0} = \text{DCH}
$$

$$
56{,}320 - (220 \times 256) = 56{,}320 - 56{,}320 = 0 = \text{00H} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{TL0} = \text{00H}
$$

**Verification (decimal reconstruction):**

$$
\text{TH0} \times 256 + \text{TL0} = 220 \times 256 + 0 = 56{,}320 \quad \checkmark
$$

**Verification (counting forward):**

$$
65{,}536 - 56{,}320 = 9216 \text{ counts} \times 1.085 \text{ µs} = 9{,}999.36 \text{ µs} \approx 10 \text{ ms} \quad \checkmark
$$

**Resulting actual frequency:**

$$
f_{actual} = \frac{1}{2 \times 9216 \times 1.085 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{1}{20{,}002.7 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 49.993 \text{ Hz}
$$

The error is **0.014%** — negligible for all practical purposes and acceptable in KTU board exams.

---

### 3.2 Complete Assembly Language Program

```assembly
; ============================================================
; PROGRAM: 50 Hz Square Wave Generation on P1.0
; CRYSTAL: 11.0592 MHz
; TIMER  : Timer 0, Mode 1 (16-bit)
; ============================================================
        ORG     0000H           ; Reset vector
        LJMP    MAIN            ; Jump to main program

        ORG     0030H           ; Main program starts here
MAIN:   MOV     TMOD, #01H     ; Timer 0, Mode 1, software start
        MOV     TH0,  #0DCH    ; Load high byte (DCH = 220)
        MOV     TL0,  #00H     ; Load low byte  (00H = 0)
        SETB    P1.0            ; Initialize P1.0 to HIGH

LOOP:   SETB    TR0            ; Start Timer 0
HERE:   JNB     TF0, HERE      ; Wait here until TF0 = 1 (overflow)
        CLR     TR0            ; Stop Timer 0
        CLR     TF0            ; Clear overflow flag (software)
        CPL     P1.0           ; Toggle P1.0 (complement)
        MOV     TH0,  #0DCH    ; RELOAD high byte (critical for Mode 1)
        MOV     TL0,  #00H     ; RELOAD low byte
        SJMP    LOOP           ; Repeat indefinitely
        END                     ; End of program
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Emphasis:** The `RELOAD` instructions (`MOV TH0, #0DCH` and `MOV TL0, #00H`) are **mandatory** in Mode 1, because the hardware does **not** preserve the preload value after overflow. Forgetting this is the **#1 most common mistake** in KTU lab exams and results in a frequency that is **slightly off and drifts over time**.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Timer Modes for Waveform Generation

| Parameter | Mode 0 (13-bit) | Mode 1 (16-bit) | Mode 2 (8-bit Auto-Reload) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Resolution** | 13 bits (8192 max count) | 16 bits (65536 max count) | 8 bits (256 max count) |
| **Longest single delay** | ~8.9 ms (at 11.0592 MHz) | ~71.1 ms (at 11.0592 MHz) | ~277.8 µs (at 11.0592 MHz) |
| **Software reload needed?** | Yes | **Yes** (mandatory) | **No** (automatic) |
| **Jitter / drift** | High (reload takes time) | Moderate | **Very low** (hardware reload) |
| **Suitable for high frequency?** | No | Moderate | **Yes (best choice)** |
| **Suitable for low frequency?** | Yes (use cascaded delay) | **Yes (single timer)** | No (too short) |
| **Common KTU use** | Rarely asked | **Default for square wave** | **Best for PWM / tones** |
| **TMOD nibble value** | `00xx xxxx` | `01xx xxxx` | `10xx xxxx` |

---

### 3.4 Extension Example: Variable-Frequency Waveform Using Mode 2

Suppose we need to generate a **1 kHz tone** (audible beep) on P1.0 using Mode 2.

$$
T_{full} = \frac{1}{1000} = 1 \text{ ms} \quad \Rightarrow \quad T_{delay} = 500 \text{ µs}
$$

$$
\text{Count} = \frac{500 \text{ µs}}{1.085 \text{ µs}} = 460.8 \approx 461
$$

$$
\text{Timer\_Value} = 256 - 461 = -205 \quad (\text{NEGATIVE — IMPOSSIBLE!})
$$

**Conclusion:** A 1 kHz signal at 11.0592 MHz requires a count of 461, but Mode 2 (8-bit) can only count up to 256. The delay is too long. Solutions:

- **Solution A:** Use a smaller delay between toggle events and toggle twice in the same waveform generation loop (effectively generating the half-period in software).
- **Solution B:** Switch to **Mode 1** (16-bit) for this frequency.
- **Solution C:** Use a **higher crystal frequency** (e.g., 24 MHz) which reduces the machine cycle to 0.5 µs, giving a count of 1000 — still too large for Mode 2.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often forget to **divide by 2** when converting desired frequency to timer delay. Always remember: **timer generates the half-period only**, not the full period.

---

### 3.5 Derivation Summary Table

| Quantity | Symbol | Formula | Numerical Value (50 Hz Example) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Oscillator frequency | $f_{osc}$ | Given | 11.0592 MHz |
| Machine cycle | $T_{MC}$ | $12 / f_{osc}$ | 1.085 µs |
| Waveform period | $T_{full}$ | $1 / f_{wave}$ | 20 ms |
| Half-period (delay) | $T_{delay}$ | $T_{full} / 2$ | 10 ms |
| Timer count | Count | $T_{delay} / T_{MC}$ | 9216 |
| Preload value | Timer_Value | $2^{16}$ − Count | 56320 |
| High byte | TH0 | Timer_Value >> 8 | DCH |
| Low byte | TL0 | Timer_Value & 0xFF | 00H |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 8051 Timer Hardware Block Diagram (Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["Crystal Oscillator f_osc 11.0592 MHz"] --> B["Divide by 12 Prescaler"]
    B --> C["Machine Cycle Clock 1.085 us per tick"]
    C --> D["Timer 0 Counter 16 bit TH0 TL0"]
    C --> E["Timer 1 Counter 16 bit TH1 TL1"]
    D --> F["Overflow Detector FFFF to 0000"]
    E --> G["Overflow Detector FFFF to 0000"]
    F --> H["TF0 Flag in TCON SFR"]
    G --> I["TF1 Flag in TCON SFR"]
    H --> J["CPU Polling via JNB TF0"]
    I --> K["CPU Polling via JNB TF1"]
    J --> L["Software Toggles P1.0 via CPL"]
    K --> M["Software Toggles P1.x via CPL"]
    L --> N["Square Wave Output on P1.0 Pin"]
    M --> O["Square Wave Output on P1.x Pin"]
    P["TMOD Register Mode Selection"] --> D
    P --> E
    Q["TR0 TR1 Control Bits in TCON"] --> D
    Q --> E
```

---

### 4.2 Assembly Program Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["START Reset Vector 0000H"] --> B["Initialize Stack and Ports"]
    B --> C["Load TMOD 01H Timer 0 Mode 1"]
    C --> D["Load TH0 DCH and TL0 00H"]
    D --> E["Set P1.0 HIGH"]
    E --> F["Set TR0 1 Start Timer"]
    F --> G{"Check TF0 1?"}
    G -->|No| G
    G -->|Yes| H["Clear TR0 Stop Timer"]
    H --> I["Clear TF0 Software"]
    I --> J["CPL P1.0 Toggle Output"]
    J --> K["Reload TH0 and TL0 DCH and 00H"]
    K --> F
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation of the flowchart:**
> The program enters a tight inner loop at "Check TF0" — this is the polling wait. Once the timer overflows, the CPU breaks out, performs the toggle, reloads the timer values, and loops back to start the timer again. The **reload step** is critical for Mode 1 and is what students most often forget.

---

### 4.3 Timing Diagram (Conceptual Waveform on P1.0)

```mermaid
gantt
    title Square Wave Generation on P1.0 (50 Hz Example)
    dateFormat  X
    axisFormat %s

    section Pin P1.0 State
    HIGH    :a1, 0, 10ms
    LOW     :a2, 10ms, 10ms
    HIGH    :a3, 20ms, 10ms
    LOW     :a4, 30ms, 10ms
    HIGH    :a5, 40ms, 10ms
    LOW     :a6, 50ms, 10ms
    HIGH    :a7, 60ms, 10ms
    LOW     :a8, 70ms, 10ms

    section Timer 0 Active
    Counting 1st half :b1, 0, 10ms
    Counting 2nd half :b2, 10ms, 10ms
    Counting 3rd half :b3, 20ms, 10ms
    Counting 4th half :b4, 30ms, 10ms
    Counting 5th half :b5, 40ms, 10ms
    Counting 6th half :b6, 50ms, 10ms
    Counting 7th half :b7, 60ms, 10ms
    Counting 8th half :b8, 70ms, 10ms
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Each "HIGH" or "LOW" segment on P1.0 lasts exactly 10 ms (the toggle interval).
- The timer counts for the same 10 ms in the background.
- One full HIGH+LOW cycle = 20 ms = 50 Hz. ✓

---

### 4.4 SFR Memory Map (Relevant to Waveform Generation)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["SFR Area 80H to FFH"] --> B["80H Port P0"]
    A --> C["88H TCON Flags and Run Bits"]
    A --> D["89H TMOD Mode Register"]
    A --> E["8AH TL1 Low Byte Timer 1"]
    A --> F["8BH Port P3"]
    A --> G["8CH TH1 High Byte Timer 1"]
    A --> H["8DH TH1 High Byte Timer 1"]
    A --> I["90H Port P1 Output Pin Bank"]
    A --> J["A8H IE Interrupt Enable"]
    A --> K["B8H IP Interrupt Priority"]
    A --> L["8AH TL0 88H actually TL0"]
    A --> M["8CH TH0 8CH TH0 High Byte Timer 0"]
```

> **Correction in memory map:** `TL0` is at address **8AH** and `TH0` is at **8CH** for Timer 0; `TL1` is at **8BH** and `TH1` is at **8DH** for Timer 1. The `TMOD` register at **89H** is **not bit-addressable** (must be written as a full byte), whereas `TCON` at **88H** **is** bit-addressable.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**What is the role of the TMOD register in 8051 timer programming? List the four timer modes.**

**Model Answer:**

The **TMOD (Timer Mode) register** at SFR address **89H** is an **8-bit, non-bit-addressable** control register used to configure the operating mode of Timer 0 and Timer 1. The upper nibble (bits 4–7) controls Timer 1 and the lower nibble (bits 0–3) controls Timer 0.

The four timer modes are:
- **Mode 0:** 13-bit timer (8048 legacy mode)
- **Mode 1:** 16-bit timer (most flexible range)
- **Mode 2:** 8-bit auto-reload timer (best for periodic waveforms)
- **Mode 3:** Split timer (Timer 0 divided into two 8-bit timers)

For waveform generation, **Mode 1** is typically used for variable low frequencies, and **Mode 2** is preferred for jitter-free high-frequency periodic signals. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Why is the divide-by-2 factor introduced when calculating the timer preload value for a square wave?**

**Model Answer:**

A square wave has a **period** $T$ that consists of **two equal half-periods**: one HIGH phase and one LOW phase. Since the microcontroller toggles the output pin once per timer overflow, the timer is responsible for generating **only one half-period** between consecutive toggles. Therefore, the delay required from the timer is:

$$
T_{delay} = \frac{T_{full}}{2} = \frac{1}{2 \times f_{wave}}
$$

If this division is forgotten, the resulting waveform will have a frequency that is **double** the intended value. For example, loading the timer to generate a 10 ms delay (instead of 5 ms) when targeting 100 Hz produces an actual 50 Hz signal. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Square Wave Generation with Mode 1

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 2 Internal Choice]**

> **Write an 8051 assembly language program to generate a 1 kHz square wave on pin P1.7 using Timer 0 in Mode 1. Assume a crystal frequency of 11.0592 MHz. Calculate the values to be loaded into TH0 and TL0 registers. Draw the flowchart of the program.**

#### Part (a): Calculations and Register Values [7 Marks]

**Given:**
- $f_{wave} = 1$ kHz = 1000 Hz
- $f_{osc} = 11.0592$ MHz
- Timer mode = Mode 1 (16-bit)

**Step 1: Calculate the machine cycle period**

$$
T_{MC} = \frac{12}{11.0592 \times 10^6} = 1.085 \text{ µs}
$$

**Step 2: Calculate the half-period (timer delay)**

$$
T_{delay} = \frac{1}{2 \times 1000} = 0.5 \text{ ms} = 500 \text{ µs}
$$

**Step 3: Calculate the required count**

$$
\text{Count} = \frac{500 \text{ µs}}{1.085 \text{ µs}} \approx 460.83 \approx 461
$$

**Step 4: Calculate the preload value (16-bit)**

$$
\text{Timer\_Value} = 65{,}536 - 461 = 65{,}075
$$

**Step 5: Split into TH0 and TL0**

$$
65{,}075 \div 256 = 254.20 \Rightarrow \text{quotient} = 254 = \text{FEH}
$$

$$
65{,}075 - (254 \times 256) = 65{,}075 - 65{,}024 = 51 = 33\text{H}
$$

**Answer: `TH0 = FEH`, `TL0 = 33H`** **(7 marks — 2 for delay formula, 2 for count, 1 for preload, 2 for byte split)**

---

#### Part (b): Complete Assembly Program with Flowchart [7 Marks]

```assembly
        ORG     0000H
        LJMP    MAIN
        ORG     0030H
MAIN:   MOV     TMOD, #01H     ; Timer 0, Mode 1
        MOV     TH0,  #0FEH    ; High byte = FEH
        MOV     TL0,  #033H    ; Low byte  = 33H
        SETB    P1.7           ; Initialize P1.7 HIGH
LOOP:   SETB    TR0            ; Start Timer 0
HERE:   JNB     TF0, HERE      ; Wait for overflow
        CLR     TR0            ; Stop timer
        CLR     TF0            ; Clear flag
        CPL     P1.7           ; Toggle P1.7
        MOV     TH0,  #0FEH    ; Reload high byte
        MOV     TL0,  #033H    ; Reload low byte
        SJMP    LOOP
        END
```

**Flowchart:** Refer to Section 4.2 of these notes, replacing P1.0 with P1.7 and the reload values with FEH/33H. **(7 marks — 3 for code, 2 for correct delay calculation reference, 2 for flowchart)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Mode 2 Auto-Reload for Buzzer Tone

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 2 Internal Choice]**

> **Design an 8051 assembly program to generate a 2 kHz tone on pin P1.5 using Timer 1 in Mode 2 (8-bit auto-reload). The crystal is 12 MHz. Explain why Mode 2 is preferred over Mode 1 for generating stable audio tones.**

#### Part (a): Calculation of TH1 value for Mode 2 [7 Marks]

**Given:**
- $f_{wave} = 2$ kHz
- $f_{osc} = 12$ MHz
- Mode 2 (8-bit)

**Step 1: Machine cycle**

$$
T_{MC} = \frac{12}{12 \times 10^6} = 1 \text{ µs}
$$

**Step 2: Half-period delay**

$$
T_{delay} = \frac{1}{2 \times 2000} = 250 \text{ µs}
$$

**Step 3: Count**

$$
\text{Count} = \frac{250}{1} = 250
$$

**Step 4: Preload (8-bit)**

$$
\text{Timer\_Value} = 256 - 250 = 6 = 06\text{H}
$$

**Step 5: In Mode 2, `TH1` holds the reload value and `TL1` is the actual counter.**

**Answer: `TH1 = 06H`** (TL1 will be reloaded automatically by hardware, but must be initialized once). **(7 marks)**

---

#### Part (b): Assembly Program and Justification of Mode 2 [7 Marks]

```assembly
        ORG     0000H
        LJMP    MAIN
        ORG     0030H
MAIN:   MOV     TMOD, #20H     ; Timer 1, Mode 2
        MOV     TH1,  #06H     ; Reload value = 06H
        MOV     TL1,  #06H     ; Initial value = 06H
        SETB    P1.5           ; Initialize HIGH
LOOP:   SETB    TR1            ; Start Timer 1
HERE:   JNB     TF1, HERE      ; Wait for overflow
        CLR     TF1            ; Clear flag (no TR1 clear needed)
        CPL     P1.5           ; Toggle P1.5
        SJMP    LOOP           ; Hardware auto-reloads TL1
        END
```

**Why Mode 2 is preferred for audio tones:**

1. **No software reload is needed** — the hardware copies `TH1` into `TL1` automatically on overflow. This eliminates the time gap between software reload instructions, which would otherwise introduce **jitter**.
2. **Predictable, crystal-accurate frequency** — ideal for musical notes, DTMF tones, and buzzer signals.
3. **Lower CPU overhead** — the inner loop has only `JNB TF1, HERE`, `CLR TF1`, and `CPL P1.5`, which executes in just a few microseconds, leaving the CPU free for other tasks.
4. **Symmetry** — the HIGH and LOW phases are perfectly equal because the reload happens instantly in hardware.

**(7 marks — 3 for code, 4 for the four-point justification)**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Lose Marks on Waveform Generation Questions (Based on Past 5 Years' Valuation Patterns):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to divide by 2** when computing the timer delay from the desired frequency. Marks lost: **2 out of 14**.
> 2. **Forgetting to reload TH0/TL0 in Mode 1** inside the loop. The waveform drifts. Marks lost: **2 out of 14**.
> 3. **Confusing TMOD nibbles** — writing `MOV TMOD, #10H` instead of `#01H` activates Timer 1, not Timer 0. Marks lost: **1 out of 14**.
> 4. **Using `CLR TR0` but never `SETB TR0`** inside the loop (timer is stopped forever after first iteration). Marks lost: **2 out of 14**.
> 5. **Not clearing TF0 in software** — the program exits the wait loop immediately on subsequent iterations, producing a non-square waveform. Marks lost: **2 out of 14**.
> 6. **Wrong byte-split direction** — writing `TH0 = low byte` and `TL0 = high byte`. Marks lost: **1 out of 14**.
> 7. **Choosing Mode 2 without checking the count fits in 8 bits** — count > 256 produces an impossible preload (negative number). Marks lost: **2 out of 14**.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **TMOD register** (address 89H) selects timer mode. Lower nibble = Timer 0, upper nibble = Timer 1.
- **TCON register** (address 88H) contains run bits (TR0, TR1) and overflow flags (TF0, TF1). It IS bit-addressable.
- **Mode 1 = 16-bit timer**, range 0 to 65,535. Software reload required.
- **Mode 2 = 8-bit auto-reload**, range 0 to 255. Hardware reload from THx to TLx.
- **Machine cycle = 12 / $f_{osc}$**. At 11.0592 MHz, this is **1.085 µs**. Memorize this value.
- **Timer generates the half-period** of a square wave. Always divide the full period by 2.
- **Key formula chain:** $f_{wave} \rightarrow T_{full} \rightarrow T_{delay} \rightarrow \text{Count} \rightarrow \text{Timer\_Value} \rightarrow \text{THx, TLx}$.
- **Timer_Value = $2^n$ − Count**, where n = 16 for Mode 1 and n = 8 for Mode 2.
- **Polling method:** `JNB TFx, label` waits for overflow. Always `CLR TFx` afterwards in Mode 1.
- **CPL P1.x** toggles the pin — single instruction, atomically flips the bit.
- **Crystal of 11.0592 MHz** is preferred over 12 MHz because it generates exact integer-machine-cycle counts for standard baud rates (9600, 4800, etc.) when Timer 1 is used as a baud rate clock — a useful fact in the next experiment.
- **The reload step is the #1 most-skipped instruction** in lab exams. Always include it inside the loop.
- **For very low frequencies** (< 14 Hz at 11.0592 MHz in Mode 1), use a **nested software loop** around the timer delay to count multiple half-periods.
- **For PWM** (variable duty cycle), use Mode 2 for the period and use a separate compare value to set the HIGH time within the period.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: 8051 C Programming Examples for Data Transfer, Arithmetic and Logic Operations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEBL408 - MICROCONTROLLER PROGRAMMING AND INTERFACING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** 8051 C Programming Examples for Data Transfer, Arithmetic and Logic Operations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**8051 C Programming** refers to the practice of writing embedded application code for the **Intel 8051 family of 8-bit microcontrollers** using the **C programming language**, typically through the **Keil µVision IDE** with the **C51 compiler**. In the context of this experiment, the focus is on three fundamental categories of operations:

1. **Data Transfer Operations** – Moving data between **CPU registers**, **internal RAM (idata)**, **external data memory (xdata)**, **code memory (code)**, and **Special Function Registers (SFRs)**.
2. **Arithmetic Operations** – Performing addition, subtraction, multiplication, division, increment, decrement, and **Decimal Adjust** operations on 8-bit and 16-bit operands.
3. **Logic Operations** – Executing bitwise AND, OR, XOR, NOT, rotate, swap, and compare functions on byte-level data.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme PCEBL408 Lab syllabus**, this experiment builds the foundation required for all subsequent interfacing experiments (LEDs, LCD, Keypad, ADC, etc.) by ensuring the student is fluent in **C-to-Assembly translation semantics** of the C51 compiler.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** The 8051 C programming constructs map **directly** to the underlying MCS-51 instruction set. Every `=` assignment, `+` operator, and `&` bitwise AND ultimately translates to one or more 8051 assembly instructions (e.g., `MOV`, `ADD`, `ANL`). Understanding this mapping is the key to writing *efficient* embedded C.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Workbench" Model

Imagine the **8051 microcontroller as a carpenter's workbench**:

- **CPU Registers (A, B, R0–R7)** → The carpenter's **two hands** (Accumulator = dominant hand, B register = helper). All "active work" happens here.
- **Internal RAM (128 bytes)** → The **toolbox drawer** right next to the carpenter. Quick access (idata), where frequently used variables are kept.
- **External Data Memory (xdata, up to 64 KB)** → The **storage shed** in the backyard. Slower to access but huge capacity.
- **Code Memory (ROM/Flash)** → The **instruction manual** the carpenter reads but cannot modify at runtime.
- **SFRs (P0, P1, P2, P3, TCON, SCON, etc.)** → The **control knobs and switches** on the workbench that connect the carpenter to the outside world (peripherals).

**Data Transfer** = moving tools between hands, drawer, and shed.
**Arithmetic** = measuring, cutting, and counting lengths on the workbench.
**Logic Operations** = deciding which pieces of wood to keep (AND), which to combine (OR), or which to flip (XOR/NOT).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Takeaway:** In 8051 C, *every operation ultimately involves the Accumulator (A)*. The C51 compiler is a "Accumulator-centric" translator — even simple expressions like `x = y + z` internally load `y` into A, add `z`, then store the result.

### 1.3 Standard 8051 Memory Metrics and Constants

| Metric | Value (Bold for emphasis) |
|---|---|
| Internal RAM (idata) | **128 bytes** (00H–7FH) |
| SFR Area | **128 bytes** (80H–FFH), bit-addressable partially |
| External Data Memory (xdata) | **64 KB** (0000H–FFFFH) |
| Code Memory (code) | **64 KB** (0000H–FFFFH) |
| Program Counter width | **16 bits** |
| Accumulator (A) width | **8 bits** |
| DPTR (Data Pointer) | **16-bit** register (DPH + DPL) |
| Stack growth direction | **Upward** (toward higher addresses) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Operational Categories — Structured Logic

#### 2.1.1 Data Transfer Operations

**Definition:** Movement of bytes/words between source and destination locations **without** altering the data's value (pure copy).

**Core 'Why':** Microcontrollers spend **~60–70% of execution time** in data movement. Optimizing transfers (e.g., using `data` pointers vs. `xdata`) directly impacts throughput.

**The 'How' — Mapping C to 8051 Mnemonics:**

```c
unsigned char x = 0x55;    // Internally -> MOV 30H, #55H  (allocate idata)
x = P1;                    // Internally -> MOV A, P1 / MOV 30H, A
P2 = x;                    // Internally -> MOV A, 30H / MOV P2, A
```

**Key SFRs for I/O Transfer:**

| SFR | Address | Bit-Addressable | Function |
|---|---|---|---|
| **P0** | 80H | Yes | Port 0 (also lower address/data bus in external mode) |
| **P1** | 90H | Yes | Port 1 (general purpose I/O) |
| **P2** | A0H | Yes | Port 2 (upper address bus in external mode) |
| **P3** | B0H | Yes | Port 3 (alternate functions: RXD, TXD, INT0, INT1, T0, T1, WR, RD) |

#### 2.1.2 Arithmetic Operations

**Core Operations and Carry/Cy Flag Effects:**

| Operation | C Operator | 8051 Mnemonic | Flag Affected | Range/Overflow |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Add | `+` | `ADD A, src` | C, AC, OV, P | 00H–FFH (8-bit) |
| Add with Carry | `+` (with carry) | `ADDC A, src` | C, AC, OV, P | Used in multi-byte addition |
| Subtract | `-` | `SUBB A, src` | C, AC, OV, P | Performs A = A – src – C |
| Multiply | `*` | `MUL AB` | C=0, OV | Result in **B:A (16-bit)** |
| Divide | `/` | `DIV AB` | C=0, OV (if B=0) | Quotient in **A**, Remainder in **B** |
| Increment | `++` | `INC A` / `INC direct` | None on PSW | 8-bit wraparound |
| Decrement | `--` | `DEC A` / `DEC direct` | None on PSW | 8-bit wraparound |
| Decimal Adjust | (after ADD) | `DA A` | C | BCD correction |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical 8051 Quirk:** The `SUBB` instruction **always subtracts the Carry flag** along with the source operand. To perform a simple `A = A – src`, you **must clear the Carry flag first** using `CY = 0;` (in C) or `CLR C` (in assembly). Forgetting this is the **#1 student mistake** in 8051 subtraction.

#### 2.1.3 Logic Operations

**Bitwise and Compare Operators:**

| Operation | C Operator | 8051 Mnemonic | Result Location |
|---|---|---|---|
| AND | `&` | `ANL A, src` / `ANL direct, A` | A (or direct) |
| OR | `\|` | `ORL A, src` / `ORL direct, A` | A (or direct) |
| XOR | `^` | `XRL A, src` / `XRL direct, A` | A (or direct) |
| NOT (complement) | `~` | `CPL A` | A |
| Rotate Left (through C) | (no direct C) | `RLC A` | A |
| Rotate Right (through C) | (no direct C) | `RRC A` | A |
| Rotate Left (no C) | (no direct C) | `RL A` | A |
| Rotate Right (no C) | (no direct C) | `RR A` | A |
| Swap Nibbles | (no direct C) | `SWAP A` | A |
| Compare | `==, !=, <, >` | `CJNE A, src, rel` | Flags only |

**The 'Why' Behind Logic Operations:**
- **AND** is used to **mask** specific bits (e.g., `P1 & 0x0F` keeps only lower nibble).
- **OR** is used to **set** specific bits (e.g., `P1 | 0x80` sets the MSB).
- **XOR** is used to **toggle** specific bits (e.g., `P1 ^ 0x80` flips the MSB).
- **CPL A (~ operator)** performs a **bitwise NOT** (1's complement).

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Units / Range | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 8-bit unsigned range | $0 \leq x \leq 255$ | Decimal | $00\text{H} \leq x \leq \text{FFH}$ |
| 8-bit signed range | $-128 \leq x \leq +127$ | Two's complement | MSB = sign bit |
| Carry flag (ADD) | $C = 1 \text{ if } (A_7 + src_7) \geq 2^8$ | Boolean | Bit 7 carry-out |
| Auxiliary Carry (ADD) | $AC = 1 \text{ if } (A_3 + src_3) \geq 2^4$ | Boolean | Bit 3 carry-out (for BCD) |
| Overflow flag (signed) | $OV = C_7 \oplus C_6$ | Boolean | XOR of bit-7 and bit-6 carries |
| Multiplication overflow | $OV = 1 \text{ if } B \neq 0 \text{ after MUL AB}$ | Boolean | Result > 255 |
| Division by zero | $OV = 1 \text{ if } B = 0 \text{ before DIV AB}$ | Boolean | Undefined state |
| DPTR addressing range | $0 \leq DPTR \leq 65535$ | xdata bytes | 64 KB space |
| Bit-addressable RAM | $20\text{H}–2\text{FH}$ | 16 bytes = 128 bits | Bit addresses 00H–7FH |
| SFR bit-addressable | $80\text{H}, 88\text{H}, 90\text{H}, 98\text{H}, ..., \text{F8H}$ | 16 SFRs | Bit addresses 80H–FFH |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Data Transfer** underpins **all peripheral interfacing** — reading sensor data into the MCU and sending control signals out.
- **Arithmetic Operations** form the basis of **DSP routines**, **filtering algorithms**, and **PID controllers** in real-time systems.
- **Logic Operations** are essential for **bit-banging protocols** (I2C, SPI emulated in software), **status flag management**, and **GPIO bit manipulation**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Connection:** Automotive ECUs, washing machine controllers, and industrial PLCs all rely on optimized 8051 C code for these three operation categories. A single unoptimized `xdata` access in a tight loop can cost **6× the cycles** of an `idata` access — a real concern in hard-real-time systems.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section provides **exhaustive, line-by-line walkthroughs** of canonical 8051 C programs covering all three operation categories. Every program is shown in C, then mapped to its compiled assembly, then traced byte-by-byte.

---

### 3.1 Program 1: Data Transfer — Block Move (Internal RAM to External RAM)

**Objective:** Copy 10 bytes from internal RAM (idata) starting at address `0x30` to external RAM (xdata) starting at address `0x1000`.

```c
#include <reg51.h>

unsigned char idata source[10] _at_ 0x30;   // Place array at idata 0x30
unsigned char xdata destination[10] _at_ 0x1000;

void main(void) {
    unsigned char i;
    for (i = 0; i < 10; i++) {
        destination[i] = source[i];
    }
    while(1);   // Infinite loop — halt
}
```

**Step 1 — Memory Allocation Analysis:**

| Variable | Memory Space | Start Address | Size |
|---|---|---|---|
| `source[0..9]` | idata | 0x30 | 10 bytes (30H–39H) |
| `destination[0..9]` | xdata | 0x1000 | 10 bytes (1000H–1009H) |
| `i` | data (R7) | — | 1 byte (compiler puts loop counter in register) |

**Step 2 — Compiled Assembly Trace (Keil C51):**

```
        MOV  R7, #00H          ; i = 0
LOOP:   MOV  A, R7             ; A = i
        ADD  A, #30H           ; A = 0x30 + i (address of source[i])
        MOV  R0, A             ; R0 = &source[i]  (use R0 for idata indirect)
        MOV  A, @R0            ; A = source[i]
        MOV  DPL, R7           ; Low byte of destination address = i
        MOV  DPH, #10H         ; High byte = 0x10
        MOVX @DPTR, A          ; destination[i] = A
        INC  R7                ; i++
        CJNE R7, #0AH, LOOP    ; if i != 10, repeat
DONE:   SJMP DONE              ; while(1)
```

**Step 3 — Byte-by-Byte Execution Trace (First Iteration, i=0):**

| Step | Register/Memory State | Action |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | R7=00H | `i = 0` initialized |
| 2 | A=00H | Load `i` into accumulator |
| 3 | A=30H | Add base address 0x30 |
| 4 | R0=30H | Store effective address in R0 |
| 5 | A=[30H] (e.g., 0xAA) | Read `source[0]` |
| 6 | DPL=00H, DPH=10H | Point DPTR to 0x1000 |
| 7 | [1000H]=0xAA | Write to `destination[0]` |
| 8 | R7=01H | Increment i |
| 9 | R7≠0AH | CJNE jumps back to LOOP |

**Step 4 — Performance Calculation:**

For 10 iterations, cycles per iteration (typical 8051 @ 12 MHz, 1 µs/cycle):
- MOV A, R7: **1 cycle**
- ADD A, #data: **2 cycles**
- MOV R0, A: **1 cycle**
- MOV A, @R0: **1 cycle**
- MOV DPL, R7: **2 cycles**
- MOV DPH, #data: **2 cycles**
- MOVX @DPTR, A: **2 cycles** (xdata write)
- INC R7: **1 cycle**
- CJNE: **2 cycles**

**Total per iteration = 14 cycles = 14 µs**
**Total for 10 iterations = 140 cycles = 140 µs** (excluding loop overhead)

$$\text{Total Time} = N \times (T_{MOV} + T_{ADD} + T_{MOVX}) = 10 \times 14\,\mu s = 140\,\mu s$$

---

### 3.2 Program 2: Arithmetic — 16-bit Addition with Carry Propagation

**Objective:** Add two 16-bit numbers stored in internal RAM and store the 16-bit result (and any carry-out) correctly.

**Hex numbers:** `Num1 = 0x1234`, `Num2 = 0xE0F0`
**Expected result:** $0x1234 + 0xE0F0 = 0xF324$ (no carry-out)

```c
#include <reg51.h>

void main(void) {
    unsigned char idata num1_lo _at_ 0x30;  // = 0x34
    unsigned char idata num1_hi _at_ 0x31;  // = 0x12
    unsigned char idata num2_lo _at_ 0x32;  // = 0xF0
    unsigned char idata num2_hi _at_ 0x33;  // = 0xE0
    unsigned char idata res_lo  _at_ 0x34;
    unsigned char idata res_hi  _at_ 0x35;
    unsigned char idata res_carry _at_ 0x36;
    
    CY = 0;   // Clear carry before addition
    res_lo = num1_lo + num2_lo;  // Add low bytes
    res_hi = num1_hi + num2_hi + CY;  // Add high bytes + carry
    res_carry = CY;  // Save final carry
    
    while(1);
}
```

**Step-by-Step Byte Arithmetic:**

**Low-byte addition:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= 0x34 + 0xF0 \\
  &= 0x124 \quad (\text{decimal: } 52 + 240 = 292)
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $292 > 255$, the result **wraps around** and the **Carry flag sets**:
- `res_lo = 0x24`
- `CY = 1`
- `AC` (auxiliary carry) = 1 (since $0x4 + 0xF0 = 0xF4$, low nibble overflow)

**High-byte addition (with carry):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= 0x12 + 0xE0 + 1 \quad (\text{carry from low byte}) \\
  &= 0xF3 \quad (\text{decimal: } 18 + 224 + 1 = 243)
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $243 \leq 255$, no further carry:
- `res_hi = 0xF3`
- `CY = 0` (final carry)

**Final result:** `res_lo = 0x24`, `res_hi = 0xF3` → 16-bit value = **0xF324** ✓
**Final carry (res_carry):** `0x00` ✓ (correct, since $0xF324 < 0x10000$)

**Why `CY = 0` is mandatory:**

```c
// WRONG (treats SUBB semantics):
res_lo = num1_lo + num2_lo;     // First call sets CY=1 (internally)
res_hi = num1_hi + num2_hi + CY; // CY is still 1 from previous ADD!
                                 // This is correct here ONLY because we want the carry

// For SUBTRACTION, you MUST clear CY:
unsigned char diff = 0x50 - 0x30;  // Internally: SUBB A, src
                                    // Performs 0x50 - 0x30 - CY(?)
                                    // If CY=1 (residual from somewhere), result = 0x1F (wrong!)
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 8051's C51 compiler automatically clears CY before `+` and sets it for `-` semantics via internal handling** — but for **inline assembly or hand-written routines**, you must explicitly clear CY before subtraction.

---

### 3.3 Program 3: Logic Operations — Bit Manipulation on Port 1

**Objective:** Read Port 1, set bit 7, clear bit 3, toggle bit 0, and write the result back to Port 1.

```c
#include <reg51.h>

void main(void) {
    unsigned char temp;
    temp = P1;       // Read Port 1
    temp = temp | 0x80;   // Set bit 7 (MSB)
    temp = temp & 0xF7;   // Clear bit 3 (mask: 1111 0111)
    temp = temp ^ 0x01;   // Toggle bit 0 (LSB)
    P1 = temp;       // Write back to Port 1
    while(1);
}
```

**Step-by-Step Bitwise Analysis (assume initial `P1 = 0x5A = 0101 1010`):**

**Step 1: Set bit 7 using OR:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{temp} &= 0x5A \quad | \quad 0x80 \\
           &= 0101\,1010 \quad | \quad 1000\,0000 \\
           &= 1101\,1010 \\
           &= 0xDA
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Clear bit 3 using AND with mask:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{mask} &= 0xF7 = 1111\,0111 \\
\text{temp} &= 0xDA \quad \& \quad 0xF7 \\
            &= 1101\,1010 \quad \& \quad 1111\,0111 \\
            &= 1101\,0010 \\
            &= 0xD2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Toggle bit 0 using XOR:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{temp} &= 0xD2 \quad \hat{} \quad 0x01 \\
           &= 1101\,0010 \quad \hat{} \quad 0000\,0001 \\
           &= 1101\,0011 \\
           &= 0xD3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final P1 = 0xD3** ✓

**Compiled Assembly Insight:**

```
        MOV  A, P1          ; Read P1 (read latch, not pin — important!)
        ORL  A, #80H        ; Set bit 7
        ANL  A, #0F7H       ; Clear bit 3
        XRL  A, #01H        ; Toggle bit 0
        MOV  P1, A          ; Write back
LOOP:   SJMP LOOP
```

---

### 3.4 Program 4: Arithmetic — Multiplication and Division Result Extraction

**Objective:** Multiply two 8-bit numbers, extract the 16-bit product; then divide the result and obtain quotient and remainder.

```c
#include <reg51.h>

void main(void) {
    unsigned char a = 0x32;   // 50 decimal
    unsigned char b = 0x0A;   // 10 decimal
    
    // The C51 compiler handles B register automatically
    // But for clarity, we use inline assembly
    unsigned char product_lo, product_hi, quotient, remainder;
    
    // Multiplication: 50 * 10 = 500 (0x01F4)
    product_lo = a * b;   // 500 % 256 = 244 (0xF4)
    product_hi = (a * b) >> 8;  // 500 / 256 = 1  (0x01)
    
    // Division: 500 / 13 = 38 remainder 6
    quotient  = (unsigned int)(product_hi * 256 + product_lo) / 13;
    remainder = (unsigned int)(product_hi * 256 + product_lo) % 13;
    
    while(1);
}
```

**Multiplication Analysis (50 × 10):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
50 \times 10 &= 500 \\
500_{10} &= 0x1F4_{16} \\
\text{product\_hi} &= 0x01 \\
\text{product\_lo} &= 0xF4
\end{aligned}
$$

**Why the split?** Because `MUL AB` produces a 16-bit result stored as `B:A` (B = high byte, A = low byte). In C, the compiler does this internally but presents a single 16-bit value to the user, which you can shift right by 8 to get the high byte.

**Division Analysis (500 ÷ 13):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
500 \div 13 &= 38 \text{ remainder } 6 \\
38_{10} &= 0x26_{16} \\
\text{quotient} &= 0x26 \\
\text{remainder} &= 0x06
\end{aligned}
$$

**Assembly Equivalent:**

```
        MOV  A, #32H        ; A = 50
        MOV  B, #0AH        ; B = 10
        MUL  AB             ; B:A = 0x01:0xF4
        MOV  30H, A         ; product_lo = 0xF4
        MOV  31H, B         ; product_hi = 0x01
        
        MOV  A, 31H         ; A = 0x01
        MOV  B, #00H        ; B = 0 (for 16-bit / 8-bit)
        ; ... division logic ...
        MOV  B, #0DH        ; B = 13
        DIV  AB             ; A = quotient, B = remainder
```

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Performance & Cycle Count

| Operation Type | C Construct | 8051 Instruction | Cycles (12 MHz) | Time (µs) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Register-to-register move | `R0 = R1` | `MOV A, R1; MOV R0, A` | 2 | 2 |
| Internal RAM read | `x = M[30H]` | `MOV A, 30H` | 1 | 1 |
| Internal RAM write | `M[30H] = x` | `MOV 30H, A` | 1 | 1 |
| External RAM read | `xdata ptr[]` | `MOVX A, @DPTR` | 2 | 2 |
| External RAM write | `xdata ptr[] = x` | `MOVX @DPTR, A` | 2 | 2 |
| Code memory read | `code c[]` | `MOVC A, @A+DPTR` | 2 | 2 |
| 8-bit addition | `a + b` | `ADD A, src` | 1 | 1 |
| 16-bit addition | (compiler generated) | 4 instructions | ~6 | 6 |
| 8-bit multiplication | `a * b` | `MUL AB` | 4 | 4 |
| 8-bit division | `a / b` | `DIV AB` | 4 | 4 |
| Bitwise AND/OR/XOR | `& \| ^` | `ANL/ORL/XRL A, src` | 1 | 1 |
| Increment (register) | `i++` | `INC A` | 1 | 1 |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Memory Architecture of the 8051

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CODE["Code Memory (ROM/Flash) - 64 KB"]
        C000["0000H - 0002H: Reset Vector"]
        C003["0003H - 002AH: Interrupt Vectors"]
        CXXX["002BH - FFFFH: User Program"]
    end

    subgraph IDATA["Internal Data Memory (idata) - 256 bytes"]
        R0R7["00H-1FH: Register Banks (4 banks x 8)"]
        BIT["20H-2FH: Bit-Addressable Area (128 bits)"]
        GEN["30H-7FH: General Purpose RAM (80 bytes)"]
        SFR["80H-FFH: Special Function Registers (SFRs)"]
    end

    subgraph XDATA["External Data Memory (xdata) - 64 KB"]
        XROM["0000H-FFFFH: External RAM (accessed via MOVX)"]
    end

    CPU[("CPU - 8051 Core\n(ALU + Registers + Control)")]

    CPU -->|PC fetches| CODE
    CPU <-->|MOV direct/@R0/@R1| IDATA
    CPU <-->|MOVX @DPTR/@R0/@R1| XDATA
```

> [!NOTE]
> The internal RAM (idata) from 80H–FFH is **physically the same address space as the SFRs**, but **accessed differently** in assembly. In C, the C51 compiler handles this distinction automatically through the `sfr`, `sbit`, and memory-space keywords (`data`, `idata`, `xdata`, `code`).

---

### 4.2 Program Flow — 16-bit Addition with Carry

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START(["Start: main()"]) --> INIT["Initialize variables\nnum1_lo=0x34, num1_hi=0x12\nnum2_lo=0xF0, num2_hi=0xE0"]
    INIT --> CLRC["CY = 0\n(clear carry flag)"]
    CLRC --> ADDL["res_lo = num1_lo + num2_lo\nADD A, src\nResult: A=0x24, CY=1"]
    ADDL --> ADDH["res_hi = num1_hi + num2_hi + CY\nADDC A, src\nResult: A=0xF3, CY=0"]
    ADDH --> STORE["res_carry = CY = 0"]
    STORE --> HALT["while(1):\nSJMP HALT"]
    HALT --> HALT

    style START fill:#90EE90
    style ADDL fill:#FFD700
    style ADDH fill:#FFD700
    style HALT fill:#FFB6C1
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR

        I3["Bit 3: 1"]

    end

    subgraph OUTPUT["Final P1 = 1101 0011 (0xD3)"]

        O1["Bit 1: 1"]
        O0["Bit 0: 1"]

    I7 --> OP1 --> O7
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*List the three categories of operations supported by the 8051 instruction set. Give one example C statement and its equivalent 8051 assembly instruction for each category.*

**Model Answer:**

The three categories are:

1. **Data Transfer Operations:** Move data between registers, RAM, and SFRs without alteration.
   - C: `P1 = 0x55;` → Assembly: `MOV P1, #55H`

2. **Arithmetic Operations:** Perform mathematical computations (add, subtract, multiply, divide).
   - C: `result = a + b;` → Assembly: `MOV A, R0; ADD A, R1; MOV R2, A`

3. **Logic Operations:** Perform bitwise AND, OR, XOR, NOT, and rotations.
   - C: `masked = P1 & 0x0F;` → Assembly: `MOV A, P1; ANL A, #0FH; MOV R2, A`

(3 marks: 1 for listing, 2 for examples)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*Why must the Carry flag (CY) be explicitly cleared before performing a subtraction operation on the 8051? What happens if it is not cleared?*

**Model Answer:**

The 8051's `SUBB` instruction performs the operation `A = A – src – CY`. Since subtraction **always** subtracts the carry flag along with the source operand:

- If CY = 0 (cleared): `A = A – src` (correct behavior).
- If CY = 1 (residual from a previous operation): `A = A – src – 1` (off-by-one error, incorrect result).

**Failure scenario:** If a previous `ADD` operation sets CY = 1, and we then execute `SUBB` without clearing, the result will be **1 less** than the intended mathematical subtraction.

**Remedy:** Always use `CY = 0;` (in C) or `CLR C` (in assembly) before a subtraction chain.

(3 marks)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Data Transfer + Arithmetic

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, PCEBL408]**

**(a)** Write an 8051 C program to transfer the contents of internal RAM locations `0x40` through `0x4F` (16 bytes) to external RAM locations starting at `0x2000`. Explain the memory spaces used and why the DPTR register is required. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Extend the above program to compute the **8-bit checksum** (sum modulo 256) of the 16 transferred bytes and store the checksum at external RAM location `0x2010`. Show the step-by-step byte calculation for a sample dataset. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

```c
#include <reg51.h>

unsigned char idata source[16] _at_ 0x40;        // Internal RAM 0x40-0x4F
unsigned char xdata destination[16] _at_ 0x2000; // External RAM 0x2000-0x200F

void main(void) {
    unsigned char i;
    for (i = 0; i < 16; i++) {
        destination[i] = source[i];
    }
    while(1);
}
```

**Memory Space Explanation (3 marks):**
- `idata` (internal RAM, 128 bytes) is fast (1-cycle access) but limited in size.
- `xdata` (external RAM, 64 KB) is large but slower (2-cycle access via MOVX).
- **DPTR (16-bit Data Pointer)** is required because external RAM addresses (e.g., 0x2000) exceed 8 bits; the DPTR holds the full 16-bit address (DPH = high byte, DPL = low byte).

**Loop Explanation (4 marks):**
- Loop counter `i` iterates 0 to 15.
- Each iteration: compute `&source[i]` (base 0x40 + offset), read byte into A.
- Set DPTR to `0x2000 + i`, write A via `MOVX @DPTR, A`.

#### Model Solution for (b):

```c
#include <reg51.h>

unsigned char idata source[16] _at_ 0x40;
unsigned char xdata destination[16] _at_ 0x2000;
unsigned char xdata checksum _at_ 0x2010;

void main(void) {
    unsigned char i, sum = 0;
    
    for (i = 0; i < 16; i++) {
        destination[i] = source[i];
        sum = sum + source[i];  // Accumulate; CY is handled by C51
    }
    checksum = sum;  // Store 8-bit sum (modulo 256 due to wrap)
    while(1);
}
```

**Step-by-Step Sample Calculation (3 marks):**

Sample data at `0x40-0x4F`: `0x10, 0x20, 0x30, 0x40, 0x50, 0x60, 0x70, 0x80, 0x90, 0xA0, 0xB0, 0xC0, 0xD0, 0xE0, 0xF0, 0x05`

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Sum} &= 0x10 + 0x20 + 0x30 + 0x40 + 0x50 + 0x60 + 0x70 + 0x80 \\
          &\quad + 0x90 + 0xA0 + 0xB0 + 0xC0 + 0xD0 + 0xE0 + 0xF0 + 0x05 \\
          &= 0x0F45 \quad (\text{decimal: } 3909) \\
\text{Checksum} &= 0x0F45 \mod 256 = 0x45 \quad (\text{decimal: } 69)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Storage:** `0x2010 = 0x45` ✓

**Why modulo 256?** Because `unsigned char sum` wraps around at 8 bits (0xFF + 1 = 0x00), effectively retaining only the lower 8 bits — which **is** the modulo 256 operation.

**Note on carry handling (1 mark):** The C51 compiler automatically discards the carry-out from `sum = sum + source[i]` when storing into an 8-bit `unsigned char`, so no explicit masking is required.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Logic + Arithmetic

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, PCEBL408 — Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** Write an 8051 C program to read a byte from Port 1, count the number of **set bits (1s)** in it, and display the count on Port 2. Use only data transfer, logic, and arithmetic operations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Demonstrate the bit-counting algorithm using the input byte `0x5B = 0101 1011`. Show every iteration of the algorithm and the final output. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

```c
#include <reg51.h>

void main(void) {
    unsigned char input, count = 0, i, mask;
    input = P1;   // Read from Port 1
    
    for (i = 0; i < 8; i++) {
        mask = 1 << i;             // Create a mask with one bit set
        if ((input & mask) != 0) { // Logic AND to test bit
            count++;               // Arithmetic increment
        }
    }
    P2 = count;  // Display count on Port 2
    while(1);
}
```

**Algorithm Explanation (4 marks):**
- The loop iterates 8 times, once per bit position (0 to 7).
- `mask = 1 << i` generates masks: `0x01, 0x02, 0x04, 0x08, 0x10, 0x20, 0x40, 0x80`.
- `input & mask` performs a **bitwise AND**; if the result is non-zero, the bit is set.
- `count++` is an **arithmetic increment**.
- Final `count` is written to Port 2 (data transfer to SFR).

**Code Optimization Note (1 mark):** A more efficient version uses the `&` trick: `count += input & 1; input >>= 1;` — but this mutates the original.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Input:** `input = 0x5B = 0101 1011`

**Iteration-by-iteration trace (3 marks):**

| i | mask = 1 << i | mask (hex) | input & mask (binary) | Non-zero? | count after |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0000 0001 | 0x01 | 0101 1011 & 0x01 = 0x01 | Yes | 1 |
| 1 | 0000 0010 | 0x02 | 0101 1011 & 0x02 = 0x02 | Yes | 2 |
| 2 | 0000 0100 | 0x04 | 0101 1011 & 0x04 = 0x00 | No | 2 |
| 3 | 0000 1000 | 0x08 | 0101 1011 & 0x08 = 0x08 | Yes | 3 |
| 4 | 0001 0000 | 0x10 | 0101 1011 & 0x10 = 0x10 | Yes | 4 |
| 5 | 0010 0000 | 0x20 | 0101 1011 & 0x20 = 0x00 | No | 4 |
| 6 | 0100 0000 | 0x40 | 0101 1011 & 0x40 = 0x40 | Yes | 5 |
| 7 | 1000 0000 | 0x80 | 0101 1011 & 0x80 = 0x00 | No | 5 |

**Verification (1 mark):** 0x5B in binary = 0101 1011 → number of 1s = **5** (bits 0, 1, 3, 4, 6) ✓

**Final output:** `P2 = 0x05 = 0000 0101` (3 marks)

**Binary representation of count (1 mark):**
- `0x05 = 0000 0101` — Port 2 pins P2.0 and P2.2 will be HIGH, all others LOW.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Typically Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to clear CY before subtraction** (-2 marks). The 8051's `SUBB` always subtracts CY.
> 2. **Confusing `|` (bitwise OR) with `||` (logical OR)** (-1 mark). In embedded C, the bitwise version is what you need for setting bits.
> 3. **Using `=` instead of `==` in comparisons** (-1 mark). `if (P1 = 0xFF)` *assigns*; `if (P1 == 0xFF)` *compares*.
> 4. **Declaring large arrays in `data` (default)** instead of `xdata` (-2 marks). Causes linker errors due to memory overflow.
> 5. **Reading the Port Pin vs. Port Latch**: `P1` in C reads the **latch** (output value), not the physical pin. To read the pin, use specific SFR bits or a different construct. This subtlety is a common exam question.
> 6. **Forgetting `while(1)` at the end of `main()`** (-1 mark). Causes the 8051 to execute undefined code after the function returns, which is a common cause of "works in simulator, fails on hardware" issues.
> 7. **Ignoring the `AC` (auxiliary carry) flag in BCD arithmetic** — when implementing BCD, you must use `DA A` after `ADD A, src`.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — 8051 C Programming (Data Transfer, Arithmetic, Logic)**

**Data Transfer Operations:**
- [ ] Internal RAM (`idata`): 128 bytes, 1-cycle access via `MOV direct` or `MOV @R0/@R1`.
- [ ] External RAM (`xdata`): 64 KB, 2-cycle access via `MOVX @DPTR` (16-bit address).
- [ ] Code memory (`code`): 64 KB, accessed via `MOVC` (program fetches, not modifiable at runtime).
- [ ] Ports P0–P3 are SFRs; writing to them drives output latches, reading returns latch value (not pin in standard C).

**Arithmetic Operations:**
- [ ] `ADD A, src` sets CY on unsigned overflow, OV on signed overflow.
- [ ] `ADDC A, src` used for multi-byte addition; CY is the propagated carry.
- [ ] `SUBB A, src` **ALWAYS** subtracts CY — clear CY first for plain subtraction.
- [ ] `MUL AB` → 16-bit result in `B:A`; OV=1 if high byte ≠ 0.
- [ ] `DIV AB` → quotient in A, remainder in B; OV=1 if B=0 (division by zero).
- [ ] `DA A` corrects BCD after `ADD` (not after `SUBB` or `INC`).
- [ ] `INC`/`DEC` do **not** affect the PSW flags.

**Logic Operations:**
- [ ] `&` (AND) → **mask/clear** bits; `(x & 0x0F)` keeps lower nibble.
- [ ] `\|` (OR) → **set** bits; `(x \| 0x80)` forces MSB high.
- [ ] `^` (XOR) → **toggle** bits; `(x ^ 0x01)` flips LSB.
- [ ] `~` (NOT) → 1's complement (bitwise inversion).
- [ ] `SWAP A` exchanges upper and lower nibbles (no direct C operator).
- [ ] Compare via `CJNE` or relational operators (`<`, `>`, `==`); affects carry flag for unsigned comparisons.
- [ ] Use `sbit` keyword for bit-level access to SFRs (e.g., `sbit LED = P1^0;`).

**Practical C51 Tips:**
- [ ] Always include `<reg51.h>` for SFR definitions.
- [ ] Use `_at_` keyword to place variables at specific memory addresses.
- [ ] Place large arrays in `xdata` to avoid overflowing internal RAM.
- [ ] Use `code` for constant lookup tables to save RAM space.
- [ ] End `main()` with `while(1);` to prevent code runaway.
- [ ] Use `sfr` and `sbit` for declaring custom SFR or bit addresses (advanced).

**Cycle-Count Cheat Codes:**
- [ ] `MOV A, Rn`: 1 cycle | `MOV direct, A`: 1 cycle
- [ ] `MOVX @DPTR, A`: 2 cycles | `MUL AB`: 4 cycles
- [ ] `DIV AB`: 4 cycles | `DA A`: 1 cycle

**Common Exam Traps:**
- [ ] CY must be cleared before `SUBB` (top reason for mark loss in subtraction problems).
- [ ] `SUBB` does not exist in plain subtraction; C51 handles it, but inline asm doesn't.
- [ ] Multiply produces **16-bit** result; high byte is in B, not discarded.
- [ ] `P1` reads the **latch**, not the physical pin (in standard 8051 C).
- [ ] `unsigned char` arithmetic wraps at 256 — this is **implicit modulo 256**, not a bug.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Compare Operations in 8051 C

### Formal Academic Definition
**Compare operations** in 8051 C programming are logical and relational evaluations performed between variables, constants, or Special Function Registers (SFRs) to make decisions in a program. They utilize C's relational operators (`==`, `!=`, `<`, `>`, `<=`, `>=`), logical operators (`&&`, `||`, `!`), and bitwise operators (`&`, `|`, `^`, `~`) to test the state of bits, bytes, or entire data words. The outcome of a comparison is always a Boolean result (`TRUE = 1` or `FALSE = 0`), which directs the program flow through control structures such as `if`, `if-else`, `switch-case`, or the conditional operator (`?:`).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** In 8051 C, comparison does NOT use the **8051 assembly `CJNE` instruction directly**. Instead, the Keil C51 compiler internally translates C relational operators into optimized assembly sequences involving `SUBB`, `JC`, `JNC`, `JZ`, `JNZ`, or `CJNE` machine instructions on the 8051 core.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine a **weighing balance (thulam)**. You place a known weight (reference value) on one pan and an unknown object (variable value) on the other. The balance tells you whether the unknown is **heavier, lighter, or equal**. Similarly, the 8051 compares two values in its ALU and tells you the relationship — *"Is P1 == 0xFF?"* or *"Is temperature < threshold?"*. The result is a yes/no (1/0) signal that decides what your program does next — like a traffic signal deciding whether to show green or red.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric:** A Boolean comparison result occupies **1 bit** in C, but the 8051 internally widens it to an **8-bit accumulator value**: `0x00` for FALSE and `0x01` for TRUE.

---

## 1.2 Rotate Operations in 8051 C

### Formal Academic Definition
**Rotate operations** in 8051 C are bit-manipulation techniques that circularly shift the bits of a byte either to the **left (rotate left)** or to the **right (rotate right)**, where the bit shifted out from one end re-enters from the opposite end. In standard ANSI C, rotation is achieved by combining the left-shift (`<<`) and right-shift (`>>`) operators with bitwise OR (`|`) to wrap the carry bit back into the byte. The 8051 natively supports rotation through its assembly instructions `RL A`, `RLC A`, `RR A`, and `RRC A`, which Keil C51 can invoke through intrinsic functions or inline assembly.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Picture a **circular conveyor belt with 8 seats** carrying passengers (bits 0–7). When the belt rotates **left by 1 position**, the passenger on seat 7 (leftmost) steps off the end and **boards seat 0** (rightmost), while everyone else shifts one seat to the left. Rotate right does the opposite. This is fundamentally different from a *shift* operation, where the bit that falls off is **lost** and a `0` enters from the other side.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metrics:**
> - **Rotate Left by 1:** `x = (x << 1) \vert (x >> 7);`
> - **Rotate Right by 1:** `x = (x >> 1) \vert (x << 7);`
> - **Rotate Left by n:** `x = (x << n) \vert (x >> (8 - n));`

---

## 1.3 Classification of Compare & Rotate Operations

| Operation Type | C Operator/Symbol | 8051 Assembly Equivalent | Bit Width |
|---|---|---|---|
| Equal to | `==` | `CJNE` + `JZ` | 8-bit / 16-bit |
| Not equal to | `!=` | `CJNE` + `JNZ` | 8-bit / 16-bit |
| Less than | `<` | `SUBB` + `JC` | 8-bit / 16-bit |
| Greater than | `>` | `SUBB` + check carry/zero | 8-bit / 16-bit |
| Bitwise AND (mask) | `&` | `ANL` | 8-bit |
| Bitwise OR (set) | `\|` | `ORL` | 8-bit |
| Bitwise XOR (toggle) | `^` | `XRL` | 8-bit |
| Logical NOT | `!` | `CPL A` + `JZ` | 1-bit result |
| Rotate Left | `<<` + `>>` combo | `RL A` | 8-bit |
| Rotate Right | `>>` + `<<` combo | `RR A` | 8-bit |
| Rotate Through Carry Left | combo + carry | `RLC A` | 9-bit (with CY) |

---

## 1.4 Why Compare & Rotate Operations Matter in 8051 Embedded Systems

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Significance:**
> - **Compare** is the backbone of **decision-making**: reading sensor thresholds, checking switch states, validating UART data, and triggering interrupts.
> - **Rotate** is the foundation of **bit-banging protocols**, **multiplication/division by 2**, **CRC checksum generation**, **LED chaser patterns**, **7-segment multiplexing**, and **data encryption algorithms**.
> - In a typical KTU lab viva, **rotate operations are tested through LED patterns** and **compare operations through conditional actuation of relays, motors, or buzzers** based on switch input.
