The extra 1 LSB is sacrificed due to the $2^n$ divisor convention. ### Derivation 2: Half-Scale Point **Claim:** When $V_{in} = V_{ref}/2$, the digital output is exactly $2^{n-1}$ (MSB = 1, all others = 0). $$ \begin{aligned} D &= \left\lfloor \dfrac{(V_{ref}/2) \cdot 2^n}{V_{ref}} \right\rfloor \\ D &= \lfloor 2^{n-1} \rfloor \\ D &= 2^{n-1} \end{aligned} $$ **For 8-bit, n=8:** $D = 2^7 = 128 = 10000000_2$. ✓ ### Derivation 3: Quantization Error The maximum quantization error is ±½ LSB: $$ E_{max} = \pm \dfrac{q}{2} = \pm \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2 \cdot 2^n} = \pm \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2^{n+1}} $$ **For 8-bit, 5V ADC:** $$E_{max} = \pm \dfrac{5}{2^9} = \pm \dfrac{5}{512} = \pm 9.77 \text{ mV}$$ ### Derivation 4: Signal-to-Quantization-Noise Ratio (SQNR) The theoretical SQNR for an ideal n-bit ADC is: $$ \text{SQNR (dB)} = 6.02 \cdot n + 1.76 $$ **For 8-bit:** $$\text{SQNR} = 6.02 \times 8 + 1.76 = 48.16 + 1.76 = 49.92 \text{ dB}$$ **For 10-bit:** SQNR = 61.96 dB. **For 16-bit:** SQNR = 98.08 dB (CD quality audio). ## 3.4 KTU Numerical Problem Set — Full Solutions ### Problem 1: Step Size and Digital Output > An 8-bit ADC has $V_{ref} = 5.12$ V. Find the step size and the digital output for $V_{in} = 3.2$ V. **Solution:** $$ \begin{aligned} q &= \dfrac{5.12}{2^8} = \dfrac{5.12}{256} = 0.02 \text{ V} = 20 \text{ mV} \\ D &= \left\lfloor \dfrac{3.2}{0.02} \right\rfloor = \lfloor 160 \rfloor = 160 \\ 160_{10} &= 10100000_2 \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > Note the clever choice of $V_{ref} = 5.12$ V: the step size becomes exactly 20 mV, which is easy to work with. This is a KTU examiner's favorite trick. ### Problem 2: Reconstructing Voltage from Digital Code > An 8-bit ADC outputs $D = 200$ with $V_{ref} = 5$ V. What is the analog input voltage? **Solution:** $$ \begin{aligned} q &= \dfrac{5}{256} = 19.53 \text{ mV} \\ V_{in} &= D \times q = 200 \times 19.53 \text{ mV} = 3.906 \text{ V} \end{aligned} $$ ### Problem 3: Conversion Time > ADC0808 is clocked at 640 kHz. Find the conversion time for 8-bit conversion. **Solution:** $$ \begin{aligned} t_{conv} &= \dfrac{n}{f_{CLK}} = \dfrac{8}{640 \times 10^3} \\ t_{conv} &= 12.5 \text{ µs} \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > The ADC0808 datasheet specifies 8 clock cycles per conversion, but the practical conversion time is around **100 µs** (it includes sampling and settling). The formula gives the *internal* SAR time only. ## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — ADC0804 vs ADC0808 | Feature | ADC0804 | ADC0808/0809 | |---|---|---| | Channels | 1 (VIN+ / VIN-) | 8 (IN0–IN7) | | Package | 20-pin DIP | 28-pin DIP | | Built-in Clock | Yes (RC network) | No (external clock required) | | Resolution | 8-bit | 8-bit | | Address Inputs | None | 3 (ADDA, ADDB, ADDC) | | Typical Use | Simple single-sensor lab experiments | Multi-channel data acquisition | ## 3.6 Conversion Sequence (State Machine for 8051) The complete handshake between the 8051 and the ADC0808 follows this state sequence: | Step | 8051 Action | ADC0808 Response | |---|---|---| | 1 | Set ADDA, ADDB, ADDC to select channel | — | | 2 | Pulse ALE HIGH then LOW | Latches the address internally | | 3 | Pulse START HIGH then LOW | Begins conversion, EOC goes LOW | | 4 | Wait / poll until EOC = HIGH | Conversion complete | | 5 | Set OE = HIGH | Places result on D0–D7 | | 6 | Read P1 → store result | Data available | | 7 | Set OE = LOW, ALE = LOW, START = LOW | Reset for next cycle | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Pitfall:** Many students forget to set **OE = HIGH** before reading Port 1. The output pins of the ADC are in **high-impedance state** until OE is asserted. This is the #1 reason for "ADC always reads 0" in lab. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — ADC0808 Interfaced with 8051 ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Analog Sensor
LM35 / Potentiometer] -->|0 to 5V| B[ADC0808
8-Channel ADC] B -->|D0-D7 8-bit Data| C[8051
Port P1] B -->|EOC Signal| D[8051
P3.5 or INT1] E[8051
P3.3 P3.4 P3.6] -->|ALE START OE Control| B F[8051
P3.0 P3.1 P3.2] -->|ADDA ADDB ADDC Address| B G[11.0592 MHz
Crystal] --> H[8051
Microcontroller] H -->|ALE divided by 3| B C --> I[LCD Display
16x2 or PC via UART] style A fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00 style B fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B style C fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400 style H fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#8B008B style I fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#B8860B ``` ## 4.2 Timing Diagram — Conversion Cycle ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant MCU as 8051 participant ADC as ADC0808 participant SENSOR as Analog Input Note over MCU,ADC: Step 1: Set Channel Address MCU->>ADC: ADDA=0, ADDB=0, ADDC=0 (Select IN0) MCU->>ADC: ALE = HIGH (Latch address) Note over MCU,ADC: Step 2: Start Conversion MCU->>ADC: START = HIGH (Begin conversion) ADC-->>ADC: Internal SAR begins ADC-->>MCU: EOC = LOW (Busy) Note over SENSOR,ADC: ADC samples VIN0 SENSOR->>ADC: Hold analog voltage stable Note over MCU,ADC: Step 3: Wait for EOC ADC-->>ADC: 8 clock cycles elapse ADC-->>MCU: EOC = HIGH (Conversion done) Note over MCU,ADC: Step 4: Read Result MCU->>ADC: OE = HIGH (Enable output) ADC-->>MCU: D0-D7 = 8-bit digital code MCU->>MCU: Store in variable (e.g., result) Note over MCU,ADC: Step 5: Cleanup MCU->>ADC: OE = LOW, ALE = LOW, START = LOW ``` ## 4.3 Internal Architecture of ADC0808 ```mermaid flowchart TB INPUT[Analog Inputs
IN0 IN1 IN2 IN3 IN4 IN5 IN6 IN7] --> MUX[8-to-1 Analog
Multiplexer] MUX --> SAR[Successive Approximation
Register SAR] SAR --> DAC[8-bit
R-2R DAC] DAC --> COMP[Comparator] COMP -->|Compare result| SAR SENSOR[VIN at MUX output] --> COMP CLK[External CLK] --> SAR CTRL[Control Logic
ALE START EOC OE] --> SAR SAR --> OUTPUT[8-bit Tri-State
Output Buffers D0-D7] OUTPUT --> BUS[Data Bus to 8051] CTRL --> EOC_OUT[EOC Pin to 8051] style INPUT fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00 style MUX fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#4682B4 style SAR fill:#98FB98,stroke:#228B22 style DAC fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#DC143C style COMP fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#8B008B style OUTPUT fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#B8860B style BUS fill:#87CEFA,stroke:#00008B ``` ## 4.4 8051 C Program — Polling-Based ADC Read (ADC0808 / IN0) ```c #include sbit ALE = P3^3; sbit START = P3^4; sbit EOC = P3^5; sbit OE = P3^6; sbit ADDA = P3^0; sbit ADDB = P3^1; sbit ADDC = P3^2; unsigned char adc_value; void delay(unsigned int time) { unsigned int i, j; for (i = 0; i < time; i++) for (j = 0; j < 1275; j++); } unsigned char read_adc(unsigned char channel) { // Step 1: Select channel (000 to 111) ADDA = (channel & 0x01); ADDB = (channel & 0x02) >> 1; ADDC = (channel & 0x04) >> 2; // Step 2: Latch the address ALE = 1; delay(1); ALE = 0; // Step 3: Start conversion START = 1; delay(1); START = 0; // Step 4: Wait for End of Conversion while (EOC == 1); // EOC goes LOW during conversion while (EOC == 0); // Wait for EOC to go HIGH // Step 5: Enable output and read data OE = 1; delay(1); adc_value = P1; // Read the 8-bit result OE = 0; return adc_value; } void main(void) { unsigned char value; while (1) { value = read_adc(0); // Read IN0 continuously P2 = value; // Display on Port 2 (LEDs) delay(100); } } ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Polling vs Interrupt:** The above code uses **polling** (continuous check of EOC). A more efficient approach uses **interrupts** by connecting EOC to INT1 (P3.3) of the 8051, freeing the CPU to perform other tasks during conversion. ## 4.5 Step-by-Step Flowchart — ADC Read Routine ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Start] --> B[Set ADDA ADDB ADDC
for desired channel] B --> C[ALE = 1 then 0
Latch address] C --> D[START = 1 then 0
Begin conversion] D --> E[EOC = LOW
Conversion in progress] E --> F{EOC = HIGH?} F -->|No| E F -->|Yes| G[OE = 1
Enable output buffers] G --> H[Read P1 into
adc_value variable] H --> I[OE = 0
Disable output] I --> J[Process / Display
adc_value] J --> K{Repeat
continuously?} K -->|Yes| B K -->|No| L[End] style A fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400 style E fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#DC143C style F fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00 style H fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B style L fill:#D3D3D3,stroke:#696969 ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfall Callout** > Students frequently lose marks on ADC interfacing questions due to: > 1. **Forgetting OE = 1** before reading Port 1 → output is tri-stated, reads garbage (–2 marks). > 2. **Confusing the order of START and ALE** — START must come AFTER ALE is latched (–1 mark). > 3. **Wrong EOC polling logic** — EOC goes LOW during conversion, then HIGH when done. The while loops `while(EOC==1); while(EOC==0);` are the correct idiom. Many students write a single `while(EOC==0);` which hangs the program if EOC is already HIGH (–2 marks). > 4. **Incorrect step size calculation** — using $(2^n - 1)$ instead of $2^n$ as the divisor (–1 mark). > 5. **Not mentioning the role of Vref** — examiners specifically look for **"Vref = 5V"** in your answer (–1 mark). --- ## PART A — 3 Mark Questions (Short Answer) ### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]** **What is an ADC? List any two types of ADCs.** **Model Answer:** An **ADC (Analog-to-Digital Converter)** is an electronic device that converts a continuous analog signal into a discrete digital code suitable for processing by a digital system such as a microcontroller. Two common types: 1. **Flash (Parallel) ADC** — Fastest type, uses $2^n - 1$ comparators. Used in video and high-speed applications. 2. **Successive Approximation Register (SAR) ADC** — Uses binary search algorithm. Offers good balance of speed and accuracy. Used in ADC0808, ADC0804 (commonly interfaced with 8051). > [!NOTE] > Other acceptable types: **Dual Slope ADC** (used in digital voltmeters), **Sigma-Delta ADC** (used in audio). --- ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **Calculate the step size of an 8-bit ADC with Vref = 5V. What is the digital output for an analog input of 1.2V?** **Model Answer:** $$ \begin{aligned} q &= \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2^n} = \dfrac{5}{256} = 0.01953 \text{ V} = 19.53 \text{ mV} \\ D &= \left\lfloor \dfrac{V_{in}}{q} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \dfrac{1.2}{0.01953} \right\rfloor \\ D &= \lfloor 61.44 \rfloor = 61 \\ 61_{10} &= 00111101_2 \end{aligned} $$ --- ## PART B — 14 Mark Questions (ESE Module Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > In KTU ESE, Part B Module-Internal Choice questions carry 14 marks each and are typically split as (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. --- ### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** #### Q.A.(a) **[7 Marks]** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]** **With a neat block diagram, explain the interfacing of ADC0808 with the 8051 microcontroller. List the functions of the ALE, START, EOC, and OE signals.** **Model Answer:** **Interfacing Diagram:** ``` +5V | Vref+ LM35 ----> IN0| ADC0808 |D0 --- P1.0 (8051) Sensor --> IN1| |D1 --- P1.1 ... --> IN7| |... ... | |D7 --- P1.7 | | P3.0 -------->|ADDA |ALE <--- P3.3 P3.1 -------->|ADDB |START<-- P3.4 P3.2 -------->|ADDC |EOC ----> P3.5 | |OE <--- P3.6 CLK|<-- 8051 ALE/3 GND ``` **Function of Control Signals:** | Signal | Full Form | Function | |---|---|---| | **ALE** | Address Latch Enable | A HIGH-to-LOW pulse latches the channel address (ADDA, ADDB, ADDC) into the ADC's internal multiplexer. | | **START** | Conversion Start | A HIGH-to-LOW pulse initiates the A/D conversion. The internal SAR begins the binary search. | | **EOC** | End Of Conversion | Goes LOW when conversion starts, returns HIGH when conversion is complete. Used by the 8051 to detect completion. | | **OE** | Output Enable | A HIGH signal enables the ADC's tri-state output buffers, allowing the 8051 to read the 8-bit digital result on D0–D7. | **Step-by-Step Process:** 1. 8051 sets ADDA, ADDB, ADDC to select the channel (e.g., 000 for IN0). 2. 8051 pulses ALE HIGH then LOW to latch the address. 3. 8051 pulses START HIGH then LOW to begin conversion. 4. EOC goes LOW (busy). 5. 8051 polls EOC; when HIGH, conversion is done. 6. 8051 sets OE = HIGH. 7. 8051 reads P1 (D0–D7) — this is the digital value. 8. 8051 sets OE = LOW. #### Q.A.(b) **[7 Marks]** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]** **Write an 8051 C program to read the analog input from channel IN0 of ADC0808 and display the digital value on Port 2 LEDs. Crystal = 11.0592 MHz.** **Model Answer:** ```c #include sbit ADDA = P3^0; sbit ADDB = P3^1; sbit ADDC = P3^2; sbit ALE = P3^3; sbit START = P3^4; sbit EOC = P3^5; sbit OE = P3^6; void delay_us(unsigned int us) { while (us--); } void delay_ms(unsigned int ms) { unsigned int i; for (i = 0; i < ms; i++) delay_us(122); // approx for 11.0592 MHz } unsigned char read_adc(void) { unsigned char value; // Select IN0 ADDA = 0; ADDB = 0; ADDC = 0; // Latch address ALE = 1; delay_us(1); ALE = 0; // Start conversion START = 1; delay_us(1); START = 0; // Wait for EOC: LOW during conversion, HIGH when done while (EOC == 1); // Wait for EOC to go LOW (conversion starts) while (EOC == 0); // Wait for EOC to go HIGH (conversion done) // Enable output OE = 1; delay_us(1); value = P1; // Read 8-bit digital data OE = 0; return value; } void main(void) { unsigned char adc_result; while (1) { adc_result = read_adc(); // Read from IN0 P2 = adc_result; // Display on Port 2 LEDs delay_ms(500); // Visible blink rate } } ``` **Explanation:** - The function `read_adc()` performs the complete handshake. - IN0 is selected by setting ADDC=ADDB=ADDA=0. - After EOC is detected HIGH, OE is asserted to enable output buffers. - The digital byte is read from P1 and returned. - In `main()`, the result is continuously displayed on Port 2 LEDs connected via current-limiting resistors (typically 330 Ω). - A 500 ms delay is added so that LEDs do not flicker too fast to be observed. --- ### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** #### Q.B.(a) **[7 Marks]** **[KTU University Exam — May 2023]** **Explain the successive approximation technique used in ADC0808. How many clock cycles are needed for an 8-bit conversion, and what is the conversion time if the clock frequency is 500 kHz?** **Model Answer:** **Successive Approximation Technique:** The ADC0808 uses a **Successive Approximation Register (SAR)** to perform a binary search on the analog input. The process is: 1. **Initialization:** At the start, the SAR register is cleared to `00000000`. 2. **Bit 7 (MSB) Test:** The SAR sets bit 7 to 1, making the trial code `10000000`. This code is fed to an internal **8-bit DAC**, producing a voltage $V_{DAC} = (128/256) \times V_{ref} = 2.5$ V (for Vref=5V). 3. **Comparison:** An internal **comparator** compares $V_{DAC}$ with $V_{in}$: - If $V_{in} > V_{DAC}$: bit 7 is kept as 1 (input is greater than half-scale). - If $V_{in} < V_{DAC}$: bit 7 is reset to 0. 4. **Bit 6 Test:** The SAR moves to bit 6 and repeats the process with the new trial code. 5. **Repeat for bits 5, 4, 3, 2, 1, 0.** Each test takes exactly one clock cycle. 6. **Result:** After 8 clock cycles, the SAR holds the final 8-bit digital code. **Number of Clock Cycles:** For an 8-bit conversion, exactly **8 clock cycles** are required (one per bit). **Conversion Time:** $$ \begin{aligned} t_{conv} &= \dfrac{n}{f_{CLK}} = \dfrac{8}{500 \times 10^3} \\ t_{conv} &= 16 \text{ µs} \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > The total **acquisition + conversion time** for ADC0808 is typically around **100 µs** including sampling and settling, but the SAR binary search itself takes 16 µs. #### Q.B.(b) **[7 Marks]** **[KTU University Exam — May 2023]** **Design an 8051-based digital voltmeter using ADC0808 to measure voltages in the range 0–5V and display the result on an LCD. Show the conversion formula and explain how the digital reading is converted to engineering units.** **Model Answer:** **Block Diagram:** ``` [Voltage to Measure] → [ADC0808 IN0] → [8051 P1] → [LCD 16x2] ↑ [Vref = +5V, GND = 0V] ↑ [CLK from 8051 ALE divided by 3] ``` **Hardware Connections:** - ADC0808 D0–D7 → 8051 P1.0–P1.7 - ADDA = ADDB = ADDC = GND (IN0 selected) - ALE → P3.3, START → P3.4, EOC → P3.5, OE → P3.6 - LCD RS → P2.0, RW → P2.1, EN → P2.2, Data → P0 - 10 kΩ potentiometer on IN0 to vary input voltage for testing **Conversion Formula (Voltmeter):** The digital reading $D$ (0 to 255) is converted back to voltage using: $$ V_{in} = D \times \dfrac{V_{ref}}{256} = D \times 0.01953 \text{ V} $$ To display in **millivolts** (better resolution on LCD): $$ V_{in\_mV} = D \times 19.53 \text{ mV} $$ **C Program:** ```c #include #include "lcd.h" // User-defined LCD routines sbit ALE = P3^3; sbit START = P3^4; sbit EOC = P3^5; sbit OE = P3^6; unsigned char read_adc(void) { ALE = 1; START = 1; // Latch + start in sequence ALE = 0; START = 0; while (EOC == 1); while (EOC == 0); OE = 1; return P1; } void main(void) { unsigned char D; unsigned int voltage_mV; char buf[16]; LCD_Init(); while (1) { D = read_adc(); voltage_mV = (unsigned int)D * 1953 / 100; // 19.53 mV scaled // Display as: "V = 2.450 V" LCD_Command(0x80); // Line 1 LCD_String("V = "); LCD_Char((voltage_mV / 1000) + '0'); // Tens of V LCD_Char('.'); LCD_Char(((voltage_mV / 100) % 10) + '0'); // First decimal LCD_Char(((voltage_mV / 10) % 10) + '0'); // Second decimal LCD_Char((voltage_mV % 10) + '0'); // Third decimal LCD_String(" V"); } } ``` **Key Points:** - $D \times 19.53$ mV gives the voltage in mV. - Integer division and modulo operators are used to extract digits for the LCD. - The voltmeter can resolve voltage changes of **19.53 mV** — sufficient for undergraduate lab experiments. --- ## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision — Read 3 times before the exam.** ### Core Concepts - **ADC** converts analog signals (continuous) to digital codes (discrete). - **ADC0808** is an 8-channel, 8-bit SAR ADC. **ADC0804** is a single-channel 8-bit SAR ADC. - The 8051 connects to the ADC via Port 1 (data) and Port 3 (control signals). ### Key Formulae - **Step Size:** $q = V_{ref} / 2^n$ → For 8-bit, 5V → **19.53 mV**. - **Digital Output:** $D = V_{in} / q$. - **Voltage from Code:** $V_{in} = D \times q$. - **Conversion Time:** $t_{conv} = n / f_{CLK}$. - **SQNR (dB):** $6.02n + 1.76$. ### Critical Control Signals (Memorize Order) 1. **Set Address** (ADDA, ADDB, ADDC) → 2. **Pulse ALE** → 3. **Pulse START** → 4. **Wait for EOC HIGH** → 5. **Set OE HIGH** → 6. **Read Port 1** → 7. **Set OE LOW**. ### EOC Polling Idiom ```c while (EOC == 1); // Wait for EOC to go LOW (start signal) while (EOC == 0); // Wait for EOC to go HIGH (conversion done) ``` ### Common Pitfalls (Avoid Marks Loss) - Forgetting to set **OE = 1** before reading. - Wrong step size formula ($2^n - 1$ instead of $2^n$). - Not specifying **Vref = +5V** in the answer. - Wrong clock frequency assumption (8051 ALE = $f_{osc}/6$). - Confusing ADC0808 (8-channel) with ADC0804 (1-channel). ### Clock Generation Trick - 8051 with 11.0592 MHz crystal → ALE = 1.8432 MHz → divide by 3 (using 74LS74 D-flip-flop) → 614.4 kHz for ADC0808. ### Exam-Ready Mnemonics - **"LASSORO"** = **L**atch address, **A**ssert **S**tart, **S**et **O**E, **R**ead P1, **O**E off. - **"ALE first, then START"** — never pulse START before ALE. - **"EOC LOW = busy, EOC HIGH = done"** — opposite logic to most other ICs. ### Application Domains to Mention Digital Voltmeter, Temperature Monitor, Data Acquisition, IoT Edge Nodes, Battery Management, Industrial Process Control. ### Quick-Reference Comparison - **Flash ADC** = fastest, lowest resolution, most expensive. - **SAR ADC** = balanced (used in ADC0808) — best for 8051. - **Dual Slope** = slowest, highest resolution, used in DVMs. - **Sigma-Delta** = high resolution audio (audio DACs, weighing scales).

topics.title: Assembly Language Program Using Bit Processing Instructions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCEBL408 - MICROCONTROLLER PROGRAMMING AND INTERFACING LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Assembly Language Program Using Bit Processing Instructions ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Bit Processing Instructions** in microcontroller assembly language are a specialized class of machine-level instructions that operate on individual **bits** (binary digits — either `0` or `1`) rather than entire bytes or words. In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** curriculum for `PCEBL408`, these instructions are central to Lab Experiment 2, where students write Assembly Language Programs (ALPs) for the **8051 family of microcontrollers** using the Keil µVision IDE. Bit processing encompasses instructions that: 1. **Set** a bit to logic `1` 2. **Clear** a bit to logic `0` 3. **Complement (toggle)** a bit between `0` and `1` 4. **AND, OR, XOR** bits with another bit or mask 5. **Test (jump if)** a bit is set or cleared 6. **Move** a bit to or from the Carry flag In 8051 architecture, **bit-addressable memory** spans two regions: - **Bit-addressable RAM**: Internal RAM locations `20H` to `2FH` (16 bytes × 8 bits = **128 addressable bits**) - **Special Function Register (SFR) bits**: Accumulator bits (`E0H–E7H`), B register bits (`F0H–F7H`), Port pins (`80H`, `90H`, `A0H`, `B0H`), and control register bits in `PSW`, `TCON`, `IE`, `IP`, etc. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Every bit-addressable location in the 8051 has a unique bit address from `00H` to `7FH` (for the bit RAM region) and from `80H` to `FFH` (for SFR bit region). A total of **256 bit addresses** are available. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy Imagine you have a **row of 128 light switches** on a control panel (this is your bit-addressable RAM). Each switch has a unique label — say `00`, `01`, `02`, … `7F`. Some switches control critical appliances (like a motor relay or LED), while others are just indicator flags (like "door open" or "timer expired"). Now imagine you can give commands like: - "**Turn ON switch number 42**" → `SETB 2AH` (since bit 42 lives in byte `2AH` at bit position 2) - "**Turn OFF switch number 05**" → `CLR 05H` - "**If switch 0E is ON, go to the alarm routine**" → `JB 0EH, ALARM` - "**Flip the state of switch 25**" → `CPL 19H` This is exactly what **bit processing instructions** do — they allow the programmer to manipulate **one tiny piece of information** (a single `0` or `1`) without disturbing the surrounding bytes. It is the assembly-language equivalent of reading or writing a **single checkbox** in a spreadsheet without altering the other columns. > [!NOTE] > **Why Bits Matter:** In embedded systems, real-world signals are inherently **binary** — a switch is either pressed or released, a sensor output is either HIGH or LOW, a motor is either ON or OFF. Bit-level instructions let the microcontroller handle these directly without wasting a full 8-bit register on a single fact. ### 1.3 Bit-Addressable Memory Map (8051) | Region | Address Range | Total Bits | Purpose | |--------|---------------|------------|---------| | Bit RAM | `20H–2FH` | **128 bits** (`00H–7FH`) | User-defined flags | | SFR bits | `80H–FFH` (bit-wise) | **128 bits** | Hardware control flags | | Carry Flag | `PSW.7` | 1 bit | Accumulator for boolean ops | | **Total** | — | **256 + 1** | Bit-addressable space | ### 1.4 Constant Reference Values - **Bit RAM size:** **128 bits** (located at byte addresses `20H`–`2FH`) - **SFR bit-addressable space:** **128 bits** (at addresses `80H`–`FFH`) - **Carry flag bit address:** **`D7H`** (PSW.7) - **Accumulator bit address:** **`E0H`** (full byte), bits `E0H–E7H` - **Port 0 bit addresses:** **`80H–87H`**, Port 1: `90H–97H`, Port 2: `A0H–A7H`, Port 3: `B0H–B7H` --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Categorical Classification of Bit Processing Instructions The 8051 bit instruction set can be organized into **five functional families**: #### Family 1: Bit Set and Clear These are the most fundamental bit operations. **`SETB bit`** — Set a specified bit to logic `1`. - Syntax: `SETB 2AH` or `SETB P1.0` - Operation: `bit ← 1` - Effect: Forces the target bit to HIGH regardless of its previous state - Use case: Turning ON an LED, asserting a control line, setting a status flag **`CLR bit`** — Clear a specified bit to logic `0`. - Syntax: `CLR 00H` or `CLR P3.7` - Operation: `bit ← 0` - Effect: Forces the target bit to LOW - Use case: Turning OFF a relay, de-asserting a handshake line, clearing a done flag > [!IMPORTANT] > **Mnemonic Trick:** **S**ETB = "Set to Binary 1"; **C**LR = "CLear to Zero (0)". #### Family 2: Bit Complement **`CPL bit`** — Complement (invert) a specified bit. - Syntax: `CPL P1.5` - Operation: `bit ← NOT(bit)` - Truth Table: - `0 → 1` - `1 → 0` - Use case: Toggling an LED at fixed intervals, software debouncing, pulse generation #### Family 3: Bit Logical Operations These instructions perform bit-wise logic between a direct bit and the Carry flag (`C`). | Instruction | Operation | Equivalent Equation | Truth Table Summary | |-------------|-----------|--------------------|--------------------| | `ANL C, bit` | AND with C | `C ← C AND bit` | Result is `1` only if both are `1` | | `ANL C, /bit` | AND with NOT | `C ← C AND (NOT bit)` | Result is `1` only if C is `1` and bit is `0` | | `ORL C, bit` | OR with C | `C ← C OR bit` | Result is `1` if either is `1` | | `ORL C, /bit` | OR with NOT | `C ← C OR (NOT bit)` | Result is `1` if C is `1` or bit is `0` | | `MOV C, bit` | Move bit to C | `C ← bit` | Direct transfer | | `MOV bit, C` | Move C to bit | `bit ← C` | Direct transfer | > [!NOTE] > **Why AND and OR with Carry?** Because the 8051 ALU does not allow direct bit-to-bit operations. The **Carry flag acts as the 1-bit accumulator** — you first move one operand into C, then perform the operation with the second operand. #### Family 4: Bit Conditional Jump Instructions These are decision-making instructions that alter program flow based on bit states. **`JB bit, rel`** — Jump if Bit is set (`bit == 1`). **`JNB bit, rel`** — Jump if Not Bit (`bit == 0`). **`JBC bit, rel`** — Jump if Bit is set, then Clear the bit (atomic test-and-clear). | Instruction | Condition for Jump | Side Effect | Typical Use | |-------------|-------------------|-------------|-------------| | `JB` | `bit = 1` | None | Polling a sensor; "if pressed, react" | | `JNB` | `bit = 0` | None | Waiting for an event; "wait until ready" | | `JBC` | `bit = 1` | **Clears bit** | Flag processing; "service and clear" | #### Family 5: Carry Flag Manipulation The Carry flag (`CY`, bit address `D7H`) is the **boolean ALU** of the 8051. - `SETB C` — Set Carry to `1` - `CLR C` — Clear Carry to `0` - `CPL C` — Complement Carry - `MOV C, bit` and `MOV bit, C` — Transfer between C and any bit ### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Instruction | Mnemonic | Bytes | Cycles | Operation | |-------------|----------|-------|--------|-----------| | Set bit | `SETB bit` | 2 | 1 | `bit ← 1` | | Clear bit | `CLR bit` | 2 | 1 | `bit ← 0` | | Complement bit | `CPL bit` | 2 | 1 | `bit ← ~bit` | | AND C with bit | `ANL C,bit` | 2 | 2 | `C ← C ∧ bit` | | AND C with NOT bit | `ANL C,/bit` | 2 | 2 | `C ← C ∧ ¬bit` | | OR C with bit | `ORL C,bit` | 2 | 2 | `C ← C ∨ bit` | | OR C with NOT bit | `ORL C,/bit` | 2 | 2 | `C ← C ∨ ¬bit` | | Move C from bit | `MOV C,bit` | 2 | 1 | `C ← bit` | | Move C to bit | `MOV bit,C` | 2 | 2 | `bit ← C` | | Jump if bit set | `JB bit,rel` | 3 | 2 | `if(bit) PC ← PC+rel` | | Jump if bit clear | `JNB bit,rel` | 3 | 2 | `if(!bit) PC ← PC+rel` | | Jump if bit set & clear | `JBC bit,rel` | 3 | 2 | `if(bit){bit←0; PC←PC+rel}` | **Bit Address Formula** for the 16-byte bit RAM (`20H` to `2FH`): $$\text{Bit Address} = (\text{Byte Address} - 20\text{H}) \times 8 + \text{Bit Position}$$ where `Bit Position ∈ {0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7}`. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Example:** Byte `2AH` = bit number `10 × 8 = 80`. The bits of `2AH` are addressed as `80H`, `81H`, `82H`, …, `87H`. ### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility | Application Domain | Role of Bit Instructions | |--------------------|--------------------------| | **Industrial Automation** | Reading limit-switch status, controlling solenoid valves | | **Automotive ECUs** | Managing individual relay outputs, sensor flag monitoring | | **Consumer Electronics** | Toggling LEDs, keypad scanning, mode-switch handling | | **Communication Protocols** | Building serial frames bit-by-bit (e.g., software UART TX) | | **Safety Systems** | Atomic flag handling in interrupt service routines | --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Bit Address Derivation — Worked Example **Problem:** Determine all 8 bit addresses belonging to internal RAM byte `28H`. **Step 1 — Identify the byte range.** The bit-addressable RAM occupies bytes `20H` to `2FH`. Byte `28H` falls within this range, so it has 8 individual bit addresses. **Step 2 — Compute the base bit address.** Apply the formula: $$\text{Bit Address} = (\text{Byte Address} - 20\text{H}) \times 8 + \text{Bit Position}$$ For byte `28H`: $$\text{Bit Address} = (28\text{H} - 20\text{H}) \times 8 + \text{Bit Position}$$ **Step 3 — Evaluate.** $$\begin{aligned} \text{Base} &= (28\text{H} - 20\text{H}) \times 8 \\ &= 08\text{H} \times 8 \\ &= 64 \text{ decimal} \\ &= 40\text{H} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 4 — Add each bit position (D0 to D7).** | Bit | Position | Address (Decimal) | Address (Hex) | |-----|----------|-------------------|---------------| | D0 | 0 | 64 | `40H` | | D1 | 1 | 65 | `41H` | | D2 | 2 | 66 | `42H` | | D3 | 3 | 67 | `43H` | | D4 | 4 | 68 | `44H` | | D5 | 5 | 69 | `45H` | | D6 | 6 | 70 | `46H` | | D7 | 7 | 71 | `47H` | **Step 5 — Verification.** Byte `28H` originally contains all the flags. After executing `CLR 43H`, bit `D3` of byte `28H` becomes `0`. Confirm by inspection: `43H = 40H + 3`, which lies in the bit region of byte `28H` (since `40H ≥ 00H` and `47H ≤ 7FH` for bit RAM). ✓ ### 3.2 Sample Program 1 — Toggling P1.0 with Bit Instructions **Aim:** Continuously toggle the LSB of Port 1 using `CPL` instruction and a delay loop. **Algorithm:** 1. Set initial value of P1.0 using `SETB`. 2. Enter infinite loop. 3. Complement P1.0. 4. Call a software delay. 5. Repeat. **Complete ALP (8051):** ```assembly ORG 0000H ; Reset vector LJMP MAIN ORG 0030H ; Main program starts here MAIN: SETB P1.0 ; Set P1.0 to logic 1 initially LOOP: CPL P1.0 ; Toggle P1.0 (complement its current state) ACALL DELAY ; Call delay subroutine SJMP LOOP ; Repeat forever ;--------- DELAY SUBROUTINE --------- DELAY: MOV R0, #0FFH ; Load outer counter D1: MOV R1, #0FFH ; Load inner counter D2: DJNZ R1, D2 ; Decrement R1; if not zero, stay DJNZ R0, D1 ; Decrement R0; if not zero, reload inner RET ; Return from subroutine END ``` **Trace Analysis:** | Step | Instruction | Effect on P1.0 | Carry (`CY`) | |------|-------------|---------------|--------------| | 1 | `SETB P1.0` | P1.0 ← `1` (LED OFF if active-low) | unchanged | | 2 | `CPL P1.0` | P1.0 ← `0` | unchanged | | 3 | `CPL P1.0` | P1.0 ← `1` | unchanged | | 4 | `CPL P1.0` | P1.0 ← `0` | unchanged | | … | (repeats) | toggling each iteration | — | **Why `CPL` instead of `SETB`/`CLR`?** `CPL` performs an automatic inversion. Each pass through the loop flips the bit — there is no need to know the current state. This produces a **50% duty-cycle square wave** at the pin. ### 3.3 Sample Program 2 — Logical Bit Composition (OR using Carry) **Aim:** Compute `P1.0 = P1.0 OR P1.1` without using byte-level OR. **Algorithmic Breakdown:** 1. Read `P1.1` into the Carry flag. 2. OR the Carry with `P1.0`. 3. Store Carry back into `P1.0`. **ALP:** ```assembly ORG 0000H MOV C, P1.1 ; C ← P1.1 (read first operand into accumulator) ORL C, P1.0 ; C ← C OR P1.0 (perform boolean OR) MOV P1.0, C ; P1.0 ← result END ``` **Truth-Table Verification:** | P1.0 (Before) | P1.1 (Before) | After `MOV C,P1.1` | After `ORL C,P1.0` | After `MOV P1.0,C` | |---------------|---------------|--------------------|--------------------|--------------------| | 0 | 0 | C = 0 | C = 0 OR 0 = **0** | P1.0 = **0** | | 0 | 1 | C = 1 | C = 1 OR 0 = **1** | P1.0 = **1** | | 1 | 0 | C = 0 | C = 0 OR 1 = **1** | P1.0 = **1** | | 1 | 1 | C = 1 | C = 1 OR 1 = **1** | P1.0 = **1** | **Conclusion:** The result matches the canonical OR truth table — proving that the bit-instruction sequence correctly mimics a byte-level `ORL` for the two LSBs. ✓ ### 3.4 Sample Program 3 — Polling and Conditional Execution **Aim:** Wait until P3.2 (INT0) goes HIGH, then set P1.7. **ALP:** ```assembly ORG 0000H CLR P1.7 ; Initialize P1.7 to 0 WAIT: JNB P3.2, WAIT ; If P3.2 == 0, loop back to WAIT SETB P1.7 ; P3.2 is now HIGH → set P1.7 HERE: SJMP HERE ; Trap CPU (do nothing) END ``` **Bit-Level Analysis:** | P3.2 state | `JNB P3.2, WAIT` decision | Action | |------------|--------------------------|--------| | 0 | Condition TRUE → jump to `WAIT` | Re-evaluate | | 1 | Condition FALSE → fall through | Execute `SETB P1.7` | This pattern is the **canonical polling loop** in 8051 firmware — it consumes 100% of CPU cycles while waiting but introduces zero interrupt overhead. ### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Instruction | Goal | Best Instruction(s) | Why | |------|---------------------|-----| | Force a bit ON | `SETB bit` | Single cycle, no dependency on prior state | | Force a bit OFF | `CLR bit` | Single cycle, no dependency on prior state | | Toggle a bit | `CPL bit` | Atomic inversion | | Test & branch (no clear) | `JB` / `JNB` | Non-destructive flag inspection | | Test & branch (clear flag) | `JBC` | Atomic test-and-clear prevents race conditions | | Boolean composition | `MOV C,bit` + `ANL/ORL C,...` + `MOV bit,C` | Carry acts as 1-bit accumulator | | Wait for an event | `JNB bit,HERE` (busy-wait) | Polling pattern | --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Bit-Addressable Memory Map (8051 Internal Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart TB A["Internal RAM 128 Bytes
00H to 7FH"]:::main A --> B["General Purpose
00H to 1FH
32 bytes, 4 banks x 8 registers"]:::gen A --> C["Bit Addressable
20H to 2FH
16 bytes x 8 bits = 128 bits
bit addresses 00H to 7FH"]:::bit A --> D["Scratchpad
30H to 7FH
80 bytes user RAM"]:::gen E["SFR Space
80H to FFH
128 special registers"]:::main E --> F["Port Registers
P0 80H, P1 90H
P2 A0H, P3 B0H
each bit-addressable"]:::port E --> G["PSW: D0H
Carry D7H, Aux D6H
OV D2, RS0/RS1 D3-D4"]:::ctrl E --> H["TCON: 88H
TF1 8FH, TR1 8EH
TF0 8DH, TR0 8CH
IE1 8BH, IT1 8AH
IE0 89H, IT0 88H"]:::ctrl E --> I["A Reg: E0H
E0H to E7H bit addr"]:::ctrl J["Carry Flag CY
bit address D7H
(Boolean Accumulator)"]:::flag C -. "Bit ops
SETB / CLR / CPL" .-> J F -. "Bit ops
JB / JNB / JBC" .-> J classDef main fill:#1e3a5f,stroke:#3b82f6,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px classDef gen fill:#f0f9ff,stroke:#0284c7,color:#0c4a6e classDef bit fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#d97706,color:#78350f,stroke-width:2px classDef port fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#16a34a,color:#14532d classDef ctrl fill:#fce7f3,stroke:#be185d,color:#831843 classDef flag fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#dc2626,color:#7f1d1d,stroke-width:2px ``` ### 4.2 Bit Instruction Execution Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR A["Program Counter PC
Fetches Instruction"]:::pc A --> B{"Decode Opcode
Bit or Byte?"}:::decode B -->|"Bit Mnemonic
detected"| C["Read Bit Address
from Operand"]:::read B -->|"Byte Mnemonic"| Z["Execute Byte-Level ALU"]:::byte C --> D["Access Internal RAM
or SFR via Bit Decoder"]:::access D --> E["Apply Operation
SET CLR CPL ANL ORL MOV"]:::op E --> F["Write Back to Bit Location"]:::write F --> G["Increment PC
Next Instruction"]:::next Z --> H["Standard Byte Pipeline"]:::byte2 G --> H classDef pc fill:#1e3a5f,stroke:#3b82f6,color:#ffffff classDef decode fill:#7c3aed,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#ffffff classDef read fill:#0891b2,stroke:#67e8f9,color:#ffffff classDef access fill:#d97706,stroke:#fcd34d,color:#ffffff classDef op fill:#dc2626,stroke:#fca5a5,color:#ffffff classDef write fill:#16a34a,stroke:#86efac,color:#ffffff classDef next fill:#0284c7,stroke:#7dd3fc,color:#ffffff classDef byte fill:#6b7280,stroke:#d1d5db,color:#ffffff classDef byte2 fill:#6b7280,stroke:#d1d5db,color:#ffffff ``` ### 4.3 Carry Flag as 1-Bit ALU — Functional Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph S1["Bit Operand Source"] OP["Any Bit-Addressable
Location
20H-2FH or SFR"] end subgraph S2["1-Bit ALU"] CY["Carry Flag CY
D7H of PSW
Acts as Accumulator"] LOGIC["Boolean Logic Unit
AND OR NOT XOR"] end OP -->|"Source Bit"| LOGIC CY -->|"Operand A"| LOGIC LOGIC -->|"Result written
back to CY"| CY CY -->|"MOV bit,C
write back"| OP classDef source fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#d97706,color:#78350f classDef alu fill:#fce7f3,stroke:#be185d,color:#831843 classDef acc fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#dc2626,color:#7f1d1d,stroke-width:2px classDef logic fill:#ddd6fe,stroke:#7c3aed,color:#3b0764 class S1 source class S2 alu class CY acc class LOGIC logic ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A — 3-Mark Short Questions **Q1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`** *What is meant by bit-addressable memory in the 8051 microcontroller? Identify its location.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** Bit-addressable memory is the region of internal RAM where **each individual bit** can be accessed using its unique 8-bit address through special bit-processing instructions. In the 8051, this memory is located in two areas: (i) **internal RAM bytes `20H` to `2FH`**, providing 128 bit addresses from `00H` to `7FH`, and (ii) **bit-addressable SFRs** (such as `ACC`, `B`, `PSW`, `P0`–`P3`, `TCON`, `IE`, `IP`) providing an additional 128 bit addresses from `80H` to `FFH`. The key advantage is that the programmer can manipulate a single bit (e.g., `SETB P1.0`) without disturbing the remaining bits of the same byte. **Q2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`** *List any four bit-processing instructions of the 8051 with one-line descriptions.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** 1. **`SETB bit`** — Sets the specified bit to logic 1. (Example: `SETB P1.0` makes P1.0 HIGH.) 2. **`CLR bit`** — Clears the specified bit to logic 0. (Example: `CLR P3.7` makes P3.7 LOW.) 3. **`CPL bit`** — Complements (inverts) the specified bit. (Example: `CPL P1.5` toggles P1.5.) 4. **`JB bit, rel`** — Jumps to the target label if the bit is set (HIGH); otherwise, falls through to the next instruction. --- ### Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice) #### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]` **(a)** Explain the bit-addressable memory of the 8051 with a neat memory map. List all bit-processing instruction categories with one example each. **[7 marks]** **(b)** Write an 8051 ALP to monitor Port pin P3.3 continuously. Whenever P3.3 is HIGH, increment R0 and set P1.0. When P3.3 is LOW, decrement R0 and clear P1.0. Show the algorithm, program, and a sample trace for three input transitions. **[7 marks]** **Model Solution (a):** **Bit-Addressable Memory Map:** | Byte Address | Bit Addresses (D0–D7) | Region | |--------------|------------------------|--------| | `20H` | `00H–07H` | Bit RAM | | `21H` | `08H–0FH` | Bit RAM | | `22H` | `10H–17H` | Bit RAM | | `23H` | `18H–1FH` | Bit RAM | | `24H` | `20H–27H` | Bit RAM | | `25H` | `28H–2FH` | Bit RAM | | `26H` | `30H–37H` | Bit RAM | | `27H` | `38H–3FH` | Bit RAM | | `28H` | `40H–47H` | Bit RAM | | `29H` | `48H–4FH` | Bit RAM | | `2AH` | `50H–57H` | Bit RAM | | `2BH` | `58H–5FH` | Bit RAM | | `2CH` | `60H–67H` | Bit RAM | | `2DH` | `68H–6FH` | Bit RAM | | `2EH` | `70H–77H` | Bit RAM | | `2FH` | `78H–7FH` | Bit RAM | **Instruction Categories with Examples:** | Category | Example | Operation | |----------|---------|-----------| | Bit set | `SETB P1.0` | P1.0 ← 1 | | Bit clear | `CLR 00H` | bit `00H` ← 0 | | Bit complement | `CPL P1.5` | P1.5 ← ~P1.5 | | Bit AND/OR with C | `ANL C,P3.2` | C ← C ∧ P3.2 | | Bit move | `MOV C,P3.3` | C ← P3.3 | | Conditional jump on bit | `JB P3.2,LABEL` | jump if P3.2 == 1 | | Conditional jump on bit clear | `JNB P3.3,LABEL` | jump if P3.3 == 0 | | Test and clear | `JBC 00H,LABEL` | jump if bit `00H` set, then clear it | **Model Solution (b):** **Algorithm:** 1. Initialize R0 to `00H`, P1.0 to LOW. 2. Check the state of P3.3. 3. If P3.3 is HIGH (set): increment R0 and `SETB P1.0`. 4. If P3.3 is LOW (clear): decrement R0 and `CLR P1.0`. 5. Repeat forever. **ALP:** ```assembly ORG 0000H MOV R0, #00H ; Initialize counter to 0 CLR P1.0 ; Initialize P1.0 to LOW LOOP: JB P3.3, HIGH_PIN ; If P3.3 == 1, branch to HIGH_PIN ;--- P3.3 is LOW branch --- DEC R0 ; R0 ← R0 - 1 CLR P1.0 ; P1.0 ← 0 SJMP LOOP ; Repeat HIGH_PIN: ;--- P3.3 is HIGH branch --- INC R0 ; R0 ← R0 + 1 SETB P1.0 ; P1.0 ← 1 SJMP LOOP ; Repeat END ``` **Sample Trace Table — 3 Input Transitions:** | Iteration | P3.3 (Before) | Branch Taken | R0 (After) | P1.0 (After) | |-----------|---------------|--------------|------------|--------------| | Start | — | — | `00H` | `0` | | 1 | 1 (HIGH) | `HIGH_PIN` | `01H` | `1` | | 2 | 1 (HIGH) | `HIGH_PIN` | `02H` | `1` | | 3 | 0 (LOW) | `LOOP → DEC` | `01H` | `0` | **Verification:** R0 increments on HIGH and decrements on LOW; P1.0 mirrors the P3.3 state. Logic matches the requirement. ✓ --- #### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]` **(a)** With the help of a block diagram, explain how the Carry flag is used as a 1-bit accumulator in 8051 bit-processing operations. Write the sequence of instructions to perform `P1.5 = P1.5 OR NOT(P3.4)`. **[7 marks]** **(b)** Write an 8051 ALP that reads bit P3.2 and bit P3.3. If both are HIGH, set P1.0. If exactly one is HIGH, clear P1.0. If both are LOW, toggle P1.0. Use only bit-processing instructions. Show the truth table and code. **[7 marks]** **Model Solution (a):** **The Carry Flag as 1-Bit Accumulator:** The 8051 ALU does not allow direct bit-to-bit boolean operations. To overcome this, the architecture designates the **Carry flag (`CY`)** as a **1-bit accumulator**: - One operand is loaded into `CY` using `MOV C,bit`. - The second operand is combined with `CY` using `ANL C,bit` or `ORL C,bit`. - The result, which resides in `CY`, is written back to a bit location using `MOV bit,C`. This three-step pattern (`LOAD → OPERATE → STORE`) is the bit-level analog of the standard byte-level pattern (`MOV A,operand → ORL A,operand → MOV result,A`). **Computation Sequence for `P1.5 = P1.5 OR NOT(P3.4)`:** ```assembly MOV C, P3.4 ; C ← P3.4 CPL C ; C ← NOT(P3.4) ; complement the loaded value ORL C, P1.5 ; C ← C OR P1.5 ; perform OR with destination MOV P1.5, C ; P1.5 ← result ``` **Truth-Table Verification:** | P1.5 | P3.4 | NOT(P3.4) | P1.5 OR NOT(P3.4) | Final P1.5 | |------|------|-----------|-------------------|------------| | 0 | 0 | 1 | 0 OR 1 = **1** | **1** | | 0 | 1 | 0 | 0 OR 0 = **0** | **0** | | 1 | 0 | 1 | 1 OR 1 = **1** | **1** | | 1 | 1 | 0 | 1 OR 0 = **1** | **1** | **Model Solution (b):** **Decision Truth Table:** | P3.3 | P3.2 | Condition | Action on P1.0 | |------|------|-----------|----------------| | 0 | 0 | Both LOW | Toggle P1.0 | | 0 | 1 | Exactly one HIGH | Clear P1.0 | | 1 | 0 | Exactly one HIGH | Clear P1.0 | | 1 | 1 | Both HIGH | Set P1.0 | **Algorithm:** 1. Check if `P3.3 AND P3.2` is true using `ANL` with Carry. 2. If Carry remains set → both HIGH → `SETB P1.0`. 3. Else, test if either is HIGH (`ORL`) → `CLR P1.0`. 4. Else (both LOW) → `CPL P1.0`. **ALP:** ```assembly ORG 0000H CLR P1.0 ; Initialize P1.0 to 0 CHECK: ;--- Test 1: Are BOTH P3.3 and P3.2 HIGH? --- MOV C, P3.3 ; C ← P3.3 ANL C, P3.2 ; C ← C AND P3.2 JC BOTH_HIGH ; if C == 1, both are HIGH ;--- Test 2: Is ANY of P3.3 / P3.2 HIGH? --- MOV C, P3.3 ; C ← P3.3 ORL C, P3.2 ; C ← C OR P3.2 JC ONE_HIGH ; if C == 1, exactly one (or logic says at least one) ;--- Else: both LOW → toggle P1.0 --- CPL P1.0 SJMP CHECK BOTH_HIGH: SETB P1.0 SJMP CHECK ONE_HIGH: CLR P1.0 SJMP CHECK END ``` **Verification Trace:** | P3.3 | P3.2 | After `ANL C,P3.2` | After `ORL C,P3.2` | Branch Taken | Final P1.0 | |------|------|---------------------|--------------------|--------------|-------------| | 0 | 0 | C = 0 | C = 0 | Else → `CPL` | toggled | | 0 | 1 | C = 0 | C = 1 | `ONE_HIGH` | 0 | | 1 | 0 | C = 0 | C = 1 | `ONE_HIGH` | 0 | | 1 | 1 | C = 1 | (skipped) | `BOTH_HIGH` | 1 | All four input combinations produce the required behavior. ✓ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Confusing bit addresses with byte addresses** — `SETB 20H` is **invalid**. Use `SETB 00H` (the bit at the start of byte `20H`) or `SETB P1.0`. > 2. **Forgetting to load one operand into Carry first** — `ANL C, P1.0` requires C to already hold a value from a prior `MOV C, ...`. > 3. **Using `JBC` without realizing it clears the bit** — students lose marks when a flag is unintentionally cleared. > 4. **Not testing all four input combinations** — a 14-mark question on bit logic without a complete truth table is incomplete. > 5. **Writing `MOV P1.0, P1.1`** — there is no direct bit-to-bit MOV in 8051. Always go through Carry: `MOV C,P1.1` → `MOV P1.0,C`. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Bit-addressable RAM** occupies bytes **`20H–2FH`** (16 bytes → 128 bits, addresses `00H–7FH`). - **Bit-addressable SFRs** contribute another 128 bit addresses (`80H–FFH`). - The **Carry flag (`CY`, bit `D7H`)** acts as the **1-bit accumulator** for boolean operations. - All bit operations require a **3-step idiom**: `MOV C,bit` → `ANL/ORL C,...` → `MOV bit,C`. - **`SETB`** forces a bit HIGH; **`CLR`** forces it LOW; **`CPL`** inverts it. - **`JB`** (jump if set), **`JNB`** (jump if not set), **`JBC`** (jump and clear) are the three conditional-bit jumps. - **`JBC`** is **atomic** — it tests and clears the bit in a single instruction; preferred for flag servicing in ISRs. - For bit RAM byte `B` and bit position `P`: $$\text{Bit Address} = (B - 20\text{H}) \times 8 + P$$ - Always include a **complete truth-table verification** in KTU exams — it is worth 2–3 marks on its own. - Programs must follow the pattern: **`ORG 0000H`**, label declarations, instructions, **`END`**.

topics.title: Assembly Language Program for Programming Ports of 8051
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEBL408 - MICROCONTROLLER PROGRAMMING AND INTERFACING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Assembly Language Program for Programming Ports of 8051

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Compliant):**
> **Port Programming in 8051** refers to the process of configuring and manipulating the **four 8-bit bidirectional I/O ports** (P0, P1, P2, P3) of the Intel 8051 microcontroller using its internal **Special Function Registers (SFRs)** mapped at addresses **80H, 90H, A0H, and B0H** respectively, through structured **Assembly Language Instructions** (MOV, SETB, CLR, ANL, ORL, CPL) executed by the 8051 CPU.

The 8051 has **four 8-bit I/O ports** providing **32 I/O lines** that allow the microcontroller to interface with external peripheral devices such as LEDs, switches, seven-segment displays, LCDs, motors, relays, and sensors.

### Port SFR Address Map (KTU Mandatory Recall)

| Port | SFR Name | Address (Hex) | Bit-Addressable | Alternate Function |
|------|----------|---------------|-----------------|---------------------|
| **P0** | P0 | **80H** | Yes (80H–87H) | AD0–AD7 (Multiplexed Address/Data bus) |
| **P1** | P1 | **90H** | Yes (90H–97H) | None (Pure I/O) |
| **P2** | P2 | **A0H** | Yes (A0H–A7H) | A8–A15 (Higher Address bus) |
| **P3** | P3 | **B0H** | Yes (B0H–B7H) | RXD, TXD, INT0, INT1, T0, T1, WR, RD |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Building Switchboard

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Electrical Switchboard Room:**
> Imagine the 8051 microcontroller is a **building's main control room**, and the **four ports are four electrical switchboards mounted on the wall**. Each switchboard has **8 switches (8 bits)**. Each switch controls a separate device (light, fan, motor).
>
> - **P0** → Switchboard 1 (a "shared" board that also connects to the building's main highway for data)
> - **P1** → Switchboard 2 (a "private" board, only for local devices)
> - **P2** → Switchboard 3 (a "shared" board for upper floors/addressing)
> - **P3** → Switchboard 4 (a "multipurpose" board with special VIP functions like intercom, alarms)
>
> **Assembly Language Instructions** are the **electrician's commands**: "Set switch 3 ON" (SETB P1.3), "Turn OFF all switches on board 1" (CLR P0), "Toggle switch 5" (CPL P2.5).

## 1.3 Key Engineering Constants & Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical 8051 Port Specifications (Board Exam Favorites):**
> - **Port Latch SFRs:** All 4 ports are **bit-addressable** (a unique feature of 8051 — among the few 8-bit microcontrollers).
> - **P0 Internal Pull-up:** **NONE** (open-drain) — requires **10 KΩ external pull-up resistors** for stable HIGH output.
> - **P1, P2, P3 Internal Pull-up:** **YES** (~30–40 KΩ weak internal pull-ups).
> - **Output Current per pin:** **10 mA** (sink/source typical, 20 mA max sink on P0).
> - **Total current per port:** **26 mA** maximum (all 8 pins combined).
> - **Crystal Frequency (fosc):** Typically **11.0592 MHz** (chosen to generate exact 9600 baud rate for UART).
> - **Machine Cycle:** **12 oscillator periods** → 1 machine cycle at 11.0592 MHz = **1.085 µs**.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architecture of 8051 Ports — The Internal D-Latch Model

Every pin of every port is connected internally through:
1. **A D-Latch (SFR bit)** — stores the *value written* by the CPU.
2. **A Transistor Driver Circuit** — drives the actual physical pin.
3. **An Input Buffer** — reads the physical pin state.

### Read-Modify-Write vs Pin-Read Distinction (CRITICAL)

> [!WARNING]
> **The Most Common 8051 Port Programming Trap:**
> Instructions like `INC P1`, `ANL P1, A`, `CPL P1.x`, `DJNZ P1, LABEL` use the **SFR Latch** (NOT the physical pin) for the read operation. If an output pin is being driven low by an *external* load, the latch still reads the *old* CPU-written value, not the pin state. This is the famous **"Read-Modify-Write Problem"**.

## 2.2 Port Alternate Functions (The 8051 Dual-Nature Ports)

| Port Pin | Alternate Function | When Activated |
|----------|--------------------|----------------|
| **P3.0** | **RxD** (Serial Input) | When using UART |
| **P3.1** | **TxD** (Serial Output) | When using UART |
| **P3.2** | **INT0** (External Interrupt 0) | When external interrupts used |
| **P3.3** | **INT1** (External Interrupt 1) | When external interrupts used |
| **P3.4** | **T0** (Timer 0 Input) | When Timer 0 in counter mode |
| **P3.5** | **T1** (Timer 1 Input) | When Timer 1 in counter mode |
| **P3.6** | **WR** (External Write Strobe) | When external RAM access |
| **P3.7** | **RD** (External Read Strobe) | When external RAM access |
| **P0.0–P0.7** | AD0–AD7 (Multiplexed) | When accessing external memory |
| **P2.0–P2.7** | A8–A15 (High Address bus) | When accessing external memory |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** During **external memory access**, P0 and P2 are *automatically* used for address/data bus operations by the 8051 hardware. The SFR latch value is *temporarily overridden* by the hardware control signals. This is why external memory interfacing "loses" P0 and P2 as general-purpose I/O.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### Time-Delay Calculation Formulas (Lab Exam Essentials)

| Formula | Expression | Units | Use Case |
|---------|------------|-------|----------|
| **Machine Cycle Period** | $T_{MC} = \dfrac{12}{f_{osc}}$ | seconds | Period of one machine cycle |
| **Total Delay (1-loop)** | $T_d = N_{cycles} \times T_{MC}$ | seconds | Single nested loop delay |
| **1-Loop Delay Count** | $T_d = [(R \times 3) + 3] \times T_{MC} \times 1$ | seconds | DJNZ loop (1 machine cycle DJNZ + 2 cycle MOV) |
| **2-Loop (Nested) Delay** | $T_d = [R_{outer} \times ((R_{inner} \times 3) + 3) + 3] \times T_{MC}$ | seconds | KTU standard delay pattern |
| **Baud Rate (UART)** | $Baud = \dfrac{2^{SMOD}}{32} \times \dfrac{f_{osc}}{12 \times (256 - TH1)}$ | bps | For SMOD=0, fosc=11.0592 MHz, TH1=253 → 9600 bps |
| **TH1 reload for 9600** | $TH1 = 256 - \dfrac{f_{osc}}{384 \times Baud}$ | decimal | Serial comms setup |

> **Sanity Check (KTU 11.0592 MHz Crystal):**
> $T_{MC} = 12 \div 11.0592 \times 10^6 = 1.085\,\mu s$

### Instruction Cycle Costs (Most-used in Port Programs)

| Instruction | Machine Cycles | Time @ 11.0592 MHz |
|-------------|----------------|---------------------|
| `MOV A, #data` | 1 | 1.085 µs |
| `MOV P1, A` | 1 | 1.085 µs |
| `CPL P1.0` | 1 | 1.085 µs |
| `DJNZ Rn, label` | 2 | 2.17 µs |
| `LCALL delay` | 2 | 2.17 µs |
| `RET` | 2 | 2.17 µs |
| `ACALL delay` | 2 | 2.17 µs |
| `SJMP label` | 2 | 2.17 µs |
| `NOP` | 1 | 1.085 µs |

## 2.4 Engineering Utility in the Real World

- **Industrial Automation:** Port pins drive relays controlling heavy machinery (via optocoupler isolation).
- **Embedded Consumer Electronics:** Microwave oven control panels, washing machine user interfaces.
- **Automotive ECUs:** Reading sensor switches and controlling actuators.
- **IoT Edge Devices:** Direct GPIO control before migrating to modern ARM Cortex-M based MCUs.
- **Educational Tool:** The 8051 is the *de facto* teaching microcontroller in Indian engineering curricula (KTU, VTU, Anna University) because of its clean orthogonality between SFRs and physical pins.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 KTU Standard Assembly Program #1 — LED Blinking

### Problem Statement
> Write an 8051 assembly language program to toggle **all 8 bits of Port P1** continuously with a delay of ~100 ms between each toggle. Assume crystal frequency = **11.0592 MHz**.

### Step-by-Step Derivation of the Delay Constant

> [!NOTE]
> **Goal:** Compute the inner-loop register value $R$ such that a nested delay subroutine produces approximately **100 ms**.

**Step 1:** Convert target delay to machine cycles.

$$T_{MC} = \frac{12}{f_{osc}} = \frac{12}{11.0592 \times 10^6} = 1.085\,\mu s$$

$$N_{cycles} = \frac{T_{delay}}{T_{MC}} = \frac{100 \times 10^{-3}}{1.085 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 92{,}165\,\text{machine cycles}$$

**Step 2:** Allocate 2 nested loops. Outer loop R2 = FFH (255) and solve for R1 = R.

$$T_{delay} = [R_2 \times ((R \times 3) + 3) + 3] \times T_{MC}$$

Substituting the 2 cycles consumed by LCALL and RET, and accounting for overhead:

$$100 \times 10^{-3} = [255 \times ((R \times 3) + 3) + 3 + 2] \times 1.085 \times 10^{-6}$$

**Step 3:** Simplify.

$$100 \times 10^{-3} = [255 \times 3R + 765 + 3 + 2] \times 1.085 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$100 \times 10^{-3} = [765R + 770] \times 1.085 \times 10^{-6}$$

**Step 4:** Solve for $R$.

$$765R = \frac{100 \times 10^{-3}}{1.085 \times 10^{-6}} - 770 \approx 92{,}165 - 770 = 91{,}395$$

$$R = \frac{91{,}395}{765} \approx 119.5 \approx 120 = 78H$$

**Step 5:** Verified delay.

$$T_{actual} = [255 \times ((120 \times 3) + 3) + 5] \times 1.085\,\mu s$$

$$T_{actual} = [255 \times 363 + 5] \times 1.085\,\mu s = 92{,}570 \times 1.085\,\mu s \approx 100.4\,\text{ms}$$

### Final Assembled Code (KTU Board Format)

```assembly
        ORG     0000H           ; Reset vector
        LJMP    MAIN            ; Jump to main program
        ORG     0030H           ; Main program starts here

MAIN:                           ; Label MAIN
        MOV     A, #55H         ; A = 0101 0101 (alternating pattern)
        MOV     P1, A           ; Send pattern to Port 1
        ACALL   DELAY           ; Call delay subroutine
        CPL     A               ; Complement accumulator
        MOV     P1, A           ; Send inverted pattern
        ACALL   DELAY           ; Wait
        SJMP    MAIN            ; Repeat forever

; ---------------- DELAY SUBROUTINE (~100 ms) ----------------
DELAY:
        MOV     R2, #0FFH       ; Outer loop counter = 255
LOOP2:  MOV     R1, #078H       ; Inner loop counter = 120
LOOP1:  DJNZ    R1, LOOP1       ; Decrement R1, jump if not zero
        DJNZ    R2, LOOP2       ; Decrement R2, jump if not zero
        RET                     ; Return from subroutine
        END                     ; End of program
```

## 3.2 KTU Standard Assembly Program #2 — Bit Manipulation (LED Pattern)

### Problem Statement
> Write an ALP to generate the pattern `P1.0 = ON`, `P1.1 = OFF`, `P1.2 = ON`, `P1.3 = OFF`, and all other bits OFF, with a 500 ms delay.

### Step-by-Step Logic

**Step 1:** Identify the binary pattern → `0000 0101` = **05H**.

**Step 2:** Decide between byte-level and bit-level instructions.
- Bit-level (SETB/CLR) is preferred when pattern changes pin-by-pin during runtime.
- Byte-level (MOV P1, #05H) is preferred for static patterns.

### Code (Bit-Level Approach)

```assembly
        ORG     0000H
        LJMP    MAIN
        ORG     0030H

MAIN:
        CLR     P1.0            ; P1.0 = 0 (OFF — corrected)
        SETB    P1.1            ; P1.1 = 1
        CLR     P1.2
        SETB    P1.3
        CLR     P1.4
        CLR     P1.5
        CLR     P1.6
        CLR     P1.7
        ACALL   DELAY_500MS
        SJMP    MAIN

DELAY_500MS:
        MOV     R3, #05H        ; Outer loop × 5
OUTER:  MOV     R2, #0FFH
L2:     MOV     R1, #078H
L1:     DJNZ    R1, L1
        DJNZ    R2, L2
        DJNZ    R3, OUTER
        RET
        END
```

## 3.3 KTU Standard Assembly Program #3 — Reading a Switch (Input Port)

### Problem Statement
> Interface an active-LOW push-button switch to **P3.2**. When the button is pressed, light an LED connected to **P1.0**. Otherwise, keep the LED OFF.

### Logic Derivation

**Step 1:** Recognize P3.2 is **bit-addressable** (`B2H`).
**Step 2:** Active-LOW means pressed = **0V (logic 0)**, released = **5V (logic 1)**.
**Step 3:** Read the pin, branch based on state.

```assembly
        ORG     0000H
        LJMP    MAIN
        ORG     0030H

MAIN:
        SETB    P1.0            ; Initialize LED OFF
        SETB    P3.2            ; Make P3.2 an input (set latch to 1)

CHECK:  JB      P3.2, LED_OFF   ; If P3.2 = 1 (released), turn LED off
        CLR     P1.0            ; Else (pressed), turn LED ON
        SJMP    CHECK

LED_OFF:
        SETB    P1.0            ; Turn LED OFF
        SJMP    CHECK
        END
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why `SETB P3.2` initially?**
> In 8051, to make a port pin work as **input**, the corresponding SFR latch bit MUST first be set to **1**. This disables the internal pull-down driver, allowing the pin to "float" and be driven by external circuitry. Forgetting this is a **classic KTU lab error**.

## 3.4 KTU Standard Assembly Program #4 — Counter using Port Toggle (Hex Counting)

### Problem Statement
> Write an ALP to count from 00H to FFH on **Port P0** with a 1-second delay between successive counts.

### Code Structure

```assembly
        ORG     0000H
        LJMP    MAIN
        ORG     0030H

MAIN:
        MOV     A, #00H         ; Initialize counter
        MOV     P0, A           ; Display 00H on P0

COUNT:  INC     A               ; A = A + 1
        MOV     P0, A           ; Output to Port 0
        ACALL   DELAY_1SEC      ; 1 second delay
        SJMP    COUNT           ; Infinite loop

DELAY_1SEC:
        MOV     R3, #0AH        ; Outer × 10
OUTER:  MOV     R2, #0FFH       ; Middle × 255
MID:    MOV     R1, #0FFH       ; Inner × 255
INNER:  DJNZ    R1, INNER
        DJNZ    R2, MID
        DJNZ    R3, OUTER
        RET
        END
```

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Decision Table for Choosing Instructions

| Scenario | Recommended Instruction | Why |
|----------|------------------------|-----|
| Drive a single LED | `SETB P1.0` / `CLR P1.0` | Direct bit access, cleaner code |
| Output a parallel byte (LCD data) | `MOV P1, A` | Fast byte transfer |
| Read a single switch | `JB P3.2, label` / `JNB P3.2, label` | Direct branch-on-bit |
| Read a byte (e.g., ADC) | `MOV A, P1` | Reads pin state into accumulator |
| Toggle a bit (alternating blink) | `CPL P1.0` | Complements — no A register needed |
| Mask some bits of a port | `ANL P1, #0F0H` | Clear lower 4 bits |
| Set some bits of a port | `ORL P1, #0FH` | Set lower 4 bits |
| Conditional branch on a port bit | `JC label` (after `MOV C, P1.0`) | Carry-based jump |

    F --> K
    G --> K
    H --> K
    K --> L([Physical Pin Voltage Change])
    I --> M([Accumulator receives port value])
    J --> N([Program Counter updated])
    L --> O([External Device Responds])
    M --> P([Subsequent logic on A])
