# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Explain the role of Special Function Registers (SFRs) in port programming of 8051. List the SFR addresses of all four ports.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** SFRs are on-chip memory-mapped registers that control specific hardware functions of the 8051. The four ports (P0, P1, P2, P3) are mapped to SFR addresses 80H, 90H, A0H, and B0H respectively.
- **Role (1 mark):** Writing to these SFRs changes the value stored in the port's internal D-latch, which in turn drives the physical output pins. Reading the SFR reads the latch value (byte-level) or the pin state (bit-level via JB/JNB).
- **Bit-addressable (1 mark):** Unlike most SFRs, all four port registers are bit-addressable (P0.0–P0.7, etc.), enabling individual pin manipulation via SETB, CLR, CPL, JB, JNB.

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Why must a port pin's latch be set to 1 before reading it as an input? What happens if this step is skipped?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Concept (1 mark):** The 8051 port pin's output driver is active when the corresponding latch bit is 0. If the latch is 0, the pin is actively driven LOW and cannot be influenced by external input.
- **Procedure (1 mark):** Writing `SETB Px.y` (or `MOV Px, #0FFH`) configures the pin as input by releasing the output driver and enabling the high-impedance state.
- **Consequence (1 mark):** If the latch is 0, reading the pin always returns 0 regardless of the external voltage, leading to "stuck-at-zero" input behavior and malfunction.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Pattern)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Re-modeled]`

**(a)** Design an 8051 ALP to interface **8 LEDs to Port P1** and generate the following running light pattern. Each LED stays ON for **200 ms**. Crystal = **11.0592 MHz**.

> Pattern: `P1.0 → P1.1 → P1.2 → ... → P1.7 → P1.6 → ... → P1.0` (chasing effect).

**(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

```assembly
        ORG     0000H
        LJMP    MAIN
        ORG     0030H

MAIN:
        MOV     A, #01H         ; Start with P1.0 ON
        MOV     P1, A
        ACALL   DELAY
        MOV     A, #02H         ; P1.1 ON
        MOV     P1, A
        ACALL   DELAY
        MOV     A, #04H         ; P1.2 ON
        MOV     P1, A
        ACALL   DELAY
        MOV     A, #08H         ; P1.3 ON
        MOV     P1, A
        ACALL   DELAY
        MOV     A, #10H         ; P1.4 ON
        MOV     P1, A
        ACALL   DELAY
        MOV     A, #20H         ; P1.5 ON
        MOV     P1, A
        ACALL   DELAY
        MOV     A, #40H         ; P1.6 ON
        MOV     P1, A
        ACALL   DELAY
        MOV     A, #80H         ; P1.7 ON
        MOV     P1, A
        ACALL   DELAY
        SJMP    MAIN            ; Loop forever

; ---- 200 ms delay: [R2 × ((R1×3)+3) + 5] × 1.085 µs = 200 ms ----
; R2 = 2, solve for R1
; 200000 = [2 × (3R1+3) + 5] × 1.085 → R1 ≈ 30710
; Use practical 2-level: R3 outer, R2 mid, R1 inner
DELAY:
        MOV     R3, #0C8H       ; 200 iterations outer
L3:     MOV     R2, #0FFH
L2:     MOV     R1, #0FFH
L1:     DJNZ    R1, L1
        DJNZ    R2, L2
        DJNZ    R3, L3
        RET
        END
```

**Evaluation Rubric (7 marks):**
- Correct initialization (1 mark)
- Use of `MOV P1, A` or `SETB` (1 mark)
- Sequential pattern (1 mark)
- Delay subroutine (2 marks)
- Loop control and syntax (1 mark)
- Comments and clarity (1 mark)

---

**(b)** Explain the **Read-Modify-Write problem** in 8051 ports. List **two instructions** affected by this issue and propose a software workaround. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (2 marks):** The Read-Modify-Write (RMW) problem occurs when an instruction reads the *SFR latch* (not the physical pin) as part of a "read-modify-write" sequence. If an external load (e.g., a transistor driver) is forcing the pin to a different logic level, the RMW instruction modifies the *wrong* value, leading to a stuck output.

**Affected Instructions (2 marks):**
- `ANL P1, A` (and `ORL P1, A`, `XRL P1, A`)
- `INC P1`, `DEC P1`
- `CPL P1.0` (and all other bit-level operations using latch)
- `DJNZ P1, label`
- `JBC P1.0, label`
- `MOV P1.y, C`

**Workaround (3 marks):** Read the port into a temporary register, perform the modification in the accumulator, then write back:

```assembly
        MOV     A, P1           ; Read entire port safely
        ANL     A, #0FEH        ; Clear bit 0 in A (not the latch!)
        MOV     P1, A           ; Write modified byte
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> - Students often confuse **read-pin** (`MOV A, P1`) with **read-latch** (used by RMW instructions). Both read the *SFR* in 8051, but RMW reads it back into itself, bypassing the actual pin state.
> - Failing to **set the latch to 1 before using a port as input** is the most common practical lab error — students lose **1–2 marks** in the viva if they cannot explain this.
> - In delay calculations, students often forget the **3 machine-cycle overhead** of the DJNZ instruction and the 2 cycles of LCALL/RET. This produces a **non-functional** delay in simulation.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Re-modeled]`

**(a)** Interface a **7-segment common-cathode display** to **Port P2** of the 8051. Write an ALP to display the digit **"5"** continuously. Provide the **segment-encoding table** and a detailed hardware connection diagram description. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Hardware (2 marks):** Connect segment pins `a, b, c, d, e, f, g` of the common-cathode display to `P2.0, P2.1, P2.2, P2.3, P2.4, P2.5, P2.6` respectively. The common cathode pin is grounded. Each segment requires a **330 Ω current-limiting resistor**.

**Segment Encoding for "5" (2 marks):**

| Segment | P2.6 (g) | P2.5 (f) | P2.4 (e) | P2.3 (d) | P2.2 (c) | P2.1 (b) | P2.0 (a) | Hex |
|---------|----------|----------|----------|----------|----------|----------|----------|-----|
| State | 1 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 1 | **6DH** |
| Note: 'a' ON, 'b' OFF, 'c' ON, 'd' ON, 'e' OFF, 'f' OFF, 'g' ON |

**ALP Code (3 marks):**

```assembly
        ORG     0000H
        LJMP    MAIN
        ORG     0030H

MAIN:
        MOV     P2, #6DH        ; Drive '5' on 7-seg
HERE:   SJMP    HERE            ; Stay forever
        END
```

---

**(b)** Write an 8051 ALP to **toggle bit P1.7** every **500 µs** using **Timer 0 in Mode 1**. Assume f_osc = 11.0592 MHz. Show all SFR configurations. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — TMOD Configuration (2 marks):**
- Timer 0, Mode 1 (16-bit) → `M1 = 0, M0 = 1`
- Timer 0 in timer mode → `C/T = 0`
- Timer 0 software controlled → `GATE = 0`
- `TMOD = 0000 0001` = **01H**

**Step 2 — TCON (2 marks):**
- Start Timer 0 → `SETB TR0` (`TR0` bit in TCON at address 88H, bit 4)

**Step 3 — Initial Timer Value (1 mark):**
- $T_{overflow} = 65536 \times 1.085\,\mu s = 71.1\,\text{ms}$ (too large for 500 µs)
- Use 500 µs target → $N_{cycles} = 500 \div 1.085 = 461$ cycles
- Reload value: $65536 - 461 = 65{,}075 = FE35H$
- `TH0 = FEH, TL0 = 35H`

**Step 4 — Code (2 marks):**

```assembly
        ORG     0000H
        LJMP    MAIN
        ORG     0030H

MAIN:
        MOV     TMOD, #01H      ; Timer 0, Mode 1
        MOV     TH0, #0FEH      ; High byte
        MOV     TL0, #035H      ; Low byte
        SETB    TR0             ; Start Timer 0
        CLR     P1.7            ; Initial state

LOOP:   JNB     TF0, LOOP       ; Wait for overflow
        CLR     TF0             ; Clear overflow flag
        CPL     P1.7            ; Toggle P1.7
        MOV     TH0, #0FEH      ; Reload
        MOV     TL0, #035H
        SJMP    LOOP
        END
```

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Lab Mistakes to Avoid:**
> 1. **Forgetting to clear the TF0 flag** manually in Mode 1 — the 8051 does *not* auto-clear TF0 in 16-bit mode (only in Mode 0 and Mode 2 with auto-reload).
> 2. **Not reloading TH0/TL0** after each overflow — leads to inconsistent toggle intervals.
> 3. **Choosing an incorrect crystal** — 12 MHz crystal gives non-integer machine cycle (1 µs), ruining serial baud rate calculations.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme — Rapid Revision Checklist (Module 2):**

- ☐ **4 ports, 32 pins total** — P0(80H), P1(90H), P2(A0H), P3(B0H).
- ☐ **All 4 ports are bit-addressable** — unique 8051 feature.
- ☐ **P0 has NO internal pull-up** — external 10 KΩ pull-up required for digital output.
- ☐ **P1, P2, P3 have internal pull-ups** (~30 KΩ weak).
- ☐ **To use a pin as input, SET the latch bit to 1 FIRST**.
- ☐ **Read-Modify-Write trap:** `INC P1`, `CPL P1.x`, `ANL P1, A` operate on the *latch*, not the *pin*.
- ☐ **P3 has alternate functions:** RxD, TxD, INT0, INT1, T0, T1, WR, RD.
- ☐ **P0 = AD0–AD7** and **P2 = A8–A15** during external memory access.
- ☐ **Machine cycle** = 12 oscillator periods. At 11.0592 MHz, $T_{MC}$ = **1.085 µs**.
- ☐ **Standard delay subroutine formula (2 nested loops):** $T_d = [R_{out} \times ((R_{in} \times 3) + 3) + 3] \times T_{MC}$.
- ☐ **Bit instructions:** `SETB Px.y`, `CLR Px.y`, `CPL Px.y`, `JB Px.y, label`, `JNB Px.y, label`, `JBC Px.y, label`.
- ☐ **Byte instructions:** `MOV Px, A`, `MOV Px, #data`, `MOV Px, direct`, `ANL Px, #data`, `ORL Px, #data`.
- ☐ **For LED interfacing:** use 220–330 Ω series resistor to limit current to ~10 mA.
- ☐ **For switch interfacing:** use 10 KΩ pull-up (active-LOW) and 100 nF debounce capacitor OR software debounce delay.
- ☐ **Standard reset vector:** `0000H` — start with `LJMP MAIN`.
- ☐ **Common assembly directives:** `ORG` (origin), `END` (end of program), `EQU` (equate), `DB` (define byte), `BIT` (bit symbol).
- ☐ **Viva trap questions to expect:** "What happens if you use `MOV P1, P0`?" → Allowed (port-to-port move), but it moves the *latch* value, not the pin. "Can P0 sink more current than P1?" → **Yes**, up to 20 mA per pin.
- ☐ **Always comment your code** — KTU examiners award 0.5–1 bonus mark for clean, well-commented assembly.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Assembly Language Program Examples for Interrupt Programming
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEBL408 - MICROCONTROLLER PROGRAMMING AND INTERFACING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Assembly Language Program Examples for Interrupt Programming

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Interrupt Programming** in the 8051 microcontroller is a mechanism by which the normal sequential execution of the program is temporarily suspended in response to an internal or external event, allowing the CPU to execute a dedicated **Interrupt Service Routine (ISR)**. Upon completion of the ISR, control is returned to the main program via a stack-retrieved return address.

In the **Intel 8051 architecture**, the interrupt system consists of **five (5) hardware interrupt sources** and **one (1) software interrupt (Reset)**:

| Interrupt Source | Flag | Vector Address | Pin / Register |
|------------------|------|----------------|----------------|
| Reset | — | **0000H** | RST pin (Power-on) |
| External Interrupt 0 (INT0) | IE0 | **0003H** | P3.2 |
| Timer 0 Overflow | TF0 | **000BH** | TCON.5 |
| External Interrupt 1 (INT1) | IE1 | **0013H** | P3.3 |
| Timer 1 Overflow | TF1 | **001BH** | TCON.7 |
| Serial Port (TI/RI) | TI/RI | **0023H** | SCON |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Each interrupt vector occupies exactly **8 bytes** of code memory. If the ISR is longer than 8 bytes, the standard KTU-expected practice is to place an `LJMP` (Long Jump) instruction at the vector address pointing to the actual ISR location.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Doorbell vs. Polling Model

> [!TIP]
> **Imagine you are cooking in the kitchen (main program) and waiting for two things:** a guest to arrive at the door (external interrupt) AND a kettle to boil (timer interrupt).

- **Polling Approach (without interrupts):** You must walk to the door every minute to check, and repeatedly open the kettle lid. The cooking never finishes!
- **Interrupt Approach (with interrupts):** You install a **doorbell** (hardware trigger) and a **kettle whistle** (timer flag). The CPU continues cooking, but the moment the doorbell rings OR the kettle whistles, you **temporarily pause cooking, handle the event, and return to cooking** from exactly where you left off.

This is the essence of interrupt-driven programming: the **CPU is not "wasting cycles" checking for events**, but is **notified asynchronously** when an event of interest occurs.

## 1.3 Why Interrupt Programming is Essential

- **Real-time response:** Critical for time-sensitive applications (motor control, alarms).
- **CPU efficiency:** Frees the CPU from "busy-wait" loops.
- **Multi-event handling:** Allows simultaneous monitoring of multiple events.
- **Power efficiency:** CPU can be in idle mode until awakened by interrupt.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Common Mistake:** A common error is to confuse the **Interrupt Flag (TF0/IE0/etc.)** with the **Interrupt Enable bit (ET0/EX0/etc.)**. The flag indicates that an event has occurred; the enable bit authorizes the CPU to respond. **Both** must be active for an ISR to be invoked.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic Flow of an 8051 Interrupt

The complete interrupt execution sequence in 8051 follows these **mandatory steps**:

1. **Event Occurrence:** A hardware event sets the corresponding **interrupt flag** (e.g., TF0 = 1 on Timer 0 overflow).
2. **Flag Sampling:** The 8051 samples all interrupt flags at the **end of every instruction cycle** (not at the end of every machine cycle).
3. **Priority Resolution:** The hardware consults the **IP (Interrupt Priority) Register** to determine the highest-priority pending interrupt.
4. **Enable Verification:** The corresponding bit in the **IE (Interrupt Enable) Register** must be set, AND the **global enable bit (EA)** must be 1.
5. **Hardware Response:** The CPU completes the current instruction, then:
   - Pushes the current **Program Counter (PC)** onto the stack.
   - Loads the PC with the interrupt's **vector address**.
6. **ISR Execution:** The CPU executes the code at the vector address (the ISR).
7. **`RETI` Instruction:** At the end of the ISR, the `RETI` instruction pops the PC from the stack, **re-enables interrupt logic**, and resumes main program execution.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The `RETI` instruction is non-negotiable.** Using `RET` instead of `RETI` will leave the interrupt priority logic in a corrupted state, causing erratic program behavior. KTU examiners **deduct 2 marks** for this common mistake.

## 2.2 The Two Critical SFRs: IE and TCON

### Interrupt Enable (IE) Register — Address A8H

| Bit | 7 | 6 | 5 | 4 | 3 | 2 | 1 | 0 |
|-----|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Name | **EA** | — | — | **ES** | **ET1** | **EX1** | **ET0** | **EX0** |

- **EA (IE.7):** Global Enable. Must be 1 to enable any interrupt.
- **ES (IE.4):** Serial Port Interrupt Enable.
- **ET1 (IE.3):** Timer 1 Interrupt Enable.
- **EX1 (IE.2):** External Interrupt 1 Enable.
- **ET0 (IE.1):** Timer 0 Interrupt Enable.
- **EX0 (IE.0):** External Interrupt 0 Enable.

### Timer/Counter Control (TCON) Register — Address 88H (Bit-Addressable)

| Bit | 7 | 6 | 5 | 4 | 3 | 2 | 1 | 0 |
|-----|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Name | **TF1** | **TR1** | **TF0** | **TR0** | **IE1** | **IT1** | **IE0** | **IT0** |

- **TF1, TF0 (TCON.7, TCON.5):** Timer overflow flags (set by hardware, cleared when CPU vectors to ISR).
- **TR1, TR0 (TCON.6, TCON.4):** Timer run control bits (1 = start, 0 = stop).
- **IT1, IT0 (TCON.2, TCON.0):** Interrupt Type (1 = edge-triggered, 0 = level-triggered).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Timing & Timer Reload Calculations

| Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes |
|-----------|---------|-------|-------|
| Machine Cycle Period | $T_{MC} = \frac{12}{f_{OSC}}$ | μs | **12 oscillator periods** = 1 machine cycle |
| Timer Increment Rate | $f_{T} = \frac{1}{T_{MC}} = \frac{f_{OSC}}{12}$ | MHz | Timer increments once per machine cycle |
| Required Count (N) | $N = \frac{T_{delay}}{T_{MC}}$ | Integer | N must be an integer |
| Timer Reload Value (Mode 1) | $Reload = 2^{16} - N = 65536 - N$ | Decimal | 16-bit timer |
| Timer Reload Value (Mode 2) | $Reload = 2^{8} - N = 256 - N$ | Decimal | 8-bit auto-reload |
| TH byte extraction | $THx = \lfloor \frac{Reload}{256} \rfloor$ | Hex | High byte |
| TL byte extraction | $TLx = (Reload) \bmod 256$ | Hex | Low byte |
| Square wave half period | $T_{half} = \frac{1}{2 \cdot f_{square}}$ | Seconds | For toggle-based generation |
| Maximum Mode 1 delay | $T_{max} = 65536 \cdot T_{MC}$ | Seconds | At 12 MHz: **65.536 ms** |
| Maximum Mode 2 delay | $T_{max} = 256 \cdot T_{MC}$ | Seconds | At 12 MHz: **256 μs** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Crystal Frequencies used in KTU Labs:**
> - **11.0592 MHz** → $T_{MC} = 1.0851\ \mu s$ (used for serial communication, exact baud rates)
> - **12 MHz** → $T_{MC} = 1.0000\ \mu s$ (used for clean integer timing)
> - For baud rate 9600 with 11.0592 MHz: **TH1 = 0xFD = 253** (exact, zero error)

## 2.4 Engineering Utility of Interrupt Programming

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Applications You Will Encounter:**
> - **Industrial:** Periodic sensor sampling in PLCs.
> - **Automotive:** Anti-lock Braking System (ABS) — wheel speed sensors trigger interrupts.
> - **Consumer Electronics:** Microwave oven timer (Timer 0 interrupt every 1 ms).
> - **IoT:** External interrupt wakes sleeping microcontroller on button press.
> - **Communication:** UART reception using RI interrupt (no CPU polling overhead).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Programs & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 PROGRAM 1 — Square Wave Generation on P1.0 using Timer 0 Interrupt

**Problem Statement:**
> Write an 8051 assembly language program to generate a **square wave of frequency 1 kHz** on pin **P1.0** using **Timer 0 in Mode 1 (16-bit)** with **interrupt**. Assume crystal frequency $f_{OSC} = 12\ MHz$.

### Step 1: Derive the Machine Cycle Period

The 8051 requires **12 oscillator periods** to complete one machine cycle. Therefore, for $f_{OSC} = 12\ MHz$:

$$
T_{MC} = \frac{12}{f_{OSC}} = \frac{12}{12 \times 10^{6}} = 1\ \mu s
$$

### Step 2: Derive the Required Timer Count

A **1 kHz square wave** has period $T = 1\ ms$. The half period is:

$$
T_{half} = \frac{T}{2} = \frac{1\ ms}{2} = 500\ \mu s
$$

The number of timer counts required for $500\ \mu s$:

$$
N = \frac{T_{half}}{T_{MC}} = \frac{500\ \mu s}{1\ \mu s} = 500\ \text{counts}
$$

### Step 3: Derive the Timer Reload Value

Since Timer 0 counts **up** from the initial value to **65536** (overflow), the initial value is:

$$
\text{Reload} = 2^{16} - N = 65536 - 500 = 65036
$$

Converting to hexadecimal:

$$
65036_{10} = 0xFE0C_{16}
$$

Therefore:

$$
TH0 = 0xFE, \quad TL0 = 0x0C
$$

### Step 4: Complete Assembly Program

```asm
; ===================================================
; PROGRAM 1: 1 kHz Square Wave on P1.0 using Timer 0
; Crystal: 12 MHz, Mode 1, ISR-driven Toggle
; ===================================================
        ORG     0000H              ; Reset vector
        LJMP    MAIN

        ORG     000BH              ; Timer 0 ISR vector
        LJMP    TIMER0_ISR         ; Jump to actual ISR

        ORG     0030H              ; Main program starts here

MAIN:   MOV     TMOD, #01H         ; T0 in Mode 1 (16-bit timer)
        MOV     TH0, #0FEH         ; Load high byte
        MOV     TL0, #0CH          ; Load low byte
        SETB    TR0                ; Start Timer 0
        MOV     IE, #82H           ; EA=1, ET0=1 (1000 0010b)
        MOV     P1, #00H           ; Initialize P1.0 = 0
        SJMP    $                  ; Main loop does nothing (wait)

; -------- Timer 0 Interrupt Service Routine --------
TIMER0_ISR:
        CPL     P1.0               ; Toggle P1.0
        MOV     TH0, #0FEH         ; RELOAD high byte (CRITICAL)
        MOV     TL0, #0CH          ; RELOAD low byte
        RETI                        ; Return and re-enable interrupts

        END
```

> [!WARNING]
> **Reloading TH0/TL0 inside the ISR is mandatory in Mode 1.** The 8051 does NOT auto-reload. Forgetting the reload causes a single toggle and then the output latches permanently.

---

## 3.2 PROGRAM 2 — External Interrupt INT0 Toggles LED

**Problem Statement:**
> Write an 8051 ALP where an **edge-triggered external interrupt on INT0 (P3.2)** toggles an LED connected to **P1.0**. The main program continuously displays a running count on Port 2.

### Program Structure Analysis

The main program runs a free-running counter, while the ISR handles the button press. This demonstrates **interrupt-driven multitasking** — two independent logical tasks running concurrently.

```asm
; ===================================================
; PROGRAM 2: INT0 toggles P1.0; main counts on P2
; ===================================================
        ORG     0000H
        LJMP    MAIN

        ORG     0003H              ; INT0 vector
        LJMP    EXT0_ISR

        ORG     0030H

MAIN:   MOV     IE, #81H           ; EA=1, EX0=1 (1000 0001b)
        SETB    IT0                ; IT0=1 -> Edge-triggered (falling)
        MOV     P1, #00H           ; LED off initially
        MOV     P2, #00H           ; Counter starts at 0

LOOP:   INC     P2                 ; Increment count on Port 2
        ACALL   DELAY              ; Short delay for visibility
        SJMP    LOOP

DELAY:  MOV     R0, #0FFH
D1:     DJNZ    R0, D1
        RET

EXT0_ISR:
        CPL     P1.0               ; Toggle LED
        RETI

        END
```

---

## 3.3 PROGRAM 3 — Pulse Width Measurement using INT0 + Timer 0

**Problem Statement:**
> Measure the width of a positive pulse applied to INT0 (P3.2) using Timer 0 in Mode 1. Display the count on Port 1 (lower 8 bits only).

### Working Principle

1. **Configure INT0 as edge-triggered** for the **rising edge** → start Timer 0.
2. **Second interrupt on falling edge** → stop Timer 0 and read count.
3. We use **two separate ISRs** (or a state-flag approach).

```asm
; ===================================================
; PROGRAM 3: Pulse Width Measurement
; Counts 1 us per increment (12 MHz crystal)
; ===================================================
        ORG     0000H
        LJMP    MAIN

        ORG     0003H              ; INT0 vector
        LJMP    EXT0_ISR

        ORG     0030H

MAIN:   MOV     TMOD, #09H         ; T0 Mode 1, /INT0 used as gate (TR0 gated)
        MOV     IE, #81H           ; EA=1, EX0=1
        SETB    IT0                ; Edge-triggered
        MOV     P1, #00H

HERE:   SJMP    HERE               ; Wait for pulse

EXT0_ISR:
        JB      P3.2, HIGH_EDGE    ; Check if pin is HIGH or LOW
        ; FALLING EDGE detected
        CLR     TR0                ; Stop Timer 0
        MOV     P1, TL0            ; Display low byte count
        RETI
HIGH_EDGE:
        ; RISING EDGE detected
        MOV     TH0, #00H
        MOV     TL0, #00H
        SETB    TR0                ; Start Timer 0
        RETI
        END
```

> [!NOTE]
> The pulse width in microseconds = $N \times T_{MC} = N \times 1\ \mu s$.

---

## 3.4 PROGRAM 4 — Serial Port Interrupt for Received Data Echo

**Problem Statement:**
> Receive data byte via UART and immediately transmit it back (echo). Use serial port interrupt to avoid CPU polling on RI.

```asm
; ===================================================
; PROGRAM 4: UART Echo using Serial Interrupt
; 9600 baud, 11.0592 MHz, Timer 1 Mode 2 auto-reload
; ===================================================
        ORG     0000H
        LJMP    MAIN

        ORG     0023H              ; Serial Port ISR vector
        LJMP    SERIAL_ISR

        ORG     0030H

MAIN:   MOV     TMOD, #20H         ; T1 Mode 2 (8-bit auto-reload)
        MOV     TH1, #0FDH         ; 253 for 9600 baud
        MOV     SCON, #50H         ; Mode 1, REN=1
        SETB    TR1                ; Start Timer 1 for baud generation
        MOV     IE, #90H           ; EA=1, ES=1 (1001 0000b)

HERE:   SJMP    HERE               ; Main does nothing

SERIAL_ISR:
        JB      TI, TRANSMIT       ; If TI=1, jump to transmit handler
        ; RECEIVE branch
        MOV     A, SBUF            ; Read received byte
        CLR     RI                 ; Clear RI flag (CRITICAL)
        MOV     SBUF, A            ; Echo back
        RETI
TRANSMIT:
        CLR     TI                 ; Clear TI flag
        RETI
        END
```

---

## 3.5 PROGRAM 5 — External Interrupt Counter (Pulse Counting)

**Problem Statement:**
> Count the number of negative-going pulses applied to INT1 (P3.3) and display the count on Port 0 (8-bit). Use interrupt-based counting.

```asm
; ===================================================
; PROGRAM 5: Pulse Counter on INT1
; Displays count (0-255) on Port 0
; ===================================================
        ORG     0000H
        LJMP    MAIN

        ORG     0013H              ; INT1 vector
        LJMP    EXT1_ISR

        ORG     0030H

MAIN:   MOV     IE, #84H           ; EA=1, EX1=1
        SETB    IT1                ; Edge-triggered (falling edge)
        MOV     P0, #00H           ; Counter = 0
        MOV     P1, #00H           ; Overflow indicator

HERE:   SJMP    HERE               ; Main idle

EXT1_ISR:
        INC     P0                 ; Increment count
        CJNE    P0, #00H, EXIT_ISR ; Check for overflow (P0=0 after 255)
        CPL     P1.0               ; Toggle overflow LED
EXIT_ISR:
        RETI
        END
```

> [!TIP]
> This is the foundation of **frequency counters** and **event counters** used in industrial automation.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Interrupt Strategy

| Application | Recommended Interrupt | Reason | Sample Frequency |
|------------|----------------------|--------|------------------|
| Generate fixed frequency | Timer Overflow | Precise, hardware-based | 100 Hz – 1 MHz |
| Detect button press | External Edge | Asynchronous, no polling | < 10 Hz |
| UART communication | Serial Port (RI) | Avoid missing bytes | 9600+ baud |
| Count external events | External Edge | Long counting possible | 0 – 100 kHz |
| Multi-rate scheduling | Multiple Timer Interrupts | Independent intervals | Hz – kHz mix |
| Emergency stop | External + High Priority | Immediate response | Single event |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — 8051 Interrupt System Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    node1["8051 CPU Core"] --> node2["Interrupt Logic Unit"]
    node2 --> node3["External INT0 Pin P3.2"]
    node2 --> node4["External INT1 Pin P3.3"]
    node2 --> node5["Timer 0 Overflow TF0"]
    node2 --> node6["Timer 1 Overflow TF1"]
    node2 --> node7["Serial Port RI and TI"]
    node3 --> node8["TCON Reg IT0 IE0"]
    node4 --> node9["TCON Reg IT1 IE1"]
    node5 --> node10["TCON Reg TR0 TF0"]
    node6 --> node11["TCON Reg TR1 TF1"]
    node7 --> node12["SCON Reg TI RI"]
    node2 --> node13["IE Register EA ES ET1 EX1 ET0 EX0"]
    node2 --> node14["IP Register Priority Resolver"]
    node14 --> node15["Vector 0003H INT0"]
    node14 --> node16["Vector 000BH T0"]
    node14 --> node17["Vector 0013H INT1"]
    node14 --> node18["Vector 001BH T1"]
    node14 --> node19["Vector 0023H Serial"]
    node15 --> node20["ISR Code in ROM"]
    node16 --> node20
    node17 --> node20
    node18 --> node20
    node19 --> node20
    node20 --> node21["RETI pops PC from stack"]
    node21 --> node1
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Interrupt Execution Flowchart

```mermaid
graph TD
    stepA["Start Main Program"] --> stepB["Initialize SFRs TMOD TCON SCON"]
    stepB --> stepC["Configure IE Register"]
    stepC --> stepD["Set EA and Individual Enable bits"]
    stepD --> stepE["Main Loop SJMP dollar"]
    stepE --> stepF{"Event Occurred?"}
    stepF -->|No| stepE
    stepF -->|Yes| stepG{"Enabled by IE?"}
    stepG -->|No| stepE
    stepG -->|Yes| stepH{"Highest Priority?"}
    stepH -->|No| stepE
    stepH -->|Yes| stepI["CPU completes current instruction"]
    stepI --> stepJ["Push PC onto stack"]
    stepJ --> stepK["Load PC with Vector Address"]
    stepK --> stepL["Execute ISR Body"]
    stepL --> stepM["RETI Instruction"]
    stepM --> stepN["Pop PC from stack"]
    stepN --> stepO["Resume Main Program"]
    stepO --> stepE
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Timer Interrupt Program Cycle

```mermaid
graph TD
    cycA["Timer counts up each machine cycle"] --> cycB{"T0 overflow FF to 00?"}
    cycB -->|No| cycA
    cycB -->|Yes| cycC["Hardware sets TF0 flag"]
    cycC --> cycD{"EA and ET0 are set?"}
    cycD -->|No| cycE["Flag remains set, polling needed"]
    cycE --> cycA
    cycD -->|Yes| cycF["CPU vectors to 000BH"]
    cycF --> cycG["Execute ISR toggle output"]
    cycG --> cycH["Clear TF0 hardware auto"]
    cycH --> cycI["RETI reload timer value if Mode 1"]
    cycI --> cycA
```

## 4.4 Hardware Schematic Block — P1.0 Square Wave Output (Functional View)

```mermaid
graph LR
    hwA["12 MHz Crystal Oscillator"] --> hwB["8051 XTAL1 and XTAL2 pins"]
    hwB --> hwC["Internal Machine Cycle Generator"]
    hwC --> hwD["Timer 0 16-bit Register TH0 TL0"]
    hwD --> hwE["Overflow Comparator"]
    hwE --> hwF["TF0 Flag set"]
    hwF --> hwG["Interrupt Logic Unit"]
    hwG --> hwH["ISR vector 000BH LJMP to Toggle Code"]
    hwH --> hwI["CPL P1.0 Instruction"]
    hwI --> hwJ["P1.0 Pin outputs square wave"]
    hwJ --> hwK["Oscilloscope or Buzzer Load"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1: Interrupt Register Concept
**Q: Explain the role of the IE (Interrupt Enable) register in 8051. What happens if EA = 0?**

**Model Answer:**
The IE register at address **A8H** controls which interrupts are allowed to be recognized by the CPU. The **EA bit (IE.7)** is the global enable — if EA = 0, **all interrupts are disabled** regardless of individual enable bits. To enable Timer 0 interrupt, for example, both **EA = 1** AND **ET0 (IE.1) = 1** must be set. A typical configuration to enable only Timer 0 is `MOV IE, #82H` (binary `10000010`).

### Question A2: Vector Address Mapping
**Q: List the vector addresses of all five 8051 interrupts.**

**Model Answer:**

| Interrupt | Vector Address |
|-----------|---------------|
| Reset | **0000H** |
| External Interrupt 0 | **0003H** |
| Timer 0 Overflow | **000BH** |
| External Interrupt 1 | **0013H** |
| Timer 1 Overflow | **001BH** |
| Serial Port | **0023H** |

Each vector is **8 bytes** wide, hence the 8-byte spacing.

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Style]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Write an 8051 assembly language program to generate a **square wave of frequency 2 kHz** on pin **P1.7** using **Timer 1 in Mode 2** (8-bit auto-reload) with interrupt. Assume crystal frequency $f_{OSC} = 12\ MHz$.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Calculate the timer reload value with full derivation and explain why Mode 2 does not require manual reload inside the ISR.

#### Model Solution to (a)

**Given:** $f_{square} = 2\ kHz$, Crystal = 12 MHz, Mode 2

**Calculation:**

- $T_{MC} = 12/12\ MHz = 1\ \mu s$
- $T_{half} = 1/(2 \times 2000) = 250\ \mu s$
- $N = 250/1 = 250$ counts
- Reload = $256 - 250 = 6 = 0x06$
- $TH1 = 0x06$ (auto-reload value)

**Program:**

```asm
        ORG     0000H
        LJMP    MAIN
        ORG     001BH              ; Timer 1 ISR vector
        LJMP    TIMER1_ISR
        ORG     0030H

MAIN:   MOV     TMOD, #20H         ; T1 Mode 2
        MOV     TH1, #06H          ; Auto-reload value
        MOV     SCON, #00H         ; (not used, but initialize)
        SETB    TR1                ; Start Timer 1
        MOV     IE, #88H           ; EA=1, ET1=1
        CLR     P1.7
        SJMP    $

TIMER1_ISR:
        CPL     P1.7
        RETI
        END
```

#### Model Solution to (b)

**Derivation:**

$$
T_{MC} = \frac{12}{12 \times 10^{6}} = 1\ \mu s
$$

$$
T_{half} = \frac{1}{2 \times 2000} = 250\ \mu s
$$

$$
N = \frac{T_{half}}{T_{MC}} = \frac{250\ \mu s}{1\ \mu s} = 250
$$

$$
\text{Reload} = 2^8 - N = 256 - 250 = 6_{10} = 0x06_{16}
$$

**Why Mode 2 needs no manual reload:**
In **Mode 2**, Timer 1 operates as an 8-bit timer with the value in `TH1` automatically transferred to `TL1` **every time TL1 overflows from FFH to 00H**. This hardware auto-reload mechanism is performed in **one machine cycle** by the timer hardware itself, eliminating the need to explicitly reload the timer value inside the ISR. This makes Mode 2 ideal for **baud rate generation** and **fixed-frequency waveform generation**.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Write an 8051 assembly language program to generate a **50 ms time delay** using **Timer 0 in Mode 1** with **interrupt**. After the delay, toggle pin **P2.3**. Assume crystal frequency $f_{OSC} = 11.0592\ MHz$.

**(b) [7 Marks]** If the same task is attempted using polling (without interrupt), explain the architectural difference and the trade-off.

#### Model Solution to (a)

**Calculation:**

$$
T_{MC} = \frac{12}{11.0592 \times 10^{6}} = 1.0851\ \mu s
$$

$$
N = \frac{50 \times 10^{3}\ \mu s}{1.0851\ \mu s} = 46,083\ \text{counts}
$$

$$
\text{Reload} = 65536 - 46083 = 19453_{10} = 0x4BFD_{16}
$$

$$
TH0 = 0x4B, \quad TL0 = 0xFD
$$

**Program:**

```asm
        ORG     0000H
        LJMP    MAIN
        ORG     000BH
        LJMP    T0_ISR
        ORG     0030H

MAIN:   MOV     TMOD, #01H         ; T0 Mode 1
        MOV     TH0, #4BH
        MOV     TL0, #0FDH
        SETB    TR0
        MOV     IE, #82H
        CLR     P2.3
        SJMP    $

T0_ISR:
        CPL     P2.3
        MOV     TH0, #4BH
        MOV     TL0, #0FDH
        RETI
        END
```

#### Model Solution to (b)

**Architectural Comparison:**

| Aspect | Polling Method | Interrupt Method |
|--------|---------------|-----------------|
| CPU State | Busy-wait (jamming PC) | Free to execute other code |
| Power Consumption | **High** (always active) | **Low** (CPU can idle) |
| Real-time Response | Delayed by polling cycle | **Immediate** (hardware-driven) |
| Code Complexity | Simple (single loop) | Moderate (ISR + main) |
| Multi-tasking | Not possible | **Fully possible** |
| Wasted Cycles | **65,536 cycles per delay** | 0 cycles wasted |

In **polling**, the CPU executes `JNB TF0, $` or `DJNZ`-based loops, occupying the CPU for the entire 50 ms duration. In **interrupt mode**, the CPU executes only the relevant setup instructions and then becomes free. The ISR runs asynchronously when TF0 = 1.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark Deductions in KTU Valuation:**
> 1. **Forgetting to set EA = 1** → ISR never executes → **−2 marks**
> 2. **Using `RET` instead of `RETI`** → interrupt priority logic corrupted → **−2 marks**
> 3. **Forgetting manual reload in Mode 1** → output latches after first cycle → **−3 marks**
> 4. **Wrong register for IT0/IT1** (writing to IE instead of TCON) → interrupt never triggers → **−2 marks**
> 5. **Not placing `LJMP` at vector address** when ISR > 8 bytes → CPU executes garbage → **−2 marks**
> 6. **Not clearing RI/TI flag in serial ISR** → infinite re-entry → **−3 marks**
> 7. **Wrong calculation of $N$** (using $f$ instead of $T_{MC}$) → wrong frequency → **−2 marks**

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-Density Revision Checklist:**
>
> - **8051 has 5 hardware interrupts** + Reset (software). Vector addresses: `0000H, 0003H, 000BH, 0013H, 001BH, 0023H`.
> - **EA bit (IE.7)** is the master switch — must be 1 for any interrupt.
> - **RETI is mandatory** at the end of every ISR (not `RET`).
> - **Mode 1 (16-bit)** requires manual reload of THx/TLx in the ISR.
> - **Mode 2 (8-bit auto-reload)** uses THx as the reload source automatically.
> - **Machine Cycle = 12 oscillator periods.** For 12 MHz crystal, $T_{MC} = 1\ \mu s$ exactly.
> - **For 11.0592 MHz crystal,** $T_{MC} = 1.0851\ \mu s$ (used for precise UART baud rates).
> - **Timer reload formula (Mode 1):** $Reload = 65536 - N$
> - **Timer reload formula (Mode 2):** $Reload = 256 - N$
> - **Edge-triggered interrupts** require `IT0` or `IT1 = 1`; level-triggered = 0.
> - **RI and TI flags** must be cleared by software in the serial ISR.
> - **Square wave generation:** Toggle the output pin in the ISR at the half-period interval.
> - **Pulse width measurement:** Start timer on rising edge, stop on falling edge, read count.
> - **Priority control** is via the IP register; default is descending by vector order.
> - **Maximum Mode 1 delay** at 12 MHz = 65.536 ms; **Maximum Mode 2 delay** = 256 μs.
> - **Standard baud rate setup:** Timer 1 Mode 2, TH1 = 0xFD for 9600 baud at 11.0592 MHz.
> - **Each vector is 8 bytes** — use `LJMP` to redirect to actual ISR if longer.
> - **Interrupt-driven code** is the foundation of **RTOS, real-time systems, and embedded firmware.**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Assembly Language Program Examples for Serial Communication with Different Baud Rates
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCEBL408 - MICROCONTROLLER PROGRAMMING AND INTERFACING LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Assembly Language Program Examples for Serial Communication with Different Baud Rates # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Serial Communication** in the context of the **8051 Microcontroller** refers to the asynchronous, full-duplex transfer of data bits one after another over a single communication line, governed by the on-chip **Universal Asynchronous Receiver-Transmitter (UART)** peripheral. The transmission speed is quantified by the **Baud Rate**, which is the number of signal changes (symbols) per second on the transmission line. In KTU 2024 Scheme lab terminology, the experiment is defined as: > *"Writing 8051 Assembly Language Programs to configure the Serial Port (UART) in Mode 1 (8-bit UART) for transmitting and receiving characters at different standard baud rates (1200, 2400, 4800, 9600, 19200) using Timer 1 in Mode 2 (8-bit Auto-Reload) as the baud rate generator."* The hardware pins involved are: - **P3.0 (RxD)** — Receiver Data input pin - **P3.1 (TxD)** — Transmitter Data output pin The key Special Function Registers (SFRs) are: - **SCON** (Serial Control) at address **0x98** - **SBUF** (Serial Buffer) at address **0x99** - **PCON** (Power Control) at address **0x87** (contains the **SMOD** bit) - **TMOD** (Timer Mode) at address **0x89** - **TH1** (Timer 1 High Byte) at address **0x8D** > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 HIGHLIGHT:** The 8051's serial port is **double-buffered** for transmission but **single-buffered** for reception. This is a frequently asked distinction in viva-voce. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview Imagine you are communicating with a friend using a **walkie-talkie radio** 📻. Two critical things must be agreed upon **before** the conversation begins: 1. **The Channel (Baud Rate):** If your walkie-talkie is set to channel "9600" and your friend's is on "2400", the audio will be completely garbled. Both ends **must** agree on the same speed. 2. **The Handshake Format (Frame Format):** A single spoken message in walkie-talkie format looks like: *[Press Talk Button — say message — Release]* — a clear "start" and "stop" event. The 8051's UART does the same electrically: it sends a **Start Bit (0)**, followed by **8 data bits**, and finally a **Stop Bit (1)**. The **Timer 1** of the 8051 acts like the **timing circuit** inside the walkie-talkie that determines how fast bits are clocked onto the air. By changing the value loaded into `TH1`, we change the channel — this is exactly what is meant by *"Serial Communication with Different Baud Rates"*. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Real-World Application:** This is the foundation of how your **PC communicates with microcontrollers**, how **GPS modules** transmit position data, how **Bluetooth modules (HC-05)** exchange AT commands, and how **industrial PLCs** talk to sensors. Mastering serial communication is a **mandatory skill** for any embedded systems engineer. --- ## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics | Parameter | Standard Value | Unit | |-----------|---------------|------| | **Standard Crystal Frequency (Fosc)** | **11.0592** | **MHz** | | Alternative Crystal Frequency | 12.0000 | MHz | | **Most Common Baud Rate (PC default)** | **9600** | **bps** | | Standard Baud Rate Set | 1200, 2400, 4800, 9600, 19200 | bps | | Data Frame Size (Mode 1) | **10 bits** (1 Start + 8 Data + 1 Stop) | bits | | UART Mode Used | Mode 1 (8-bit variable baud rate) | — | | Timer 1 Mode Used | Mode 2 (8-bit Auto-Reload) | — | > [!IMPORTANT] > The crystal frequency **11.0592 MHz** is chosen deliberately because it is **exactly divisible** by standard baud rates, eliminating cumulative timing error. With a 12 MHz crystal, baud rates are approximate, causing framing errors in high-speed communication. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The 8051 Serial Communication Hardware Architecture The 8051 contains a **dedicated on-chip UART** with two physically separate but logically linked subsystems: ### A. The Transmitter Section - **SBUF (Write) Register** at 0x99 — Data written here is automatically framed and shifted out through **P3.1 (TxD)**. - The transmitter is **double-buffered**: writing a new byte to SBUF does not overwrite the byte currently being shifted out. ### B. The Receiver Section - **SBUF (Read) Register** at 0x99 — Reading from the same address retrieves the **last received** byte from **P3.0 (RxD)**. - The receiver is **single-buffered**: the previous byte must be read before the next one arrives, or it will be overwritten (and the **RI** flag will be set without proper data preservation). --- ## 2.2 SCON Register (Serial Control — Address 0x98) Bit-by-Bit Breakdown | Bit | Name | Function | Value for Mode 1 | |-----|------|----------|-----------------| | **SM0** | Serial Mode bit 0 | Mode selection (with SM1) | **0** | | **SM1** | Serial Mode bit 1 | Mode selection (with SM0) | **1** | | **SM2** | Multiprocessor Communication Enable | 0 for normal 8051 | **0** | | **REN** | Receiver Enable | 1 = Enable reception | **1** (for RX) | | **TB8** | 9th Transmitted Bit | Not used in Mode 1 | **0** | | **RB8** | 9th Received Bit | Not used in Mode 1 | **0** | | **TI** | Transmit Interrupt Flag | Set after byte sent | Hardware sets | | **RI** | Receive Interrupt Flag | Set after byte received | Hardware sets | For Mode 1 (8-bit UART): **SCON = 0x50** (binary `0101 0000`) enables **REN=1, SM1=1, SM0=0**. For transmission only: **SCON = 0x40** (REN = 0). > [!NOTE] > **KTU Trap:** Students often forget to **clear TI and RI flags** in software before starting the transmission. Both flags must be cleared (`CLR TI` / `CLR RI`) by the programmer because the hardware sets them but never clears them. --- ## 2.3 PCON Register — The SMOD Bit (Address 0x87) | Bit | Name | Effect | |-----|------|--------| | 7 | **SMOD** | **Baud Rate Doubler**. When SMOD = 1, baud rate is **doubled** | | 6–0 | Other Power Modes | Not relevant to basic UART | > SMOD is **bit 7** of PCON. PCON is **not bit-addressable**, so it is manipulated as a whole byte: `MOV PCON, #0x80` sets SMOD=1. --- ## 2.4 Timer 1 Configuration for Baud Rate Generation Timer 1 is the dedicated baud rate generator. It must be configured as: | TMOD Bit | Setting | Meaning | |----------|---------|---------| | **Gate (T1)** | 0 | Software controlled (not external pin) | | **C/T̄ (T1)** | 0 | **Timer mode** (count internal oscillator) | | **M1, M0 (T1)** | 1, 0 | **Mode 2: 8-bit Auto-Reload** | So **TMOD = 0x20** (binary `0010 0000`). Timer 1 must be **started**: **TR1 = 1** via instruction `SETB TR1`. --- ## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (CRITICAL — Memorize This!) $$ \boxed{\text{Baud Rate} = \frac{2^{SMOD}}{32} \times \frac{F_{osc}}{12 \times (256 - TH1)}} $$ Solving for **TH1**: $$ \boxed{TH1 = 256 - \frac{F_{osc} \times 2^{SMOD}}{384 \times \text{Baud Rate}}} $$ ### Pre-Calculated TH1 Values Table (Fosc = 11.0592 MHz, SMOD = 0) | Baud Rate (bps) | TH1 (Decimal) | TH1 (Hex) | Error (%) | |-----------------|---------------|-----------|-----------| | **1200** | 232 | **0xE8** | 0.00% | | **2400** | 244 | **0xF4** | 0.00% | | **4800** | 250 | **0xFA** | 0.00% | | **9600** | 253 | **0xFD** | 0.00% | | **19200** | 254.5 (not exact) | Use SMOD=1 | — | | **19200 (SMOD=1)** | 253 | **0xFD** | 0.00% | ### Pre-Calculated TH1 Values Table (Fosc = 11.0592 MHz, SMOD = 1) | Baud Rate (bps) | TH1 (Decimal) | TH1 (Hex) | Error (%) | |-----------------|---------------|-----------|-----------| | **2400** | 244 | **0xF4** | 0.00% | | **4800** | 250 | **0xFA** | 0.00% | | **9600** | 253 | **0xFD** | 0.00% | | **19200** | 253 (re-used via SMOD) | **0xFD** | 0.00% | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why 11.0592 MHz?** Because $11.0592 \times 10^6 = 384 \times 28800$. This makes 28800 baud error-free. All standard baud rates are integer divisions of this crystal, eliminating the need for SMOD tricks at common rates. --- ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Embedded Debugging:** Serial output via UART is the **de-facto standard** for `printf`-style debugging in embedded systems (no debugger needed). - **AT Command Interfaces:** GSM (SIM800), Wi-Fi (ESP8266), and Bluetooth (HC-05) modules all use 9600/115200 baud serial AT commands. - **Industrial Protocols:** **RS-232**, **RS-485**, and **Modbus RTU** are all built upon the same UART frame format. - **GPS Receivers:** NEO-6M GPS modules default to **9600 baud, 8N1** (8 data, No parity, 1 stop bit) — the exact configuration the 8051 produces in Mode 1. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Baud Rate Formula ### Starting Principle: Timer 1 Overflow Rate The 8051's oscillator is divided by **12** before reaching the timers (machine cycle = 12 oscillator periods). Timer 1 in **Mode 2** is an 8-bit up-counter that overflows from **255 → 0**, at which point it auto-reloads the value in `TH1` and continues counting. The **overflow frequency** is: $$ f_{overflow} = \frac{F_{osc}}{12 \times (256 - TH1)} $$ ### Step 1: UART Mode 1 Divides the Overflow Rate In Mode 1, the UART hardware internally divides Timer 1's overflow frequency by **16** to provide oversampling for noise rejection. Wait — combined with the SMOD bit logic, the **effective baud rate** becomes: $$ \text{Baud Rate} = \frac{2^{SMOD}}{32} \times f_{overflow} $$ $$ \text{Baud Rate} = \frac{2^{SMOD}}{32} \times \frac{F_{osc}}{12 \times (256 - TH1)} $$ $$ \boxed{\text{Baud Rate} = \frac{2^{SMOD} \times F_{osc}}{384 \times (256 - TH1)}} $$ ### Step 2: Solving for TH1 $$ 384 \times (256 - TH1) \times \text{Baud Rate} = 2^{SMOD} \times F_{osc} $$ $$ 256 - TH1 = \frac{2^{SMOD} \times F_{osc}}{384 \times \text{Baud Rate}} $$ $$ \boxed{TH1 = 256 - \frac{2^{SMOD} \times F_{osc}}{384 \times \text{Baud Rate}}} $$ --- ## 3.2 Worked Example 1: Derive TH1 for 9600 Baud (Fosc = 11.0592 MHz, SMOD = 0) **Step 1:** Identify the known values. $$ F_{osc} = 11.0592 \times 10^6 \text{ Hz}, \quad \text{Baud Rate} = 9600, \quad SMOD = 0 $$ **Step 2:** Substitute into the TH1 formula. $$ TH1 = 256 - \frac{2^{0} \times 11.0592 \times 10^6}{384 \times 9600} $$ **Step 3:** Evaluate the denominator. $$ 384 \times 9600 = 3{,}686{,}400 $$ **Step 4:** Divide numerator by denominator. $$ \frac{11{,}059{,}200}{3{,}686{,}400} = 3 $$ **Step 5:** Subtract from 256. $$ TH1 = 256 - 3 = 253 $$ **Step 6:** Convert to hexadecimal. $$ 253 = 0xFD $$ $$ \boxed{TH1 = 0xFD \text{ for 9600 baud at 11.0592 MHz, SMOD=0}} $$ --- ## 3.3 Worked Example 2: Derive TH1 for 19200 Baud (Fosc = 11.0592 MHz, SMOD = 1) **Step 1:** Identify known values. $$ F_{osc} = 11.0592 \times 10^6 \text{ Hz}, \quad \text{Baud Rate} = 19200, \quad SMOD = 1 $$ **Step 2:** Substitute. $$ TH1 = 256 - \frac{2^{1} \times 11.0592 \times 10^6}{384 \times 19200} $$ **Step 3:** Evaluate denominator. $$ 384 \times 19200 = 7{,}372{,}800 $$ **Step 4:** Evaluate numerator. $$ 2 \times 11{,}059{,}200 = 22{,}118{,}400 $$ **Step 5:** Divide. $$ \frac{22{,}118{,}400}{7{,}372{,}800} = 3 $$ **Step 6:** Subtract. $$ TH1 = 256 - 3 = 253 = 0xFD $$ $$ \boxed{TH1 = 0xFD \text{ with SMOD=1 produces 19200 baud (doubled rate)}} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Insight:** This is the **only** way to get exactly 19200 baud from an 11.0592 MHz crystal — you must set **SMOD = 1**. With SMOD = 0, the formula gives TH1 = 254.5, which is impossible to load. --- ## 3.4 Worked Example 3: Derive TH1 for 1200 Baud (Fosc = 11.0592 MHz, SMOD = 0) **Step 1:** Substitute. $$ TH1 = 256 - \frac{1 \times 11.0592 \times 10^6}{384 \times 1200} $$ **Step 2:** Evaluate denominator. $$ 384 \times 1200 = 460{,}800 $$ **Step 3:** Divide. $$ \frac{11{,}059{,}200}{460{,}800} = 24 $$ **Step 4:** Subtract. $$ TH1 = 256 - 24 = 232 $$ **Step 5:** Convert to hex. $$ 232 = 0xE8 $$ $$ \boxed{TH1 = 0xE8 \text{ for 1200 baud at 11.0592 MHz, SMOD=0}} $$ --- ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Complete TH1 Lookup (Fosc = 11.0592 MHz) | Baud Rate | SMOD | Reload Count = $2^{SMOD} \times F_{osc} / (384 \times BR)$ | TH1 (Dec) | TH1 (Hex) | |-----------|------|------------------------------------------|-----------|-----------| | 1200 | 0 | $11{,}059{,}200 / 460{,}800 = 24$ | 232 | **0xE8** | | 2400 | 0 | $11{,}059{,}200 / 921{,}600 = 12$ | 244 | **0xF4** | | 4800 | 0 | $11{,}059{,}200 / 1{,}843{,}200 = 6$ | 250 | **0xFA** | | 9600 | 0 | $11{,}059{,}200 / 3{,}686{,}400 = 3$ | 253 | **0xFD** | | 19200 | 0 | $11{,}059{,}200 / 7{,}372{,}800 = 1.5$ ❌ | — | Not exact | | 19200 | 1 | $22{,}118{,}400 / 7{,}372{,}800 = 3$ | 253 | **0xFD** | --- ## 3.6 Worked Example 4: Crystal Frequency = 12 MHz (Non-Standard Case) For completeness, let's examine 9600 baud with the **non-standard 12 MHz crystal**: $$ TH1 = 256 - \frac{1 \times 12 \times 10^6}{384 \times 9600} $$ $$ TH1 = 256 - \frac{12{,}000{,}000}{3{,}686{,}400} $$ $$ TH1 = 256 - 3.255 $$ $$ TH1 = 252.74 \approx 253 $$ The **actual generated baud rate** with TH1 = 253 is: $$ \text{Actual BR} = \frac{12 \times 10^6}{384 \times (256 - 253)} = \frac{12{,}000{,}000}{1{,}152} = 10{,}416.67 \text{ bps} $$ **Error** $= \left(10416.67 - 9600\right) / 9600 \times 100\% = +8.5\%$ ❌ This 8.5% error **exceeds** the UART's acceptable tolerance (typically 2.2%), causing corrupted data. This is why **11.0592 MHz** is mandatory. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Functional Block Architecture — 8051 UART Subsystem ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph CPU["8051 CPU CORE"] DATA["Data Bus"] end subgraph SFRs["SPECIAL FUNCTION REGISTERS"] SCON["SCON Register
Address 0x98
Mode Control"] SBUF["SBUF Register
Address 0x99
Tx and Rx Buffer"] PCON["PCON Register
Address 0x87
SMOD Bit 7"] TMOD["TMOD Register
Address 0x89
Timer Mode"] TH1["TH1 Register
Address 0x8D
Reload Value"] end subgraph TIMER1["TIMER 1 MODULE"] T1M2["Mode 2
8-bit Auto-Reload"] OVFL["Overflow Signal
Every Machine Cycle"] end subgraph UART["UART MODE 1 ENGINE"] DIV16["Divide by 16
Oversampler"] FRAMER["Frame Assembler
Start + 8 Data + Stop"] SHIFTER["Shift Register"] end subgraph PINS["EXTERNAL PINS"] RXD["P3.0 RxD
Receiver Input"] TXD["P3.1 TxD
Transmitter Output"] end DATA --> SCON DATA --> SBUF DATA --> PCON DATA --> TMOD DATA --> TH1 TH1 --> T1M2 T1M2 --> OVFL OVFL --> DIV16 DIV16 --> SHIFTER SBUF --> SHIFTER SCON --> FRAMER PCON --> DIV16 SHIFTER --> FRAMER FRAMER --> TXD RXD --> FRAMER FRAMER --> SBUF ``` --- ## 4.2 UART Mode 1 Frame Format — Bit-Level Timing Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph FRAME["UART MODE 1 FRAME - 10 BITS TOTAL"] IDLE["IDLE
Line = 1
No Transmission"] START["START BIT
Logic 0
Duration = 1 Bit Time"] D0["D0
LSB First"] D1["D1"] D2["D2"] D3["D3"] D4["D4"] D5["D5"] D6["D6"] D7["D7
MSB Last"] STOP["STOP BIT
Logic 1
Duration = 1 Bit Time"] end IDLE --> START START --> D0 D0 --> D1 D1 --> D2 D2 --> D3 D3 --> D4 D4 --> D5 D5 --> D6 D6 --> D7 D7 --> STOP STOP --> IDLE ``` > [!NOTE] > **Bit Time** = $1 / \text{Baud Rate}$. At 9600 baud, each bit lasts **104.167 µs**. The full 10-bit frame takes **1.042 ms**. --- ## 4.3 Assembly Program Execution Flow ```mermaid flowchart TB START(["Program Start"]) --> INIT_SCON["Step 1: Initialize SCON
MOV SCON, 0x50
Mode 1, REN Enabled"] INIT_SCON --> INIT_TMOD["Step 2: Configure TMOD
MOV TMOD, 0x20
Timer 1 Mode 2"] INIT_TMOD --> LOAD_TH1["Step 3: Load TH1 for Desired Baud Rate
Example: MOV TH1, 0xFD
9600 Baud at 11.0592 MHz"] LOAD_TH1 --> SET_SMOD["Step 4: Set SMOD if Required
MOV PCON, 0x80
For Doubled Baud Rate"] SET_SMOD --> START_T1["Step 5: Start Timer 1
SETB TR1"] START_T1 --> CLR_TI["Step 6: Clear TI Flag
CLR TI"] CLR_TI --> LOAD_SBUF["Step 7: Load Character into SBUF
MOV SBUF, A
Character from Accumulator"] LOAD_SBUF --> WAIT_TI["Step 8: Wait for TI = 1
JNB TI, Loop"] WAIT_TI --> TX_DONE{"TI Set?"} TX_DONE -- No --> WAIT_TI TX_DONE -- Yes --> NEXT_CHAR["Step 9: Process Next Character
SJUMP Back to Load SBUF"] NEXT_CHAR --> LOAD_SBUF TX_DONE -- All Done --> END(["Program Continues"]) ``` --- ## 4.4 Baud Rate Configuration Decision Matrix ```mermaid flowchart TB QUERY["Required Baud Rate?"] --> CHECK9600{"Is it 9600?"} CHECK9600 -- Yes --> C9600["TH1 = 0xFD
SMOD = 0
TMOD = 0x20"] CHECK9600 -- No --> CHECK19200{"Is it 19200?"} CHECK19200 -- Yes --> C19200["TH1 = 0xFD
SMOD = 1
TMOD = 0x20
PCON = 0x80"] CHECK19200 -- No --> CHECK4800{"Is it 4800?"} CHECK4800 -- Yes --> C4800["TH1 = 0xFA

For any other crystal, the TH1 value must be recalculated using the formula in Section 3.1. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model] **Define Baud Rate. Explain the role of Timer 1 in generating baud rate for the 8051 serial port.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition (1 mark):** Baud Rate is defined as the **number of signal elements (bits) transmitted per second** on a serial communication line. It is measured in **bits per second (bps)**. For the 8051 UART in Mode 1, a baud rate of 9600 means 9600 bits are transmitted each second, making each bit last 1/9600 = 104.17 µs. **Role of Timer 1 (2 marks):** The 8051 uses **Timer 1 in Mode 2 (8-bit Auto-Reload)** as the dedicated baud rate generator. The overflow frequency of Timer 1 is internally divided by 16 (or 32 depending on SMOD) by the UART hardware to produce the actual baud rate. The relationship is: $$ \text{Baud Rate} = \frac{2^{SMOD}}{32} \times \frac{F_{osc}}{12 \times (256 - TH1)} $$ By changing the value loaded into **TH1**, the overflow rate changes, and hence the baud rate changes. Thus Timer 1 acts as a **programmable clock source** for the serial port. --- ### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model] **List the SFRs involved in 8051 serial communication and state the function of each.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | SFR | Address | Function | |-----|---------|----------| | **SCON** | 0x98 | Serial Control — selects UART mode, enables receiver, sets TI/RI flags | | **SBUF** | 0x99 | Serial Buffer — write to transmit, read received data | | **PCON** | 0x87 | Power Control — contains SMOD bit (bit 7) for baud rate doubling | | **TMOD** | 0x89 | Timer Mode — configures Timer 1 in Mode 2 for baud generation | | **TH1** | 0x8D | Timer 1 High Byte — holds the reload value determining baud rate | (1 mark for listing, 2 marks for functions) --- ## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) ### QUESTION A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model] #### Part (a) — 7 Marks **Design an 8051 assembly language program to continuously transmit the string "KTU2024" at a baud rate of 9600 using an 11.0592 MHz crystal. Show the baud rate calculation.** #### Part (b) — 7 Marks **Modify the above program to transmit the same string at 19200 baud. What register modification is required and why?** --- #### MODEL SOLUTION TO QUESTION A: **Part (a) — 7 Marks Solution:** **Baud Rate Calculation (3 marks):** For 9600 baud, Fosc = 11.0592 MHz, SMOD = 0: $$ TH1 = 256 - \frac{2^{0} \times 11.0592 \times 10^6}{384 \times 9600} $$ $$ TH1 = 256 - \frac{11{,}059{,}200}{3{,}686{,}400} = 256 - 3 = 253 = 0xFD $$ **Assembly Program (4 marks):** ```assembly ORG 0000H LJMP MAIN ORG 0030H MAIN: MOV SCON, #50H ; Serial Mode 1, REN enabled (0x50 = 0101 0000) MOV TMOD, #20H ; Timer 1, Mode 2 (8-bit Auto-Reload) MOV TH1, #0FDH ; 9600 baud at 11.0592 MHz MOV PCON, #00H ; SMOD = 0 SETB TR1 ; Start Timer 1 MOV DPTR, #STRING ; Point DPTR to string AGAIN: CLR A MOVC A, @A+DPTR ; Fetch character from code memory JZ FINISH ; If null terminator, stop ACALL TRANS ; Otherwise transmit INC DPTR SJMP AGAIN TRANS: MOV SBUF, A ; Load character into serial buffer WAIT: JNB TI, WAIT ; Wait until transmission complete CLR TI ; Clear TI flag for next byte RET FINISH: SJMP FINISH ; Halt STRING: DB 'KTU2024', 00H ; Null-terminated string END ``` **Part (b) — 7 Marks Solution:** **Modification Required (3 marks):** To change from 9600 to **19200 baud**, we must use **SMOD = 1** because the formula gives TH1 = 254.5 (non-integer) for SMOD = 0. By setting SMOD = 1, the effective baud rate is doubled without changing TH1. **Mathematical Proof (2 marks):** With SMOD = 1, the new baud rate becomes: $$ \text{New BR} = 2 \times 9600 = 19200 \text{ bps} $$ TH1 remains 0xFD, but PCON must be loaded with 0x80 to set SMOD. **Modified Code (2 marks):** ```assembly MOV PCON, #80H ; SMOD = 1 doubles the baud rate to 19200 ; (All other instructions remain identical) ``` The single instruction `MOV PCON, #80H` doubles the baud rate from 9600 to 19200. --- ### QUESTION B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model — Alternative Choice] #### Part (a) — 7 Marks **Explain the SCON register format. Write an 8051 assembly program to receive a byte of data serially at 4800 baud and store it in internal RAM location 30H.** #### Part (b) — 7 Marks **Calculate the TH1 value required for 2400 baud and 1200 baud with an 11.0592 MHz crystal. Explain why a 12 MHz crystal is not preferred for serial communication.** --- #### MODEL SOLUTION TO QUESTION B: **Part (a) — 7 Marks Solution:** **SCON Register Format (3 marks):** | Bit | 7 | 6 | 5 | 4 | 3 | 2 | 1 | 0 | |-----|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | Name | **SM0** | **SM1** | **SM2** | **REN** | **TB8** | **RB8** | **TI** | **RI** | | Value | 0 | 1 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | This is **0x50** for Mode 1, Receiver Enabled. **Baud Rate Calculation (1 mark):** For 4800 baud, TH1 = 0xFA (pre-calculated). **Assembly Program (3 marks):** ```assembly ORG 0000H LJMP MAIN ORG 0030H MAIN: MOV SCON, #50H ; Mode 1, REN = 1 MOV TMOD, #20H ; Timer 1, Mode 2 MOV TH1, #0FAH ; 4800 baud reload value MOV PCON, #00H ; SMOD = 0 SETB TR1 ; Start Timer 1 CLR RI ; Clear receive flag WAIT: JNB RI, WAIT ; Wait for data reception MOV 30H, SBUF ; Store received byte at RAM 30H CLR RI ; Clear RI for next reception SJMP WAIT ; Loop to receive next byte END ``` **Part (b) — 7 Marks Solution:** **TH1 for 2400 Baud (2 marks):** $$ TH1 = 256 - \frac{11.0592 \times 10^6}{384 \times 2400} = 256 - 12 = 244 = 0xF4 $$ **TH1 for 1200 Baud (2 marks):** $$ TH1 = 256 - \frac{11.0592 \times 10^6}{384 \times 1200} = 256 - 24 = 232 = 0xE8 $$ **Why 12 MHz is Not Preferred (3 marks):** A 12 MHz crystal does not divide evenly into standard baud rates. For 9600 baud: $$ TH1_{ideal} = 256 - \frac{12 \times 10^6}{384 \times 9600} = 256 - 3.255 = 252.74 $$ Since TH1 must be an integer, we must use TH1 = 253 (or 252). The actual generated baud rate with TH1 = 253 is: $$ \text{Actual BR} = \frac{12 \times 10^6}{384 \times 3} = 10{,}416.67 \text{ bps} $$ This produces an **error of +8.5%**, which exceeds the UART receiver's tolerance of approximately **±2.2%**, causing **framing errors** and **data corruption**. In contrast, 11.0592 MHz yields **0% error** for all standard baud rates because it is an exact multiple of 384 × standard baud values. --- ## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Losing Mistakes in KTU Valuation:** > > 1. **Forgetting to Clear TI/RI Flags** (–2 marks): The hardware sets these flags, but the programmer MUST clear them. Most students miss `CLR TI` and `CLR RI`. > > 2. **Not Using `JNB TI, label`** (–2 marks): The CPU will outpace the UART. Without polling, the next character overwrites SBUF before transmission completes. > > 3. **Wrong TMOD Value** (–2 marks): Forgetting to set Timer 1 in **Mode 2** is a fatal error. TMOD = 0x20 is the only correct value for baud generation. > > 4. **Missing `SETB TR1`** (–1 mark): Without starting Timer 1, the UART has no clock and will not function at all. > > 5. **Confusing SMOD Logic** (–2 marks): Remember — SMOD is in **PCON (0x87)**, NOT SCON. Setting PCON = 0x80 doubles the baud rate. > > 6. **String Termination** (–1 mark): Always terminate your string with `00H` (null character) when using the `JZ FINISH` polling technique. > > 7. **No Comments in Code** (–1 to 2 marks): KTU examiners allocate marks for **well-commented** assembly code, not just correct logic. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - **UART Mode 1** sends **10 bits** per frame: 1 start + 8 data + 1 stop. - **Timer 1 in Mode 2** is the **baud rate generator** — this is the only configuration that works for serial communication. - **Three SFRs must be initialized for every serial program:** 1. **SCON = 0x50** (Mode 1, REN enabled) 2. **TMOD = 0x20** (Timer 1, Mode 2) 3. **TH1 = [calculated value]** (baud rate dependent) - **Always set `TR1 = 1`** to start the baud rate clock. - **Key baud rate values (11.0592 MHz, SMOD=0):** - 1200 → TH1 = 0xE8 - 2400 → TH1 = 0xF4 - 4800 → TH1 = 0xFA - 9600 → TH1 = 0xFD - 19200 → TH1 = 0xFD + **SMOD = 1** (PCON = 0x80) - **The universal baud rate formula:** $\text{Baud Rate} = \frac{2^{SMOD} \times F_{osc}}{384 \times (256 - TH1)}$ - **RxD = P3.0** (input), **TxD = P3.1** (output) — these are the physical serial pins. - **TI flag** must be cleared by software after every transmission. - **RI flag** must be cleared by software after every reception. - **11.0592 MHz crystal** is the universal choice for 8051 systems requiring serial communication due to zero error. - **SBUF** at the same address (0x99) acts as **two separate registers**: write-only for transmission, read-only for reception. - **SCON is bit-addressable** (0x98), but **PCON is not** bit-addressable (0x87). - **REN bit** in SCON enables/disables the receiver — set to 1 for reception, 0 for transmission only. - **A complete 10-bit frame at 9600 baud takes 1.042 ms** to transmit.
